example_name: Amazon France Logistik Fine
example_background: Amazon collected and processed employee personal data through monitoring technologies in its warehouses, citing legitimate interests for operational efficiency, safety, and productivity. This included data on movement patterns, break times, and performance metrics from thousands of workers who engaged with the company as employees, with assessments needed on expectation alignment.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL fined Amazon France Logistique €32 million in 2024 after finding disproportionate data processing. Amazon modified its monitoring systems to limit data collection scope.
source_rule:
Recital 47 Overriding Legitimate Interest*
1The legitimate interests of a controller, including those of a controller to which the personal data may be disclosed, or of a third party, may provide a legal basis for processing, provided that the interests or the fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject are not overriding, taking into consideration the reasonable expectations of data subjects based on their relationship with the controller. 2Such legitimate interest could exist for example where there is a relevant and appropriate relationship between the data subject and the controller in situations such as where the data subject is a client or in the service of the controller. 3At any rate the existence of a legitimate interest would need careful assessment including whether a data subject can reasonably expect at the time and in the context of the collection of the personal data that processing for that purpose may take place. 4The interests and fundamental rights of the data subject could in particular override the interest of the data controller where personal data are processed in circumstances where data subjects do not reasonably expect further processing. 5Given that it is for the legislator to provide by law for the legal basis for public authorities to process personal data, that legal basis should not apply to the processing by public authorities in the performance of their tasks. 6The processing of personal data strictly necessary for the purposes of preventing fraud also constitutes a legitimate interest of the data controller concerned. 7The processing of personal data for direct marketing purposes may be regarded as carried out for a legitimate interest.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 88 - Data protection in employment

example_name: Vodafone Portugal Direct Marketing Fine
example_background: Vodafone Portugal processed customer contact details for direct marketing communications, relying on legitimate interests without conducting a proper balancing test or providing opt-out mechanisms aligned with customer expectations in their service relationship. This affected millions of subscribers receiving unsolicited promotional messages.
process_and_outcome: The Portuguese DPA fined Vodafone €225,000 in 2020 for inadequate legitimate interests assessments in marketing. Vodafone updated its marketing consent processes and suppression lists.

example_name: Lloyds Banking Group Legitimate Interest Compliance
example_background: Lloyds Banking Group implemented a structured Legitimate Interests Assessment (LIA) framework for processing customer data in fraud detection and anti-money laundering activities. The bank conducted balancing tests considering customer expectations in banking relationships, documenting interests like preventing financial crime against data subject rights, and provided transparency via privacy notices.
process_and_outcome: Following ICO guidance, Lloyds integrated LIAs into operations, avoiding enforcement actions and demonstrating effective compliance during regulatory audits in 2022.
source_rule:
Recital 47 Overriding Legitimate Interest*
1The legitimate interests of a controller, including those of a controller to which the personal data may be disclosed, or of a third party, may provide a legal basis for processing, provided that the interests or the fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject are not overriding, taking into consideration the reasonable expectations of data subjects based on their relationship with the controller. 2Such legitimate interest could exist for example where there is a relevant and appropriate relationship between the data subject and the controller in situations such as where the data subject is a client or in the service of the controller. 3At any rate the existence of a legitimate interest would need careful assessment including whether a data subject can reasonably expect at the time and in the context of the collection of the personal data that processing for that purpose may take place. 4The interests and fundamental rights of the data subject could in particular override the interest of the data controller where personal data are processed in circumstances where data subjects do not reasonably expect further processing. 5Given that it is for the legislator to provide by law for the legal basis for public authorities to process personal data, that legal basis should not apply to the processing by public authorities in the performance of their tasks. 6The processing of personal data strictly necessary for the purposes of preventing fraud also constitutes a legitimate interest of the data controller concerned. 7The processing of personal data for direct marketing purposes may be regarded as carried out for a legitimate interest.

example_name: HMRC Fraud Prevention Processing
example_background: HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC) in the UK processes taxpayer personal data for fraud prevention purposes under legitimate interests, where strictly necessary, such as cross-referencing declarations with third-party data. This occurs in the context of taxpayers' interactions with the authority, with assessments confirming alignment with reasonable expectations for tax administration integrity.
process_and_outcome: HMRC's practices were upheld in ICO audits as compliant, with public guidance reinforcing the approach, avoiding penalties and serving as a model for public sector application.

example_name: DPAs Fine TikTok €345 Million for Children's Data Breaches
example_background: In 2023, multiple European Data Protection Authorities coordinated an investigation into TikTok's processing of children's personal data. The findings included inadequate default privacy settings for users under 16, allowing public profiles and biometric data processing via facial recognition without proper safeguards. Network configurations failed to prevent unauthorized access to user data, and there were issues with age verification mechanisms. The platform's algorithm-driven content delivery exposed minors' data to risks from external actors. TikTok had over 19 million EU users under 16 at the time.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC, as lead authority, imposed a €345 million fine in September 2023 following the Article 65 dispute resolution procedure with other EU DPAs. TikTok was ordered to implement stricter age-appropriate settings, suspend biometric processing, and enhance network protections.
source_rule:
Recital 49 Network and Information Security as Overriding Legitimate Interest*
1The processing of personal data to the extent strictly necessary and proportionate for the purposes of ensuring network and information security, i.e. the ability of a network or an information system to resist, at a given level of confidence, accidental events or unlawful or malicious actions that compromise the availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of stored or transmitted personal data, and the security of the related services offered by, or accessible via, those networks and systems, by public authorities, by computer emergency response teams (CERTs), computer security incident response teams (CSIRTs), by providers of electronic communications networks and services and by providers of security technologies and services, constitutes a legitimate interest of the data controller concerned. 2This could, for example, include preventing unauthorised access to electronic communications networks and malicious code distribution and stopping ‘denial of service’ attacks and damage to computer and electronic communication systems.
relevant_rules: GDPR Recital 49 - Network and Information Security
GDPR Article 8 - Conditions for Children's Consent

example_name: ENISA CSIRT Certification Framework Implementation
example_background: The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) developed and implemented a certification framework for national and EU CSIRTs under the Cybersecurity Act. CSIRTs in member states, such as those operated by national authorities, adopted measures for incident detection, response, and recovery. These include continuous monitoring of networks, vulnerability management, and secure information sharing via platforms like the CSIRT Network. For instance, Germany's BSI CSIRT handles thousands of incidents annually, ensuring resilience of critical information systems across sectors.
process_and_outcome: Successful certifications were granted to multiple CSIRTs, demonstrating compliance with EU standards. This led to improved cross-border cooperation and no major enforcement actions against certified entities.

example_name: Cloudflare Network Security Enhancements Post-Breach
example_background: In 2019, Cloudflare disclosed a partial plaintext exposure of cookies and authentication tokens from its Workers platform due to a memory leak in a third-party analytics tool. The incident lasted two hours, affecting a small subset of traffic. Cloudflare immediately terminated the tool, rotated keys, and implemented zero-trust architecture improvements, including enhanced logging and anomaly detection across its global edge network serving millions of sites.
process_and_outcome: No regulatory fines were issued due to swift disclosure and mitigation. Cloudflare published a detailed blog post promoting transparency and updated its security posture, preventing recurrence.

example_name: Deloitte CERT Network Security Practices
example_background: Deloitte's Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT) operates as part of its managed security services, providing 24/7 monitoring for client networks. They employ SIEM systems, threat hunting, and automated response capabilities to maintain availability, integrity, and confidentiality of client data. Examples include defending against ransomware and DDoS events for financial institutions, ensuring minimal downtime through predefined playbooks and regular penetration testing.
process_and_outcome: Deloitte's practices have been audited and certified under ISO 27001 and SOC 2, with no major incidents leading to penalties. Client retention and case studies highlight effectiveness in real-time threat mitigation.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Biometric Data Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, responsible for Facebook's EU operations, processed biometric data through facial recognition technology enabled by default for users uploading photos and videos. This feature automatically detected and tagged faces, creating a database of facial templates linked to user profiles. The processing occurred without explicit consent from data subjects, particularly affecting EU users whose images were analyzed. The Irish Data Protection Commission examined the practices in light of GDPR requirements for special category data.
process_and_outcome: In November 2023, the Irish DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion for violations related to international data transfers, with additional scrutiny on biometric processing. Meta disabled facial recognition in the EU but faced ongoing investigations. The company appealed aspects of the decision.
source_rule:
Recital 51 Protecting Sensitive Personal Data*
1Personal data which are, by their nature, particularly sensitive in relation to fundamental rights and freedoms merit specific protection as the context of their processing could create significant risks to the fundamental rights and freedoms. 2Those personal data should include personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, whereby the use of the term ‘racial origin’ in this Regulation does not imply an acceptance by the Union of theories which attempt to determine the existence of separate human races. 3The processing of photographs should not systematically be considered to be processing of special categories of personal data as they are covered by the definition of biometric data only when processed through a specific technical means allowing the unique identification or authentication of a natural person. 4Such personal data should not be processed, unless processing is allowed in specific cases set out in this Regulation, taking into account that Member States law may lay down specific provisions on data protection in order to adapt the application of the rules of this Regulation for compliance with a legal obligation or for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 5In addition to the specific requirements for such processing, the general principles and other rules of this Regulation should apply, in particular as regards the conditions for lawful processing. 6Derogations from the general prohibition for processing such special categories of personal data should be explicitly provided, inter alia, where the data subject gives his or her explicit consent or in respect of specific needs in particular where the processing is carried out in the course of legitimate activities by certain associations or foundations the purpose of which is to permit the exercise of fundamental freedoms.

example_name: TikTok Special Category Data Fine
example_background: TikTok processed special categories of personal data from minors' accounts, including data revealing racial or ethnic origin through profile information, video content analysis, and inferred characteristics from user behavior and content uploads. The platform's algorithms categorized users based on such data for personalized recommendations. This occurred without explicit consent or legal basis, particularly concerning children under 16, raising concerns about risks to fundamental rights.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC imposed a €345 million fine on TikTok in September 2023 for violations in children's data processing, including special categories. Additional findings addressed default privacy settings and data retention. TikTok committed to implementing changes.

example_name: British Airways Racial Data Breach
example_background: British Airways experienced a cyber-attack where hackers accessed a database containing passengers' special category data, including details on racial or ethnic origin, religious beliefs, trade union membership, and health information. The breach affected over 400,000 customers, with data exposed due to vulnerabilities in the booking system. The processing of such data in the customer database was not adequately secured.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined British Airways £20 million (reduced from £183 million on appeal) in October 2020. The airline improved cybersecurity measures.

example_name: Google and Facebook Illinois BIPA Violations
example_background: Google and Facebook utilized facial recognition technology in photo tagging features (Google Photos and Facebook tagging), processing biometric data from millions of users' uploaded images without obtaining explicit consent. This created facial recognition templates for unique identification. The practices primarily affected Illinois residents, where state law requires consent for biometric data processing.
process_and_outcome: Class action lawsuits under Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA) resulted in a $650 million settlement with Facebook in 2021 and ongoing litigation with Google leading to a tentative $100 million settlement in 2022. Both companies modified features to comply.

example_name: Schufa Holding AG Ethnic Origin Processing
example_background: Schufa Holding AG, a major German credit reporting agency, processed personal data potentially revealing racial or ethnic origin by inferring such characteristics from names, addresses, and other indicators in credit scoring models. This data was used for risk assessments shared with third parties without explicit consent or legal basis under GDPR.
process_and_outcome: The Hessian DPA fined Schufa €15 million in December 2022 after investigation. Schufa adjusted its scoring models to exclude prohibited inferences.

example_name: European Commission GDPR Compliance Model
example_background: The European Commission implemented strict internal policies for processing special categories of personal data in staff management systems, requiring explicit consent or public interest justifications. Biometric data processing for access control used purpose-limited systems with technical safeguards, ensuring no unique identification beyond authentication needs.
process_and_outcome: Internal audits and external reviews confirmed adherence, with no enforcement actions. The Commission published compliance reports demonstrating effective safeguards.

example_name: Sanofi Health Data Processing Fine
example_background: Sanofi processed health data of employees, including sick leave details and medical conditions, in HR management systems without explicit consent or necessity. Data was shared across departments for performance analytics, potentially revealing sensitive information without proper safeguards.
process_and_outcome: The French CNIL fined Sanofi €1.45 million in December 2023 following an investigation prompted by employee complaints. Sanofi updated its data processing activities.

example_name: DoorDash Data Breach Fine 2019
example_background: In 2019, DoorDash, a food delivery platform, suffered a breach affecting 4.9 million users, where attackers accessed names, phone numbers, email addresses, delivery addresses, partial credit card numbers, and in some cases, the last four digits of Social Security numbers and driver's license numbers via an API vulnerability. The company delayed notifying affected users and authorities.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined DoorDash £2.65 million in December 2021 for inadequate security measures and late reporting. No special categories were explicitly health-related, but the case highlighted processing failures.
source_rule:
Recital 52 Exceptions to the Prohibition on Processing Special Categories of Personal Data*
1Derogating from the prohibition on processing special categories of personal data should also be allowed when provided for in Union or Member State law and subject to suitable safeguards, so as to protect personal data and other fundamental rights, where it is in the public interest to do so, in particular processing personal data in the field of employment law, social protection law including pensions and for health security, monitoring and alert purposes, the prevention or control of communicable diseases and other serious threats to health. 2Such a derogation may be made for health purposes, including public health and the management of health-care services, especially in order to ensure the quality and cost-effectiveness of the procedures used for settling claims for benefits and services in the health insurance system, or for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes. 3A derogation should also allow the processing of such personal data where necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims, whether in court proceedings or in an administrative or out-of-court procedure.

example_name: Swedish Authority Health Data Processing Fine 2020
example_background: The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) examined a municipality's processing of employee health data for absence reporting. The municipality required employees to submit medical certificates with detailed health information to HR without explicit consent or legal basis, processing sensitive health data beyond what was necessary for sick leave management.
process_and_outcome: IMY imposed a fine of SEK 100,000 in 2020, determining the processing lacked a lawful basis under GDPR exceptions and appropriate safeguards.

example_name: CNIL Fine Against Avast for Health Data 2022
example_background: Avast collected browsing data from users via browser extensions, including sensitive health-related information such as visits to medical sites, and sold aggregated datasets to third parties without adequate anonymization or consent. The processing involved special categories of data without the required explicit consent or public interest derogation.
process_and_outcome: The French CNIL fined Avast €30 million in April 2022 following a complaint and investigation into unlawful data sales.

example_name: NHS Digital COVID-19 Data Processing Compliance
example_background: During the COVID-19 pandemic, NHS Digital in the UK processed special categories of health data, including vaccination status and test results, for public health monitoring, contact tracing, and vaccine management under explicit derogations in UK law aligned with GDPR. Suitable safeguards like pseudonymization and strict access controls were implemented to ensure data protection.
process_and_outcome: The processing was reviewed by the ICO and deemed compliant with public interest exceptions, with no enforcement actions taken. It supported national health responses effectively.

example_name: Estonian Health Insurance Fund GDPR Compliance
example_background: The Estonian Health Insurance Fund processes large volumes of health data for benefits claims, cost-effectiveness analysis, and public health monitoring. It relies on national law derogations, implementing technical measures like encryption, role-based access, and regular audits to handle special categories securely.
process_and_outcome: Audits by the Estonian Data Protection Inspectorate confirmed adherence to GDPR exceptions, with no fines issued and recognition as a best practice in e-health data management.

example_name: Danish Tax Authority Employee Health Data Fine
example_background: The Danish Data Protection Agency investigated the Tax Authority's processing of employee health data for absence management, where detailed medical information was stored centrally without sufficient safeguards or a clear public interest basis under employment law exceptions.
process_and_outcome: A fine of DKK 1,875,000 was issued in 2021 for processing special categories without proper legal derogation or anonymization.

example_name: Finnish Municipality Health Data Violation 2021
example_background: A Finnish municipality processed employees' health data, including diagnoses and sick leave details, in a shared HR system accessible to multiple departments without pseudonymization or strict necessity checks, exceeding employment law derogations.
process_and_outcome: The Data Protection Ombudsman imposed administrative fines totaling €15,600 in 2021 after handling complaints about unauthorized access.
source_rule:
Recital 52 Exceptions to the Prohibition on Processing Special Categories of Personal Data*
1Derogating from the prohibition on processing special categories of personal data should also be allowed when provided for in Union or Member State law and subject to suitable safeguards, so as to protect personal data and other fundamental rights, where it is in the public interest to do so, in particular processing personal data in the field of employment law, social protection law including pensions and for health security, monitoring and alert purposes, the prevention or control of communicable diseases and other serious threats to health. 2Such a derogation may be made for health purposes, including public health and the management of health-care services, especially in order to ensure the quality and cost-effectiveness of the procedures used for settling claims for benefits and services in the health insurance system, or for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes. 3A derogation should also allow the processing of such personal data where necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims, whether in court proceedings or in an administrative or out-of-court procedure.

example_name: Swedish Healthcare Authority GDPR Fine
example_background: In 2020, Region Kalmar County Council in Sweden faced scrutiny after an employee at a health center used a private USB stick to download sensitive patient data, including medical records containing special categories of personal data such as health diagnoses and treatment histories. The data was processed without adequate security measures, leading to potential unauthorized access. The incident highlighted issues in handling health data within the social care system, where professional secrecy obligations apply. The processing was intended for internal administrative purposes but lacked proper safeguards like encryption or access controls on removable media.
process_and_outcome: The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) imposed a fine of SEK 30 million (approximately €2.9 million) in 2022 for insufficient technical and organizational measures to protect health data. The authority noted repeated similar incidents, leading to the penalty.
source_rule:
Recital 53 Processing of Sensitive Data in Health and Social Sector*
1Special categories of personal data which merit higher protection should be processed for health-related purposes only where necessary to achieve those purposes for the benefit of natural persons and society as a whole, in particular in the context of the management of health or social care services and systems, including processing by the management and central national health authorities of such data for the purpose of quality control, management information and the general national and local supervision of the health or social care system, and ensuring continuity of health or social care and cross-border healthcare or health security, monitoring and alert purposes, or for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes, based on Union or Member State law which has to meet an objective of public interest, as well as for studies conducted in the public interest in the area of public health. 2Therefore, this Regulation should provide for harmonised conditions for the processing of special categories of personal data concerning health, in respect of specific needs, in particular where the processing of such data is carried out for certain health-related purposes by persons subject to a legal obligation of professional secrecy. 3Union or Member State law should provide for specific and suitable measures so as to protect the fundamental rights and the personal data of natural persons. 4Member States should be allowed to maintain or introduce further conditions, including limitations, with regard to the processing of genetic data, biometric data or data concerning health. 5However, this should not hamper the free flow of personal data within the Union when those conditions apply to cross-border processing of such data.

example_name: CNIL Fine Against Doctolib
example_background: Doctolib, a French online medical appointment booking platform, was investigated following complaints about its handling of patient health data. The platform processed special categories of personal data including medical appointment details, health conditions implied by appointment types, and patient contact information shared with healthcare professionals. In 2019-2020, users reported that cookies on the site tracked health-related browsing behavior without explicit consent, and data was transmitted to third parties like Google Analytics without proper anonymization. The processing occurred in the context of managing health services but exceeded necessary purposes for individual patient benefits.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the French data protection authority CNIL fined Doctolib €1.5 million for lack of valid consent for cookie-based processing of health data and inadequate information provided to users. The company was ordered to update its practices.

example_name: NHS Digital Data Disclosure to HSCIC
example_background: In 2016, the UK's Health and Social Care Information Centre (HSCIC) disclosed identifiable health data of over 900 patients to third parties without proper consent or anonymization. The data included sensitive medical histories, diagnoses, and treatment records processed for research and statistical purposes. The disclosure was part of broader data-sharing practices within the national health system for quality control and public health monitoring, but lacked sufficient safeguards, leading to public outcry and investigations into compliance with data protection principles for health data.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice of £170,000 to HSCIC in 2016. The organization undertook remediation, including better governance for data releases.

example_name: Luxembourg Hospital GDPR Fine
example_background: The Centre Hospitalier du Nord in Luxembourg was found to have systematically processed sensitive health data of patients, including diagnoses and treatment information, on unencrypted laptops and unsecured email communications. This occurred between 2017 and 2020 during routine management of health records for care services and administrative purposes. Employees, bound by professional secrecy, inadvertently exposed data through poor security practices, affecting thousands of patients in the social care context.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Luxembourg data protection authority fined the hospital €150,000 for violations related to security of health data processing. Corrective measures were mandated.

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into Mediscreen
example_background: Mediscreen, an Irish medical testing laboratory, processed special categories of health data including drug test results and genetic information for workplace screening programs. A 2018 breach exposed this data online due to misconfigured Amazon Web Services buckets, making sensitive results publicly accessible for weeks. The data was handled for employment health purposes but without adequate access controls, impacting employee privacy.
process_and_outcome: The Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated and issued a reprimand in 2020, requiring improvements but no fine due to remediation efforts. The company secured the data post-discovery.

example_name: Spain AEPD Fine to Hospitales Vithas
example_background: In 2021, the Vithas hospital group in Spain was investigated after a cyberattack compromised patient health records, including clinical histories and test results. The incident involved ransomware accessing special category data processed for health service management and patient care continuity across facilities.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish AEPD imposed a €1.2 million fine in 2023 for inadequate security measures protecting health data, with orders for enhanced cybersecurity.

example_name: UK NHS COVID-19 Contact Tracing App Compliance
example_background: The UK's NHS COVID-19 app, launched in 2020, processed proximity and location data linked to health status for public health monitoring and alert purposes. Designed with privacy by design, it used decentralized processing where health data remained on users' devices, only anonymized identifiers shared centrally for contact notifications, aligning with needs for health security and cross-border compatibility.
process_and_outcome: The ICO reviewed and approved the implementation in 2020 as compliant, with no enforcement actions. The app contributed to pandemic response without major breaches.

example_name: EU EDPB Guidelines on Health Data Processing
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued guidelines in 2020 on GDPR-compliant processing of health data for scientific research and public health during COVID-19. These outlined conditions for pseudonymized processing in health systems for statistical purposes, emphasizing necessity, proportionality, and safeguards like DPIAs for national health authorities.
process_and_outcome: Adopted across EU member states, serving as best practice with no fines associated; used in compliance audits for health data projects.

example_name: UK Electoral Commission Investigation into Vote Leave Data Practices
example_background: During the 2016 Brexit referendum, the Vote Leave campaign, led by prominent figures, engaged in joint operations with other groups like BeLeave. They collected and processed personal data on voters' political opinions through online quizzes and targeted digital advertising on platforms like Facebook and Google. Data was aggregated to micro-target individuals based on inferred political views, raising questions about consent, transparency, and safeguards for processing political opinions in electoral activities.
process_and_outcome: The UK Electoral Commission investigated and found breaches of data protection laws, imposing fines totaling £85,000 on Vote Leave and BeLeave in 2018. The ICO also probed the matter but focused enforcement elsewhere; the case highlighted gaps in oversight for political data processing.
source_rule:
Recital 56 Processing Personal Data on People's Political Opinions by Parties*
Where in the course of electoral activities, the operation of the democratic system in a Member State requires that political parties compile personal data on people’s political opinions, the processing of such data may be permitted for reasons of public interest, provided that appropriate safeguards are established.

example_name: Facebook EU Political Ad Transparency Compliance
example_background: Following GDPR enforcement and public scrutiny over political data use, Facebook implemented a comprehensive political advertising library and verification system across the EU. Advertisers running ads on elections, politics, or social issues must verify identity, declare funding sources, and disclose targeting criteria, including interests that may relate to political opinions. Data on political opinions is not collected directly but safeguards limit processing to public interest electoral needs with transparency tools for voters to scrutinize ads.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission reviewed compliance positively in 2020-2021 audits, noting effective safeguards that aligned with GDPR requirements. No major fines issued for this aspect, and it served as a model for platforms ahead of EU elections.
source_rule:
Recital 56 Processing Personal Data on People's Political Opinions by Parties*
Where in the course of electoral activities, the operation of the democratic system in a Member State requires that political parties compile personal data on people’s political opinions, the processing of such data may be permitted for reasons of public interest, provided that appropriate safeguards are established.

example_name: CNIL Fine on Fiducial for Political Data Processing
example_background: Fiducial, a French accounting firm with political affiliations, maintained a database of personal data on approximately 25,000 elected officials and candidates, including political opinions, affiliations, and voting records. The data was collected from public sources and used for targeted communications during electoral periods without explicit consent or sufficient safeguards, exceeding permissible public interest processing for democratic operations.
process_and_outcome: The French CNIL investigated and issued a €75,000 fine in 2021, ordering data deletion and implementation of stricter access controls. The decision emphasized the need for proportionality in processing political opinions.
source_rule:
Recital 56 Processing Personal Data on People's Political Opinions by Parties*
Where in the course of electoral activities, the operation of the democratic system in a Member State requires that political parties compile personal data on people’s political opinions, the processing of such data may be permitted for reasons of public interest, provided that appropriate safeguards are established.

example_name: Palantir Technologies EU Political Data Contract Scrutiny
example_background: Palantir, a data analytics firm, secured contracts with EU political entities for processing voter data, including inferred political opinions from public records and social media. Operations involved building profiles for electoral strategy, prompting DPA reviews on safeguards like data minimization, pseudonymization, and purpose limitation during democratic processes.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian DPA cleared Palantir's processing in 2020 after verifying technical and organizational measures, including encryption and audit logs, deeming it permissible under public interest for elections with appropriate safeguards.
source_rule:
Recital 56 Processing Personal Data on People's Political Opinions by Parties*
Where in the course of electoral activities, the operation of the democratic system in a Member State requires that political parties compile personal data on people’s political opinions, the processing of such data may be permitted for reasons of public interest, provided that appropriate safeguards are established.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)

example_name: Spanish AEPD Sanction on Political Party Database
example_background: A Spanish political party compiled a database with personal data on supporters and potential voters, including explicit political opinions gathered via petitions, events, and online forms. The processing lacked transparent information to data subjects and adequate security measures, despite claims of public interest in electoral activities.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish AEPD imposed a €3,000 fine in 2019 and required remediation, including data purging and policy updates, for non-compliance with special category data rules.
source_rule:
Recital 56 Processing Personal Data on People's Political Opinions by Parties*
Where in the course of electoral activities, the operation of the democratic system in a Member State requires that political parties compile personal data on people’s political opinions, the processing of such data may be permitted for reasons of public interest, provided that appropriate safeguards are established.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: Google EU Elections Transparency Measures
example_background: Google introduced an EU-wide political ads transparency library in 2019, requiring advertisers to verify identity, report spending, and detail targeting parameters that could include political interests. This applied to ads related to elections or political issues, ensuring visibility into data processing that might infer or target based on political opinions, with safeguards like ad repositories and restrictions on sensitive targeting.
process_and_outcome: EU regulators acknowledged the measures in 2020 as aligning with GDPR public interest processing, contributing to informed democratic participation without enforcement actions.
source_rule:
Recital 56 Processing Personal Data on People's Political Opinions by Parties*
Where in the course of electoral activities, the operation of the democratic system in a Member State requires that political parties compile personal data on people’s political opinions, the processing of such data may be permitted for reasons of public interest, provided that appropriate safeguards are established.

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into TikTok Political Data
example_background: TikTok faced scrutiny for processing user data, including inferred political opinions from video interactions and profiles, during EU electoral periods. Minors' data was particularly examined, with concerns over lack of safeguards for sensitive inferences in algorithmic recommendations and ad targeting.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC issued a €345 million fine in 2023 primarily for child data but included directives on political data safeguards. TikTok committed to enhanced age verification and sensitivity filters.
source_rule:
Recital 56 Processing Personal Data on People's Political Opinions by Parties*
Where in the course of electoral activities, the operation of the democratic system in a Member State requires that political parties compile personal data on people’s political opinions, the processing of such data may be permitted for reasons of public interest, provided that appropriate safeguards are established.

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Fine 2022
example_background: Google Ireland Limited processed personal data of users in France for targeted advertising purposes without providing clear and transparent information about the processing activities, including the logic involved in automated decision-making and profiling. The company displayed cookie consent banners that did not adequately inform users about the purposes of data processing, the categories of data collected, and the recipients of the data. Users were not given sufficient details on how their data would be used across Google's services, leading to a lack of transparency in the data processing operations. This occurred in the context of the company's extensive data collection practices through its search engine, YouTube, and advertising platforms, where billions of users interact daily without full awareness of the data flows and purposes.
process_and_outcome: The French data protection authority CNIL conducted an investigation following complaints from privacy organizations. CNIL found multiple violations of GDPR transparency obligations and imposed a fine of 150 million euros on Google Ireland Limited. The decision was upheld after appeals, requiring the company to update its information notices and consent mechanisms.
source_rule:
Recital 60 Information Obligation*
1The principles of fair and transparent processing require that the data subject be informed of the existence of the processing operation and its purposes. 2The controller should provide the data subject with any further information necessary to ensure fair and transparent processing taking into account the specific circumstances and context in which the personal data are processed. 3Furthermore, the data subject should be informed of the existence of profiling and the consequences of such profiling. 4Where the personal data are collected from the data subject, the data subject should also be informed whether he or she is obliged to provide the personal data and of the consequences, where he or she does not provide such data. 5That information may be provided in combination with standardised icons in order to give in an easily visible, intelligible and clearly legible manner, a meaningful overview of the intended processing. 6Where the icons are presented electronically, they should be machine-readable.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland GDPR Fine 2022
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited operated Facebook services in the EU, collecting extensive personal data from users for behavioral advertising without providing transparent information on the processing purposes, the existence of profiling, or the consequences of such profiling. The privacy policy and cookie banners failed to clearly explain data retention periods, data sharing with third parties, and the legal basis for processing. Users were presented with pre-ticked consent boxes and vague descriptions of data uses, particularly in the context of tracking user behavior across websites and apps to build detailed profiles for ad targeting.
process_and_outcome: CNIL investigated after receiving complaints and analyzed the company's consent mechanisms. It issued a 150 million euro fine for lack of transparent information provision under GDPR. Meta was ordered to revise its practices, and the fine was part of a series of enforcement actions against the company.

example_name: TikTok Ireland GDPR Fine 2023
example_background: TikTok Ireland Limited processed personal data of minors without providing age-appropriate transparent information about data processing purposes, profiling for algorithmic content recommendation, and data sharing practices. The app's privacy information was buried in lengthy policies not easily accessible to young users, failing to inform about the necessity of providing data and consequences of refusal. This affected millions of EU users, particularly children, in the context of short-video sharing and personalized feeds driven by extensive profiling.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated following multiple complaints and imposed a fine of 345 million euros for transparency and child data violations. TikTok was required to implement changes to its information obligations and age verification processes.

example_name: Apple GDPR Transparency Improvements
example_background: Apple Inc. updated its App Tracking Transparency (ATT) framework for iOS users in the EU, requiring apps to obtain explicit consent before tracking users across apps and websites. The company provided clear, layered privacy information explaining data processing purposes, profiling for advertising, and user rights, including standardized icons and nutrition labels for apps detailing data collection practices. This was implemented in response to GDPR requirements to ensure users understand data uses.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions were taken against Apple for these practices; instead, it served as a model cited by regulators. The framework enhanced user control and transparency, leading to widespread industry adoption.

example_name: CNIL Meta Platforms Cookie Notice Fine 2020
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited's cookie notice on Facebook.com and Instagram.com did not provide clear information to users about the deposit and reading of cookies for advertising purposes, lacking details on processing operations, profiling consequences, and options for refusal. The banners used non-transparent language and pre-checked boxes, not informing users adequately about data collected from third-party sites.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a 30 million euro fine after online complaints and audits confirmed transparency failures. Meta was instructed to update its notices for better legibility and clarity.

example_name: Whisk Media GDPR Fine 2022
example_background: Whisk Media, part of Just Eat Takeaway.com, collected children's personal data through quizzes on commercial websites without transparent parental consent mechanisms or clear information about processing purposes, data retention, and profiling for marketing. Children provided names, emails, and preferences, but notices were not age-appropriate or detailed enough.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA fined Whisk Media 475,000 euros following complaints. The company ceased the practices and improved disclosures.

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Fine for Transparency Violations
example_background: In December 2019, the French data protection authority investigated Google Ireland Limited's processing of personal data for personalized advertising services. The investigation revealed that Google's privacy policy and cookie banner did not provide sufficient information to users about the purposes of data processing, the data collected, and user rights. The policy was deemed too general and not specific enough regarding data retention periods, categories of data processed, and recipients of data. The cookie consent mechanism layered approach was found inadequate as essential information was buried in additional layers, making it difficult for users to understand the full scope of data collection and use in online advertising contexts. This occurred amid growing concerns over complex tracking technologies in digital advertising ecosystems involving multiple actors.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL imposed a fine of 50 million euros on Google Ireland Limited. The decision highlighted failures in fulfilling transparency obligations under GDPR Articles 5, 12, 13, and 14. Google was ordered to bring its practices into compliance within two months.
source_rule:
Recital 58 The Principle of Transparency*
1The principle of transparency requires that any information addressed to the public or to the data subject be concise, easily accessible and easy to understand, and that clear and plain language and, additionally, where appropriate, visualisation be used. 2Such information could be provided in electronic form, for example, when addressed to the public, through a website. 3This is of particular relevance in situations where the proliferation of actors and the technological complexity of practice make it difficult for the data subject to know and understand whether, by whom and for what purpose personal data relating to him or her are being collected, such as in the case of online advertising. 4Given that children merit specific protection, any information and communication, where processing is addressed to a child, should be in such a clear and plain language that the child can easily understand.

example_name: H&M Data Protection Fine for Employee Monitoring
example_background: In October 2020, the Hamburg DPA investigated H&M's call center operations in Nuremberg, Germany. Employees' private conversations overheard during breaks were recorded in detailed protocols shared across the company without employees' knowledge. These notes included sensitive personal information about health, family matters, and financial situations. Employees were not informed about this data collection, its purposes, or their rights, with information not provided in clear, plain language. The practice persisted for years, affecting hundreds of workers.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg DPA issued a fine of 35.25 million euros for violations of GDPR transparency and data minimization principles. H&M terminated the practice and implemented new data protection measures.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Transparency Fine
example_background: In 2023, the Irish DPC conducted an inquiry into TikTok Technology Limited's processing of children's personal data. The platform's age verification and privacy settings were inadequate, with information about data collection, profiling for advertising, and data sharing not presented in clear, plain language suitable for children. Default settings allowed extensive data processing without appropriate safeguards, and parental controls were not effectively communicated. The investigation covered periods when millions of under-13 users accessed the service.
process_and_outcome: The DPC imposed a fine of 345 million euros, the largest ever for child data violations. TikTok was required to revise age assurance measures and transparency notices.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Notification Delay
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a data breach affecting 400,000 customers' payment card details via a Magecart attack on its website. The airline delayed notifying affected individuals and the ICO about the extent of data compromised, including names, addresses, and card information. Privacy notices did not clearly explain data processing for bookings and the risks involved, with complex language obscuring third-party sharing for advertising purposes.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO proposed a 183 million GBP fine in 2020 (later reduced to 20 million GBP post-Brexit). The case underscored transparency in breach communications under GDPR Article 34.

example_name: Whistleblower Case Meta Platforms v DPC
example_background: In 2023, the Irish DPC investigated Facebook's use of EU-US data transfers under standard contractual clauses. The case involved pseudonymized behavioral data not uniquely identifying individuals without additional controller or user info. Following a whistleblower complaint, scrutiny focused on whether Meta sought extra identification data improperly.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled in Schrems II invalidating Privacy Shield; DPC issued a €1.2 billion fine in 2023, upheld on appeal. Meta suspended transfers and implemented new safeguards.
source_rule:
Recital 57 Additional Data for Identification Purposes*
1If the personal data processed by a controller do not permit the controller to identify a natural person, the data controller should not be obliged to acquire additional information in order to identify the data subject for the sole purpose of complying with any provision of this Regulation. 2However, the controller should not refuse to take additional information provided by the data subject in order to support the exercise of his or her rights. 3Identification should include the digital identification of a data subject, for example through authentication mechanism such as the same credentials, used by the data subject to log-in to the on-line service offered by the data controller.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)

example_name: Vodafone Ireland GDPR Compliance Practice
example_background: Vodafone Ireland implemented a customer rights portal allowing users to exercise GDPR rights using login credentials for digital identification. The system accepted additional information provided by customers to facilitate access requests without requiring the controller to acquire new data solely for identification purposes.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement action; recognized in DPC audits as effective practice. Vodafone maintained compliance through regular audits and transparency reports.
source_rule:
Recital 57 Additional Data for Identification Purposes*

example_name: AstraZeneca COVID-19 Trial Data Leak
example_background: In late 2020, AstraZeneca inadvertently published sensitive participant data from its COVID-19 vaccine clinical trials on its contractor's website. The data included names, dates of birth, contact details, and IP addresses of up to 300 volunteers from the Phase I/II trial in the UK. The files were accessible for about two weeks before being removed. The incident occurred during the processing of health data for public health research aimed at developing a vaccine during the global pandemic. The exposure raised concerns about potential re-identification and misuse of trial participants' sensitive health information.
process_and_outcome: The UK's MHRA and ICO reviewed the incident. No formal fine was issued, but AstraZeneca faced public scrutiny and conducted an internal review. The company apologized and strengthened data handling protocols for clinical trials.
source_rule:
Recital 54 Processing of Sensitive Data in Public Health Sector*
1The processing of special categories of personal data may be necessary for reasons of public interest in the areas of public health without consent of the data subject. 2Such processing should be subject to suitable and specific measures so as to protect the rights and freedoms of natural persons. 3In that context, ‘public health’ should be interpreted as defined in Regulation (EC) No 1338/2008 of the European Parliament and of the Council (11), namely all elements related to health, namely health status, including morbidity and disability, the determinants having an effect on that health status, health care needs, resources allocated to health care, the provision of, and universal access to, health care as well as health care expenditure and financing, and the causes of mortality. 4Such processing of data concerning health for reasons of public interest should not result in personal data being processed for other purposes by third parties such as employers or insurance and banking companies.
involved_parties: UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA)
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 9(2)(i) (public interest in public health)

example_name: Swedish Healthcare Data Breach by Region Uppsala
example_background: In 2022, Region Uppsala in Sweden suffered a ransomware attack that compromised sensitive health data of around 500,000 patients. The attackers accessed electronic health records containing diagnoses, treatments, medications, and personal identifiers. The healthcare provider processes vast amounts of special category data for public health services, including morbidity tracking and care provision. The breach disrupted services and led to data exfiltration published on the dark web.
process_and_outcome: The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) investigated and fined Region Uppsala €1.65 million in 2023 for inadequate security measures. The region implemented remediation including better encryption and access controls.
source_rule:

example_name: Irish Data Protection Commission vs. COVID Tracker App
example_background: Ireland launched the COVID Tracker app in July 2020 to support public health contact tracing during the pandemic. The app processed location data and health status indicators to alert users of potential exposure to COVID-19. Concerns arose over the necessity and proportionality of collecting precise location data alongside pseudonymized health data, potentially allowing re-identification. The processing was justified under public health interest but faced scrutiny for exceeding strict necessities.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC conducted an investigation and issued a public statement in 2021 confirming compliance after modifications, such as disabling location history. No fine was imposed, and the app was praised for aiding public health while respecting privacy.

example_name: CNIL Fine on Hôpital Privé de l'Ouest Parisien
example_background: In 2021, the French private hospital group Hôpital Privé de l'Ouest Parisien experienced a data breach where health data of 500 patients was published online by hackers. The breach stemmed from weak password practices and lack of multi-factor authentication on an employee portal. The hospital processes sensitive health data including medical histories, treatments, and test results for patient care, which falls under public health provision despite being a private entity.
process_and_outcome: France's CNIL investigated and imposed a €1.68 million fine in 2023, citing failures in securing special category data processing. The hospital was ordered to enhance cybersecurity measures.

relevant_rules: Article L. 1110-4 of the French Public Health Code

example_name: NHS England COVID-19 Data Processing Inquiry
example_background: During the COVID-19 pandemic, NHS England processed large-scale health data through the COVID-19 dashboard and vaccination programs, aggregating data on health status, vaccine uptake, and mortality causes for public health monitoring and resource allocation. The processing involved special categories of data without individual consent, relying on public interest grounds. Public concerns were raised about data sharing with third parties for research.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO reviewed the practices in 2021 and determined they were lawful and proportionate under public health exemptions, with appropriate safeguards like pseudonymization. No enforcement action was taken.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland GDPR Fine for Health Data
example_background: Facebook (Meta) processed sensitive health data inferred from user interactions, such as pregnancy-related posts and medical condition discussions, for targeted advertising. This occurred despite GDPR prohibitions on processing special categories without explicit basis, and the data was shared with advertisers. The processing was not limited to public health purposes and involved third-party commercialization.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's DPC fined Meta €275 million in 2022 as part of the 'pay or consent' model investigation, highlighting unlawful sensitive data processing. Meta appealed aspects of the decision.

relevant_rules: GDPR Recital 54 (limits on public health processing)

example_name: Spain's AEPD Fine on Hospital Clínic de Barcelona
example_background: In 2019, Hospital Clínic de Barcelona sent 556 patients' health data via email without encryption or password protection, including HIV test results and other sensitive medical information. The data was processed for healthcare provision and public health monitoring, but the transmission method exposed it to interception risks.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish AEPD imposed a €1.7 million fine in 2020 for breaching security obligations in health data processing. The hospital revised its data transmission protocols.
source_rule:
