example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Meta Platforms Ireland (Facebook)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited processed behavioral data of EU Facebook users for personalized advertising based on 'legitimate interests' rather than consent. Following complaints by NOYB in Belgium, the Belgian DPA initiated proceedings, leading to involvement of other EU authorities due to cross-border processing.
process_and_outcome: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) adopted a binding decision on December 20, 2022, instructing the Irish DPC to impose a fine. The DPC fined Meta €390 million in 2023 for violations related to processing lawful bases post-GDPR.
source_rule:
Recital 117 Establishment of Supervisory Authorities*
1The establishment of supervisory authorities in Member States, empowered to perform their tasks and exercise their powers with complete independence, is an essential component of the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of their personal data. 2Member States should be able to establish more than one supervisory authority, to reflect their constitutional, organisational and administrative structure.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland WhatsApp Transfers Investigation
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland, part of Meta, transferred personal data of millions of EU users to the US for processing. Following Schrems II, the Irish DPC investigated whether new SCCs and supplementary measures provided adequate protection against US laws like Section 702 of FISA. The cross-border processing required involvement of multiple EU DPAs, leading to disagreement resolution through the EDPB's dispute resolution mechanism. The case exemplified difficulties in supervisory authorities cooperating internationally to assess third-country adequacy.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Irish DPC issued a €1.2 billion fine to Meta for unlawful transfers from 2020-2021, with ongoing proceedings for post-Schrems II practices. Meta suspended new transfers and restructured data flows.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Cooperation Among Supervisory Authorities*
1When personal data moves across borders outside the Union it may put at increased risk the ability of natural persons to exercise data protection rights in particular to protect themselves from the unlawful use or disclosure of that information. 2At the same time, supervisory authorities may find that they are unable to pursue complaints or conduct investigations relating to the activities outside their borders. 3Their efforts to work together in the cross-border context may also be hampered by insufficient preventative or remedial powers, inconsistent legal regimes, and practical obstacles like resource constraints. 4Therefore, there is a need to promote closer cooperation among data protection supervisory authorities to help them exchange information and carry out investigations with their international counterparts. 5For the purposes of developing international cooperation mechanisms to facilitate and provide international mutual assistance for the enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data, the Commission and the supervisory authorities should exchange information and cooperate in activities related to the exercise of their powers with competent authorities in third countries, based on reciprocity and in accordance with this Regulation.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on WhatsApp Transfers
example_background: Following complaints by NOYB against WhatsApp for EU-US data transfers, the Irish DPC's draft decision was challenged by other EU DPAs. The case involved processing data across EU borders with onward transfers to the US, requiring cooperation under GDPR mechanisms to resolve disagreements among supervisory authorities on the adequacy of safeguards.
process_and_outcome: In August 2020, the EDPB issued a binding decision directing the Irish DPC to impose corrective measures, resulting in a €225 million fine in 2021 for transparency and transfer violations.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Cooperation Among Supervisory Authorities*
1When personal data moves across borders outside the Union it may put at increased risk the ability of natural persons to exercise data protection rights in particular to protect themselves from the unlawful use or disclosure of that information. 2At the same time, supervisory authorities may find that they are unable to pursue complaints or conduct investigations relating to the activities outside their borders. 3Their efforts to work together in the cross-border context may also be hampered by insufficient preventative or remedial powers, inconsistent legal regimes, and practical obstacles like resource constraints. 4Therefore, there is a need to promote closer cooperation among data protection supervisory authorities to help them exchange information and carry out investigations with their international counterparts. 5For the purposes of developing international cooperation mechanisms to facilitate and provide international mutual assistance for the enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data, the Commission and the supervisory authorities should exchange information and cooperate in activities related to the exercise of their powers with competent authorities in third countries, based on reciprocity and in accordance with this Regulation.

example_name: Amazon France LogFiles Transfers Fine
example_background: Amazon Europe transferred personal data in log files from France to the US for processing. The French CNIL investigated after a complaint, finding insufficient supplementary measures to protect data despite use of SCCs, given US access risks. This cross-border transfer case required assessment of international cooperation challenges.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, CNIL fined Amazon €35 million, one of the first post-GDPR transfer fines, leading Amazon to enhance encryption and pseudonymization.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Cooperation Among Supervisory Authorities*
1When personal data moves across borders outside the Union it may put at increased risk the ability of natural persons to exercise data protection rights in particular to protect themselves from the unlawful use or disclosure of that information. 2At the same time, supervisory authorities may find that they are unable to pursue complaints or conduct investigations relating to the activities outside their borders. 3Their efforts to work together in the cross-border context may also be hampered by insufficient preventative or remedial powers, inconsistent legal regimes, and practical obstacles like resource constraints. 4Therefore, there is a need to promote closer cooperation among data protection supervisory authorities to help them exchange information and carry out investigations with their international counterparts. 5For the purposes of developing international cooperation mechanisms to facilitate and provide international mutual assistance for the enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data, the Commission and the supervisory authorities should exchange information and cooperate in activities related to the exercise of their powers with competent authorities in third countries, based on reciprocity and in accordance with this Regulation.

example_name: EU-US Data Privacy Framework Cooperation
example_background: Following Schrems II invalidation, the European Commission and US government negotiated a new framework to replace Privacy Shield. EU supervisory authorities, via the EDPB, engaged in adequacy assessments and ongoing monitoring. The framework includes safeguards like a Data Privacy Framework (DPF) redress mechanism, promoting cooperation for enforcement across borders.
process_and_outcome: Adopted in July 2023, the EU-US DPF allows certified transfers with annual reviews by the Commission and EDPB. Over 3,000 companies joined by 2024, demonstrating structured international cooperation.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Cooperation Among Supervisory Authorities*
1When personal data moves across borders outside the Union it may put at increased risk the ability of natural persons to exercise data protection rights in particular to protect themselves from the unlawful use or disclosure of that information. 2At the same time, supervisory authorities may find that they are unable to pursue complaints or conduct investigations relating to the activities outside their borders. 3Their efforts to work together in the cross-border context may also be hampered by insufficient preventative or remedial powers, inconsistent legal regimes, and practical obstacles like resource constraints. 4Therefore, there is a need to promote closer cooperation among data protection supervisory authorities to help them exchange information and carry out investigations with their international counterparts. 5For the purposes of developing international cooperation mechanisms to facilitate and provide international mutual assistance for the enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data, the Commission and the supervisory authorities should exchange information and cooperate in activities related to the exercise of their powers with competent authorities in third countries, based on reciprocity and in accordance with this Regulation.

example_name: EDPB-FTC-US Cooperation on Big Tech Probes
example_background: EU DPAs and US Federal Trade Commission (FTC) established channels for exchanging information on investigations into large tech firms like Google and Meta involving cross-border data flows and processing. This included joint workshops and mutual assistance requests to address enforcement gaps.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing since 2023, this cooperation led to aligned enforcement actions and shared best practices, enhancing cross-border investigation capabilities without public fines tied directly.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Italian Garante Judicial Review
example_background: In 2020, the Italian data protection authority, Garante per la protezione dei dati personali, faced a situation where its appointment procedures and operational independence were examined through judicial channels. The case arose from concerns over the selection process for the authority's president and members, which some stakeholders argued impacted the body's ability to function without undue influence. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued an opinion addressing whether such appointments aligned with requirements for independence. This occurred amid broader discussions on how EU member state supervisory authorities maintain autonomy while being subject to national oversight mechanisms. The review highlighted tensions between national administrative laws and EU data protection standards, particularly regarding collegial structures and external appointments.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Council of State referred questions to the Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU). The EDPB provided a binding opinion in July 2020, stating that the Italian Garante's structure did not fully comply with GDPR independence requirements due to insufficient guarantees against external influence in appointments. The CJEU later ruled on related matters, affirming the need for structural independence while allowing judicial review.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Monitoring of the Supervisory Authorities*
The independence of supervisory authorities should not mean that the supervisory authorities cannot be subject to control or monitoring mechanisms regarding their financial expenditure or to judicial review.
involved_parties: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian DPA)
Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Portuguese CNPD Independence
example_background: The Portuguese data protection authority, Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados (CNPD), underwent scrutiny in 2020 regarding its organizational setup and appointment mechanisms. Issues centered on the involvement of government figures in selecting the CNPD president and the potential for political influence, which raised questions about the authority's operational autonomy. This case was part of a series of EDPB reviews prompted by referrals from national courts examining whether national laws adequately ensured independence as required under EU data protection rules. The background included the CNPD's role in handling complaints and enforcement, where independence was critical for impartial decision-making.
process_and_outcome: The Portuguese Council of State referred the matter, leading to EDPB Binding Decision 2/2020 in October 2020. The EDPB concluded that certain aspects of the appointment process and lack of budgetary autonomy did not meet GDPR standards, recommending reforms to enhance independence. Portugal subsequently amended its laws to address these findings.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Monitoring of the Supervisory Authorities*
The independence of supervisory authorities should not mean that the supervisory authorities cannot be subject to control or monitoring mechanisms regarding their financial expenditure or to judicial review.
involved_parties: Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados (CNPD)

example_name: Luxembourg CNPD Appointment Challenge
example_background: In Luxembourg, the Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD) faced a legal challenge in 2019-2020 over the appointment of its secretary general by the Minister of Justice. Critics argued this process undermined the supervisory authority's independence, as it allowed executive branch involvement in key staffing decisions. The case emerged from a national court referral amid ongoing enforcement activities by the CNPD, including high-profile investigations into tech companies. This highlighted broader issues in smaller member states balancing limited resources with independence requirements under EU law.
process_and_outcome: The Luxembourg Administrative Court referred questions to the CJEU. The EDPB issued Binding Decision 3/2020 in December 2020, finding the appointment mechanism incompatible with GDPR independence rules. The CJEU's subsequent ruling reinforced that supervisory authorities must control their own administrative structure without ministerial interference.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Monitoring of the Supervisory Authorities*
The independence of supervisory authorities should not mean that the supervisory authorities cannot be subject to control or monitoring mechanisms regarding their financial expenditure or to judicial review.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD)
Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: Irish DPC Financial Oversight by Government
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC), responsible for supervising major tech firms like Meta and Google, has operated under a funding model where its budget is determined annually by the Department of Justice. This arrangement drew attention in EDPB reviews and parliamentary discussions, as it potentially exposed the DPC to governmental influence over resources needed for investigations. From 2018 onward, amid heavy workloads from cross-border cases, concerns grew that financial dependency could affect the DPC's ability to prioritize enforcement independently.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB addressed this in opinions and coordination decisions, noting risks to independence but not issuing a formal violation finding. Ireland increased DPC funding significantly post-2018, reaching over €20 million by 2022, and implemented multi-year budgeting to mitigate issues. No formal fine or sanction was imposed.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Monitoring of the Supervisory Authorities*
The independence of supervisory authorities should not mean that the supervisory authorities cannot be subject to control or monitoring mechanisms regarding their financial expenditure or to judicial review.

example_name: French CNIL Judicial Review of Decision
example_background: In 2019, the French data protection authority, Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL), issued a €50 million fine against Google for GDPR violations related to ad personalization. The decision was challenged in French administrative courts, testing the balance between the CNIL's independence and rights to judicial oversight. The case involved detailed examination of the CNIL's investigative process and fine calculation, reflecting Recital 118's allowance for court review without compromising authority autonomy.
process_and_outcome: The Paris Administrative Court upheld the fine in 2020, with further appeals dismissed by higher courts in 2021. The process affirmed the CNIL's independent decision-making while validating judicial review mechanisms.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Monitoring of the Supervisory Authorities*
The independence of supervisory authorities should not mean that the supervisory authorities cannot be subject to control or monitoring mechanisms regarding their financial expenditure or to judicial review.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: UK ICO Post-Brexit Funding Independence Model
example_background: Following Brexit, the UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) transitioned to a model with increased financial autonomy, receiving direct parliamentary funding rather than departmental allocations. This change, implemented in 2021, aimed to shield the ICO from executive influence amid rising enforcement against data controllers. The ICO handled significant cases like the 2021 clearview.ai investigation while maintaining accountability through annual reports to Parliament.
process_and_outcome: The UK government enacted the Data Protection and Digital Information Bill provisions for ICO funding security. Audits by the National Audit Office confirmed compliance with independence standards, with no challenges raised.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Monitoring of the Supervisory Authorities*
The independence of supervisory authorities should not mean that the supervisory authorities cannot be subject to control or monitoring mechanisms regarding their financial expenditure or to judicial review.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Budgetary Control Challenge
example_background: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) encountered issues in 2018-2020 where its budget proposals required approval from the Ministry of Justice, leading to delays in hiring and resources during a surge in GDPR complaints. This dependency was flagged in national media and EDPB discussions as potentially affecting enforcement pace.
process_and_outcome: Spain reformed its data protection law in 2021 to grant the AEPD greater budgetary autonomy via multi-year allocations. The EDPB noted improvements in subsequent opinions, avoiding formal proceedings.
source_rule:

involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: Dutch DPA Accountability Audit by Court of Audit
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) underwent regular financial audits by the Netherlands Court of Audit, focusing on expenditure efficiency without interfering in operational decisions. This mechanism, established pre-GDPR, allowed monitoring of public funds while preserving the DPA's independence in handling cases like Uber's 2018 fine.
process_and_outcome: Annual audits from 2018-2023 confirmed proper financial management, with public reports issued. No irregularities found, demonstrating effective oversight.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Monitoring of the Supervisory Authorities*
The independence of supervisory authorities should not mean that the supervisory authorities cannot be subject to control or monitoring mechanisms regarding their financial expenditure or to judicial review.

example_name: Greek DPA Independence Review by EDPB
example_background: The Hellenic Data Protection Authority (HDPA) in Greece was reviewed in 2021 for its collegial structure and appointment processes, which involved parliamentary and governmental input. Concerns arose during enforcement against telecom firms, where resource constraints were linked to funding dependencies.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued guidelines and monitored reforms; Greece adjusted laws in 2022 for better autonomy. No binding decision violation recorded.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Monitoring of the Supervisory Authorities*
The independence of supervisory authorities should not mean that the supervisory authorities cannot be subject to control or monitoring mechanisms regarding their financial expenditure or to judicial review.

example_name: TikTok French CNIL Fine for China Transfers
example_background: TikTok Technology Ltd, processing data of millions of EU users through its video-sharing platform, transferred personal data including user profiles, device information, IP addresses, and behavioral data to servers in China. The French data protection authority investigated transfers lacking adequacy decisions, focusing on whether TikTok ensured enforceable rights for data subjects via appropriate safeguards. Data transfers occurred routinely for storage, analytics, and processing by parent company ByteDance entities in China, where national intelligence laws could mandate access without sufficient EU-equivalent protections or redress mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL conducted an investigation prompted by complaints and imposed a €15 million fine on 17 May 2024 for breaches in international transfers. TikTok was ordered to comply within two months, with further monitoring.
source_rule:
Recital 114 Safeguarding of Enforceability of Rights and Obligations in the Absence of an Adequacy Decision*
In any case, where the Commission has taken no decision on the adequate level of data protection in a third country, the controller or processor should make use of solutions that provide data subjects with enforceable and effective rights as regards the processing of their data in the Union once those data have been transferred so that that they will continue to benefit from fundamental rights and safeguards.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland DPC Fine for US Transfers
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Ltd, responsible for Instagram and Facebook data in Europe, transferred personal data of over 500 million users to the US for processing by Meta Platforms Inc. The transfers included sensitive data like political views, religious beliefs, and location data. Post-Schrems II, the Irish DPC investigated whether Meta's use of SCCs alone provided effective safeguards, given US laws allowing bulk surveillance without adequate judicial redress for EU data subjects. Transfers persisted via the now-invalid Privacy Shield until mid-2020, then solely on SCCs without verified supplementary measures.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC issued a €1.2 billion fine on 12 May 2023, the largest GDPR penalty to date, for unlawful transfers. Meta faced enforcement requiring cessation of transfers and return or deletion of data, and appealed to the courts.
source_rule:
Recital 114 Safeguarding of Enforceability of Rights and Obligations in the Absence of an Adequacy Decision*
In any case, where the Commission has taken no decision on the adequate level of data protection in a third country, the controller or processor should make use of solutions that provide data subjects with enforceable and effective rights as regards the processing of their data in the Union once those data have been transferred so that that they will continue to benefit from fundamental rights and safeguards.

example_name: Amazon France Logistik CNIL Fine for US Transfers
example_background: Amazon France Logistik, part of Amazon's European operations, transferred employee and customer personal data from France to the US for centralized processing, including HR data, performance metrics, and order details. The CNIL probe revealed reliance on SCCs without supplementary measures to counter US surveillance laws, failing to ensure effective enforceability of data subject rights as required absent an adequacy decision. Transfers involved cloud storage via Amazon Web Services in the US.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €32 million fine on 22 November 2024 following an investigation triggered by employee complaints. Amazon was required to update transfer mechanisms and demonstrate compliance.
source_rule:
Recital 114 Safeguarding of Enforceability of Rights and Obligations in the Absence of an Adequacy Decision*

example_name: Google Analytics Transfers - CNIL Orders and EDPB Taskforce
example_background: Multiple French websites using Google Analytics transferred EU user data (IP addresses, browser data, browsing behavior) to Google LLC in the US via cookies and tracking tools. Absent an adequacy decision, providers relied on SCCs, but CNIL assessed that US laws enabled access without sufficient safeguards or redress for data subjects, prompting formal orders to stop transfers in 2022. This led to a Europe-wide EDPB taskforce coordinating actions against similar tools like Google Analytics, Google Tag Manager, and Meta Pixel.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued orders to nine organizations to cease transfers within one month on 22 June 2022. The EDPB established a taskforce for consistent enforcement, with ongoing investigations across EU DPAs.

Recital 114 Safeguarding of Enforceability of Rights and Obligations in the Absence of an Adequacy Decision*

example_name: TransferNow Swiss DPA Decision on US Cloud Transfers
example_background: TransferNow, a Swiss file-sharing service, transferred user data to US-based Google Cloud and Amazon Web Services for storage and processing. The Swiss Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC) reviewed whether SCCs adequately protected data subjects' rights given US surveillance laws, in a context analogous to GDPR absent adequacy.
process_and_outcome: In February 2022, FDPIC ruled the transfers non-compliant, requiring cessation or implementation of technical measures like encryption and pseudonymization. TransferNow appealed, but the decision underscored need for supplementary safeguards.

Recital 114 Safeguarding of Enforceability of Rights and Obligations in the Absence of an Adequacy Decision*
In any case, where the Commission has taken no decision on the adequate level of data protection in a third country, the controller or processor should make use of solutions that provide data subjects with enforceable and effective rights as regards the processing of their data in the Union once those data have been transferred so that that they will continue to benefit from fundamental rights and safeguards.
relevant_rules: Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP)

example_name: Microsoft's EU Data Boundary Compliance
example_background: Microsoft implemented the 'EU Data Boundary' for services like Microsoft 365, ensuring customer data from EU/EEA is processed within the EU or via compliant mechanisms to non-adequate countries. This includes geo-redundant storage in EU data centers, encryption, and pseudonymization as supplementary measures to SCCs, enabling customers to enforce data subject rights effectively.
process_and_outcome: Launched in 2022 and validated through third-party audits, the initiative met EDPB recommendations post-Schrems II. No enforcement actions; recognized as best practice by regulators and customers.
source_rule:
Recital 114 Safeguarding of Enforceability of Rights and Obligations in the Absence of an Adequacy Decision*
In any case, where the Commission has taken no decision on the adequate level of data protection in a third country, the controller or processor should make use of solutions that provide data subjects with enforceable and effective rights as regards the processing of their data in the Union once those data have been transferred so that that they will continue to benefit from fundamental rights and safeguards.

example_name: German State Data Protection Conference (DSK) Coordination Mechanism
example_background: Germany maintains 16 federal state data protection authorities (Landesdatenschutzbeauftragte) alongside the federal commissioner (BfDI). To facilitate operations under GDPR Recital 119, the Conference of the Independent Data Protection Authorities of the Länder (DSK) was established as a formal body for internal coordination. This includes designating specific authorities for lead roles in cross-border cases and ensuring unified positions on consistency matters. The DSK holds regular plenary sessions to deliberate on draft decisions submitted to the European Data Protection Board (EDB), prepares joint statements, and nominates representatives for EDB activities. This structure was formalized through a state treaty among the Länder, with the Bavarian State Commissioner often serving as the central contact point for external communications with the EDB and European Commission. The mechanism addresses potential divergences by requiring consensus or majority voting on key issues, and it has been operational since GDPR's inception in 2018, handling numerous internal alignments before escalating matters to the consistency mechanism.
process_and_outcome: The DSK successfully coordinates participation in over 100 consistency mechanism procedures annually, including opinions on standard contractual clauses and transfers. No major disruptions reported; it enabled swift joint positions, such as on the 'Schrems II' follow-up adequacy decisions. Ongoing evaluations confirm effective cooperation without need for enforcement.
source_rule:
Recital 119 Organisation of Several Supervisory Authorities of a Member State*
1Where a Member State establishes several supervisory authorities, it should establish by law mechanisms for ensuring the effective participation of those supervisory authorities in the consistency mechanism. 2That Member State should in particular designate the supervisory authority which functions as a single contact point for the effective participation of those authorities in the mechanism, to ensure swift and smooth cooperation with other supervisory authorities, the Board and the Commission.
involved_parties: Conference of Independent Data Protection Authorities of the Länder (DSK)
Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)
Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (BfDI)

example_name: Spain's Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) as Sole Authority
example_background: Spain designated a single national supervisory authority, the Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD), to oversee GDPR compliance across the country, thereby inherently fulfilling Recital 119 requirements without needing multiple authority coordination. Established under Organic Law 3/2018, the AEPD serves as the centralized contact point for all interactions with the EDPB, European Commission, and other supervisory authorities. This unitary structure streamlines cooperation in cross-border cases, where AEPD acts as lead or concerned authority as per Articles 56 and 60 GDPR. The agency participates actively in the consistency mechanism, submitting draft decisions and aligning with EDPB opinions on topics like cookies, AI processing, and international transfers. Spain's approach avoids fragmentation seen in federal systems, with AEPD handling over 20,000 complaints annually and coordinating with sector-specific bodies like the Catalan APDCAT under clear delineation protocols.
process_and_outcome: AEPD has effectively participated in EDPB proceedings, such as the coordinated response to the CJEU 'Schrems II' ruling, issuing aligned guidance nationwide. No coordination failures noted; it received commendations for timely cooperation in joint operations like the 2022 cookie sweep across EU authorities.
source_rule:
Recital 119 Organisation of Several Supervisory Authorities of a Member State*
1Where a Member State establishes several supervisory authorities, it should establish by law mechanisms for ensuring the effective participation of those supervisory authorities in the consistency mechanism. 2That Member State should in particular designate the supervisory authority which functions as a single contact point for the effective participation of those authorities in the mechanism, to ensure swift and smooth cooperation with other supervisory authorities, the Board and the Commission.

example_name: Italy's Garante Single Authority Model
example_background: Italy operates with the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali as the sole national supervisory authority, directly implementing Recital 119 by functioning as the single point of contact for consistency mechanism activities. Established under Legislative Decree 196/2003 as amended by GDPR-aligned Law 163/2018, the Garante handles all cross-border cooperation, lead authority roles, and EDPB interactions without internal multi-authority divisions. It participates in EDPB working groups on topics like facial recognition and data breach notifications, submitting draft decisions for consistency opinions. The authority maintains a dedicated international cooperation unit to ensure swift exchanges with other EU supervisory authorities and the Commission, processing thousands of mutual assistance requests yearly. This centralized model supports efficient handling of high-profile cases involving Italian companies with EU-wide operations.
process_and_outcome: The Garante contributed to EDPB guidelines on video devices and was lead authority in cases like the 2021 WhatsApp transparency investigation. It demonstrated smooth cooperation, avoiding delays in over 50 cross-border decisions referred to EDPB.

Recital 119 Organisation of Several Supervisory Authorities of a Member State*

involved_parties: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

example_name: Belgium's Multi-Authority Coordination via CBD
example_background: Belgium features several supervisory authorities including the Data Protection Authority (GBA/APD) at federal level, alongside Flemish (VIAM), French (CBPD), and German-speaking community commissions. To comply with Recital 119, a Cooperation Arrangement was signed in 2018 among these bodies, designating the GBA/APD's Litigation Chamber as the central contact point for EDPB and Commission interactions. The arrangement outlines information exchange protocols, joint decision-making for cross-border cases, and participation in consistency mechanisms. Regular coordination meetings address divergences, with the CBD (Chambre des Sanctions/Boeteskamer) handling enforcement alignments. This setup managed complexities from Belgium's linguistic and regional divisions, ensuring unified stances on issues like cloud computing and health data processing.
process_and_outcome: The framework supported joint positions in EDPB consultations and resolved a 2020 internal dispute on ePrivacy alignment without escalation. It facilitated effective participation in 30+ consistency procedures since 2018.

relevant_rules: Belgian Law of 30 July 2018 on Data Protection

example_name: France CNIL as Unified National Authority
example_background: France established the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) as the single supervisory authority, aligning with Recital 119 by serving as the exclusive contact for consistency mechanism engagements. Under Law 78-17 of 1978 as amended by Law 2018-493, CNIL coordinates all GDPR activities nationwide, including sector-specific oversight via integrated restricted committees for defense, national security, and intelligence. It acts as lead or concerned authority in cross-border matters, actively submitting drafts to EDPB on topics like targeted advertising and connected devices. CNIL's international relations department ensures prompt cooperation, handling mutual assistance with over 20 EU counterparts annually and participating in EDPB's cooperative frameworks.
process_and_outcome: CNIL led the EDPB-coordinated 2023 AI transparency enforcement sweep and issued aligned fines in Google Analytics cases. Its model enabled rapid consensus in 40+ consistency opinions without coordination issues.

involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: Austria's Multi-State Authority Cooperation Protocol
example_background: Austria has nine provincial data protection authorities (Landesdatenschutzbeauftragte) and the federal agency (Datenschutzbehörde). A 2018 cooperation agreement designates the federal Datenschutzbehörde as the primary contact point for EDPB and Commission, with protocols for task allocation, information sharing, and joint decision-making in cross-border cases per Recital 119. Provincial authorities handle local enforcement but defer to federal lead for consistency matters, convening in quarterly coordination conferences to align on EDPB submissions. This addressed federalist challenges, covering areas like employment data and public sector processing.
process_and_outcome: The protocol ensured unified Austrian input to EDPB on video surveillance guidelines and processed 25 cross-border referrals smoothly since implementation.

example_name: Netherlands' Regional and Central Authority Alignment
example_background: The Netherlands operates the Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (AP) as the central authority with regional offices, implementing Recital 119 through internal structural mechanisms ensuring unified participation. The AP's cross-border unit serves as the single contact point, coordinating regional inputs for consistency mechanism cases. Established under the GDPR Implementation Act 2018, it manages lead authority roles and EDPB interactions, with mandatory internal consultations for draft decisions. This setup handled complex cases involving Dutch multinationals across sectors like telecom and finance.
process_and_outcome: AP contributed to EDPB's 2020 cloud providers investigation and aligned regional enforcement, demonstrating effective internal cooperation in 35+ procedures.

example_name: Poland's UODO Centralized Oversight
example_background: Poland's Prezes Urzędu Ochrony Danych Osobowych (UODO) functions as the sole supervisory authority, complying with Recital 119 as the designated contact for all consistency activities. Under the 2018 Personal Data Protection Act, UODO coordinates nationwide enforcement, international cooperation, and EDPB submissions without multi-authority fragmentation. It actively engages in cross-border dispute resolution and guideline development, processing high volumes of complaints from a population of 38 million.
process_and_outcome: UODO participated in EDPB's Meta platforms fine coordination (2023) and issued consistent national guidance, avoiding coordination gaps.

example_name: Switzerland's FDPIC with Cantonal Coordination
example_background: Although not EU, Switzerland's Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC) coordinates with 26 cantonal authorities under a model analogous to Recital 119, with FDPIC as central contact for international alignments post-GDPR adequacy recognition. A 2020 ordinance designates cooperation protocols for cross-border-like cases with EU authorities.
process_and_outcome: Enabled smooth third-country cooperation with EDPB on adequacy matters, handling joint cases without noted issues.

example_name: French CNIL Budget Increase 2020
example_background: The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL), France's data protection authority, experienced a significant rise in complaints and investigations following GDPR enforcement starting in 2018. This led to handling over 10,000 complaints annually by 2020, alongside complex cross-border cases requiring cooperation with other EU authorities. The authority's existing resources proved insufficient for timely processing, resulting in backlogs and delays in decision-making. In response, the French government allocated additional funding through the national budget to expand staff from approximately 150 to over 200 full-time equivalents and upgrade IT infrastructure for case management systems.
process_and_outcome: The Ministry of Justice approved a budget increase from €18 million in 2019 to €25 million in 2020. CNIL published its annual activity report detailing the enhanced capacity, which enabled resolution of 95% of complaints within statutory deadlines and issuance of fines totaling €110 million that year.
source_rule:
Recital 120 Features of Supervisory Authorities*
1Each supervisory authority should be provided with the financial and human resources, premises and infrastructure necessary for the effective performance of their tasks, including those related to mutual assistance and cooperation with other supervisory authorities throughout the Union. 2Each supervisory authority should have a separate, public annual budget, which may be part of the overall state or national budget.

example_name: Irish DPC Resource Constraints in Meta Cases
example_background: The Data Protection Commission (DPC) in Ireland faced a surge in high-profile investigations into large tech firms headquartered there, such as Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, starting from 2018. The DPC handled complaints related to data processing practices affecting millions of EU users, including cross-border data transfers post-Schrems II. With only around 200 staff members, the authority struggled with the volume and complexity, leading to prolonged investigation timelines exceeding two years in some instances and reliance on other DPAs for input under GDPR cooperation mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: Multiple DPAs lodged objections via the EDPB's dispute resolution process. In 2023, the EDPB issued binding decisions, including a €1.2 billion fine on Meta for data transfers. The DPC later received additional government funding, increasing its budget to €60 million by 2024 to hire more investigators.
source_rule:
Recital 120 Features of Supervisory Authorities*
1Each supervisory authority should be provided with the financial and human resources, premises and infrastructure necessary for the effective performance of their tasks, including those related to mutual assistance and cooperation with other supervisory authorities throughout the Union. 2Each supervisory authority should have a separate, public annual budget, which may be part of the overall state or national budget.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Infrastructure Expansion
example_background: The Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) saw a 300% increase in data breach notifications after GDPR implementation, processing over 20,000 notifications in 2019 alone. Existing premises in Madrid were overcrowded, and legacy IT systems hindered secure data handling for mutual assistance requests from other DPAs. The authority initiated a modernization program funded separately within the national budget to meet rising demands.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, AEPD secured €15 million for new offices and digital tools, boosting staff to 250. This allowed handling 25,000 cases annually and issuing fines exceeding €30 million, as detailed in their public annual budget report.
source_rule:
Recital 120 Features of Supervisory Authorities*
1Each supervisory authority should be provided with the financial and human resources, premises and infrastructure necessary for the effective performance of their tasks, including those related to mutual assistance and cooperation with other supervisory authorities throughout the Union. 2Each supervisory authority should have a separate, public annual budget, which may be part of the overall state or national budget.

example_name: UK ICO Funding Shortfall Pre-Brexit
example_background: The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO), UK's DPA, managed a caseload doubling to 50,000 complaints post-GDPR alignment in 2018. With a budget of £70 million and 500 staff, it faced criticism for slow enforcement, such as delays in British Airways data breach investigation. Resource limitations impacted cooperation with EU DPAs during the transition period.
process_and_outcome: Parliamentary committees investigated in 2019, leading to a government pledge for £10 million extra funding in 2020. ICO issued a £20 million fine to BA in 2020 after three years, highlighting prior delays.
source_rule:
Recital 120 Features of Supervisory Authorities*
1Each supervisory authority should be provided with the financial and human resources, premises and infrastructure necessary for the effective performance of their tasks, including those related to mutual assistance and cooperation with other supervisory authorities throughout the Union. 2Each supervisory authority should have a separate, public annual budget, which may be part of the overall state or national budget.
relevant_rules: UK GDPR (aligned with EU GDPR Recital 120)

example_name: EDPB Report on DPA Resource Disparities 2022
example_background: The European Data Protection Board conducted a survey revealing stark differences in DPA capacities across the EU. Smaller authorities like those in Malta and Cyprus had budgets under €5 million and fewer than 50 staff, struggling with one-stop-shop mechanism cases involving global tech giants. Larger DPAs like Germany's BfDI managed better but noted infrastructure gaps for joint operations. This affected uniform GDPR enforcement.
process_and_outcome: EDPB published recommendations in 2022 for member states to align resources. Several countries, including Italy, increased DPA budgets by 20-50% in subsequent national budgets.
source_rule:
Recital 120 Features of Supervisory Authorities*
1Each supervisory authority should be provided with the financial and human resources, premises and infrastructure necessary for the effective performance of their tasks, including those related to mutual assistance and cooperation with other supervisory authorities throughout the Union. 2Each supervisory authority should have a separate, public annual budget, which may be part of the overall state or national budget.
involved_parties: Various EU DPAs (e.g., BfDI Germany, Garante Italy)

example_name: German BfDI Annual Budget Allocation
example_background: The Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI) oversees federal data protection in Germany, handling complaints from public and private sectors. Post-GDPR, it processed 15,000 cases yearly, necessitating separate budget lines for IT security and cross-border cooperation tools. The federal government provided dedicated funding outside general ministry allocations.
process_and_outcome: 2023 budget reached €28 million with 300 staff, enabling fines of €100 million issued that year. Annual reports confirmed full compliance with resource mandates.

involved_parties: German Federal Ministry of the Interior

example_name: Dutch DPA Staff Shortages Exposed in WhatsApp Fine
example_background: The Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (AP) in the Netherlands investigated WhatsApp's data sharing practices under GDPR's one-stop-shop. Limited human resources delayed coordination with the Irish DPC and other authorities, extending the probe over three years amid thousands of similar complaints EU-wide.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, AP issued a €225 million fine independently after EDPB input. Government responded with €10 million budget boost in 2022 to hire 50 more staff.

example_name: Italian Garante Resource Enhancement Post-Fine Surge
example_background: The Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali faced over 5,000 complaints annually and issued record fines, including €10 million to Google in 2021. Inadequate premises and IT led to inefficiencies in mutual assistance. Italy allocated a separate public budget line.
process_and_outcome: 2022 budget increased to €22 million, adding 100 staff and new facilities. Enabled processing 6,500 cases and €28 million in fines.

example_name: Belgian DPA Underfunding Criticism 2021
example_background: The Gegevensbeschermingsautoriteit (GBA) in Belgium managed a high volume of SME complaints and tech probes with a modest €12 million budget and 120 staff. Delays in cases like Facebook tracking affected EU cooperation.
process_and_outcome: European Parliament report highlighted issues; Belgium raised budget to €18 million in 2023, improving case resolution rates.

1Each supervisory authority should be provided with the financial and human resources, premises and infrastructure necessary for the effective performance of their tasks, including those related to mutual assistance and cooperation with other supervisory authorities throughout the Union. 2Each supervisory authority should have a separate, public annual budget, which may be part of the overall state or national budget.

example_name: UK ICO Commissioner Appointment Controversy 2016
example_background: In the United Kingdom, the appointment process for the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) Commissioner Elizabeth Denham in 2016 drew attention due to concerns over the transparency and independence of the selection procedure. The position was advertised with a salary range that was later increased, and the process involved the Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) handling applications. Critics, including openDemocracy and others, questioned whether the process fully adhered to requirements for an independent and transparent appointment, especially as the ICO oversees data protection enforcement under upcoming GDPR implementation. The role required the appointee to demonstrate independence from government influence, and public discourse highlighted potential risks to the authority's perceived neutrality given the government's direct involvement in shortlisting and final recommendation to Parliament.
process_and_outcome: Parliamentary questions were raised, and the House of Commons Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Committee reviewed the process. Denham was appointed by the Queen on the advice of the Secretary of State. No formal sanctions were imposed, but the episode prompted discussions on strengthening safeguards. Subsequent guidance was issued to improve transparency in public appointments.
source_rule:
Recital 121 Independence of the Supervisory Authorities*
1The general conditions for the member or members of the supervisory authority should be laid down by law in each Member State and should in particular provide that those members are to be appointed, by means of a transparent procedure, either by the parliament, government or the head of State of the Member State on the basis of a proposal from the government, a member of the government, the parliament or a chamber of the parliament, or by an independent body entrusted under Member State law. 2In order to ensure the independence of the supervisory authority, the member or members should act with integrity, refrain from any action that is incompatible with their duties and should not, during their term of office, engage in any incompatible occupation, whether gainful or not. 3The supervisory authority should have its own staff, chosen by the supervisory authority or an independent body established by Member State law, which should be subject to the exclusive direction of the member or members of the supervisory authority.
involved_parties: Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS)
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 52 and Recital 121 (independence of supervisory authorities)
UK Public Appointments Code of Practice

example_name: French CNIL Independent Structure Reforms 2019
example_background: The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) in France underwent structural adjustments to align with GDPR requirements for supervisory authority independence. Prior to full GDPR enforcement, CNIL's composition included members appointed by the President, Parliament, and judiciary. Post-GDPR, Law No. 2018-493 formalized a collegial structure with 18 members appointed through diverse channels: 4 by the President of the Republic, 2 by the President of the National Assembly, 2 by the President of the Senate, 4 by the President of the Constitutional Council from the judiciary, 3 by the National Assembly from its members, and 3 by the Senate from its members. This ensured balanced representation and separation from single executive control, with members serving non-renewable 6-year terms and prohibited from other occupations.
process_and_outcome: The reforms were implemented without challenge, enabling CNIL to issue major fines such as against Google in 2019 for €50 million. CNIL maintained operational autonomy, hiring staff under its exclusive direction.

example_name: German State DSA Appointment Challenges
example_background: In the German federal state of Hesse, the appointment of the Data Protection Supervisory Authority (DSA) head in 2017 faced scrutiny when the state parliament elected Thilo Weichert, a long-serving data protection expert. While Weichert had prior roles in other state authorities, concerns arose about potential carryover influences from previous positions and the parliament's role in the election process. GDPR Recital 121 emphasizes appointments via transparent procedures by parliament or independent bodies to safeguard independence. Critics noted that political affiliations of nominating parties could indirectly affect neutrality, though Weichert was selected from multiple candidates.
process_and_outcome: The appointment proceeded, but it led to public debates and calls for more stringent independence criteria. The Hessian DSA continued operations, handling complaints and enforcement independently.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Independence Requirements 2019
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued formal guidance in 2019 addressing national implementations of supervisory authority independence under GDPR. Several member states' drafts were reviewed, highlighting issues such as government veto powers over budgets, staff appointments by external ministries, and members holding parallel political roles. For instance, initial Italian proposals allowed government appointment of the Garante president with short terms, while Spanish structures involved parliamentary but politically influenced selections. The EDPB stressed that members must refrain from incompatible occupations and that staff must be under exclusive authority direction.
process_and_outcome: EDPB opinions prompted revisions in multiple states, including Italy and Spain, leading to enhanced protections like longer non-renewable terms and budget autonomy. No fines, but structural changes enforced.

involved_parties: National supervisory authorities in Italy, Spain, others

example_name: Irish DPC Governance Model Post-GDPR
example_background: Ireland's Data Protection Commission (DPC) established a two-tier structure compliant with GDPR independence standards. The DPC comprises a Commission with a Chairperson and two Commissioners, appointed by the Government on Parliamentary recommendation following public competition by the Public Appointments Service. Appointments are for fixed 4-year terms, non-renewable consecutively, with requirements for relevant expertise and no incompatible occupations. Staff are recruited independently under DPC direction, with budget allocated via the Department of Justice but ring-fenced for operational autonomy.
process_and_outcome: The model withstood scrutiny in high-profile cases like WhatsApp enforcement in 2020, where DPC handled investigations autonomously despite criticisms of leniency. No independence-based challenges succeeded.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Collegial Independence Structure
example_background: The Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) operates as a collegial body with a President and Deputy appointed by Congress of Deputies from a list of three candidates proposed by the Government, ensuring parliamentary oversight. Members serve 5-year terms, renewable once, and are barred from other public or private roles during tenure. Staff selection is managed internally, with exclusive direction by the Council. This setup was adjusted post-GDPR to prevent executive dominance, reflecting Recital 121's emphasis on transparent, multi-branch appointment processes.
process_and_outcome: AEPD issued fines such as €15 million to Vodafone in 2021, demonstrating operational independence. The structure has been upheld without legal challenges on independence grounds.

example_name: Dutch CBP/AP Independence Audit 2020
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens, AP) faced an internal and external review of its independence following GDPR transposition. Concerns emerged over the Minister of Justice's role in budget approvals and staff secondments from government departments, potentially conflicting with exclusive direction requirements. Members are appointed by Royal Decree on Council of State advice for 4-year terms, with prohibitions on other occupations. The review examined whether these arrangements fully isolated the AP from ministerial interference.
process_and_outcome: The audit recommended and led to procedural enhancements, including dedicated budget lines and minimized secondments. No penalties, but adjustments made to reinforce autonomy.

example_name: Italian Garante Privacy Authority Reforms 2018
example_background: Italy's Garante per la protezione dei dati personali restructured under GDPR to feature a President appointed by Parliament from government-nominated candidates and four members elected by Chambers with two-thirds majority. Terms are 7 years, non-renewable, with strict rules against incompatible activities. Staff are selected autonomously. This addressed prior concerns where executive influence was stronger, aligning with Recital 121's call for parliamentary or independent body appointments to ensure integrity and separation from daily politics.
process_and_outcome: Reforms enabled Garante to enforce fines like €28.5 million against Google in 2020. The collegial setup has been praised for balanced decision-making.

example_name: Austrian DSB Appointment Transparency Case 2019
example_background: The Austrian Datenschutzbehörde (DSB) appointment of its director by the Federal Chancellor on proposal from the Justice Minister raised questions under GDPR scrutiny. While parliamentary notification occurs post-appointment, the process lacked the multi-stakeholder proposal mechanisms outlined in Recital 121. The appointee must act independently, but critics argued the executive-centric selection risked perceived bias, especially with staff partially funded through federal channels.
process_and_outcome: No formal enforcement, but EDPB noted concerns in opinions, leading to discussions on procedural transparency enhancements without structural change.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Lead Authority Case (2023)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, the EU headquarters for Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, engaged in behavioral advertising practices that involved processing personal data of users across multiple European Union member states. The company's main establishment was in Ireland, making the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) the potential lead authority. However, the processing substantially affected data subjects in other member states such as Germany, France, Italy, and Spain due to the widespread use of targeted ads based on user data collected EU-wide. Other national supervisory authorities raised objections, arguing that the Irish DPC should designate itself as lead authority and coordinate a cross-border investigation rather than handling it as a purely local matter. This led to prolonged discussions within the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) on the criteria for determining substantial effects across borders.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision in April 2023, directing the Irish DPC to proceed with a cross-border investigation and impose corrective measures. The Irish DPC subsequently fined Meta €1.2 billion for violations related to data transfers to the US, with the lead authority mechanism ensuring consistency across member states.
source_rule:
Recital 124 Lead Authority Regarding Processing in Several Member States*
1Where the processing of personal data takes place in the context of the activities of an establishment of a controller or a processor in the Union and the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State, or where processing taking place in the context of the activities of a single establishment of a controller or processor in the Union substantially affects or is likely to substantially affect data subjects in more than one Member State, the supervisory authority for the main establishment of the controller or processor or for the single establishment of the controller or processor should act as lead authority. 2It should cooperate with the other authorities concerned, because the controller or processor has an establishment on the territory of their Member State, because data subjects residing on their territory are substantially affected, or because a complaint has been lodged with them. 3Also where a data subject not residing in that Member State has lodged a complaint, the supervisory authority with which such complaint has been lodged should also be a supervisory authority concerned. 4Within its tasks to issue guidelines on any question covering the application of this Regulation, the Board should be able to issue guidelines in particular on the criteria to be taken into account in order to ascertain whether the processing in question substantially affects data subjects in more than one Member State and on what constitutes a relevant and reasoned objection.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
German, French, Italian, Spanish DPAs (concerned authorities)

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Lead Authority Dispute (2022)
example_background: Google Ireland Limited provided analytics services to controllers across the EU, with processing activities occurring through its main establishment in Ireland. Data processing involved tracking user behavior on websites hosted by controllers in multiple member states, substantially affecting data subjects in countries like Austria, Italy, and Germany. The Austrian DPA initiated an investigation into a local controller's use of Google Analytics, but other DPAs argued that the Irish DPC should act as lead authority given Google's EU base and the cross-border nature of the service provision.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB clarified in 2022 guidelines that Google Ireland should be treated as the lead processor, with the Irish DPC as lead authority for cross-border processing. This resulted in coordinated guidance and multiple national bans lifted pending lead authority review.
source_rule:
Recital 124 Lead Authority Regarding Processing in Several Member States*

relevant_rules: EDPB Guidelines 2/2019 on Lead SA Criteria

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing Fine (2023)
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, established in Ireland as its main EU entity, processed personal data of minors across the European Union through its short-video platform. The processing from the single Irish establishment substantially affected children in multiple member states, including the Netherlands, Ireland, and others, due to age verification failures and default public profiles. The Dutch DPA conducted an initial investigation based on complaints from Dutch residents, but other authorities asserted that the Irish DPC should serve as lead authority given TikTok's main establishment.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision in 2023, confirming the Irish DPC as lead authority. The DPC imposed a €345 million fine on TikTok for GDPR violations related to children's data, with cooperation among concerned authorities.

example_name: Amazon EU Lead Authority Designation (2021)
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., with its main establishment in Luxembourg, operated e-commerce and advertising services processing EU-wide personal data. Advertising practices substantially affected consumers in multiple member states from this single establishment. The Luxembourg DPA (CNPD) was designated as lead authority, cooperating with DPAs from France (CNIL), Germany (BfDI), and others following complaints about targeted ads.
process_and_outcome: The CNPD, as lead authority, conducted a cross-border investigation with input from concerned authorities, issuing a formal decision in 2021 on compliance with data minimization principles. No fine was imposed at that stage, but ongoing monitoring was established.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Cross-Border Case (2020)
example_background: British Airways, part of International Consolidated Airlines Group with EU establishments including in the UK (pre-Brexit) and Ireland, experienced a data breach affecting 400,000 customers across Europe. Processing occurred in the context of multiple EU establishments, with substantial effects on data subjects in the UK, Ireland, Germany, and France. The UK ICO initially acted but faced questions on lead authority status post-Brexit, leading to coordination with Irish and other DPAs.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined British Airways £20 million in 2020, with input from EU concerned authorities under GDPR cooperation mechanisms. The case highlighted transitional lead authority issues.

example_name: Schrems II Follow-on Lead Authority Actions (2020-2023)
example_background: Following the Schrems II CJEU ruling invalidating Privacy Shield, Facebook's data transfers from Ireland to the US involved processing affecting users across all EU member states. The Irish DPC, as lead authority for Facebook's main establishment, handled the primary investigation, but 11 other DPAs objected, citing substantial effects on their residents and seeking stronger remedies.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision in 2020 compelled the Irish DPC to suspend transfers and impose suspensions. In 2023, a €1.2 billion fine was levied, demonstrating lead authority coordination.
source_rule:
Recital 124 Lead Authority Regarding Processing in Several Member States*
1Where the processing of personal data takes place in the context of the activities of an establishment of a controller or a processor in the Union and the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State, or where processing taking place in the context of the activities of a single establishment of a controller or processor in the Union substantially affects or is likely to substantially affect data subjects in more than one Member State, the supervisory authority for the main establishment of the controller or processor or for the single establishment of the controller or processor should act as lead authority. 2It should cooperate with the other authorities concerned, because the controller or processor has an establishment on the territory of their Member State, because data subjects residing on their territory are substantially affected, or because a complaint has been lodged with them. 3Also where a data subject not residing in that Member State has lodged a complaint, the supervisory authority with which such complaint has been lodged should also be a supervisory authority concerned. 4Within its tasks to issue guidelines on any question covering the application of this Regulation, the Board should be able to issue guidelines in particular on the criteria to be taken into account in order to ascertain whether the processing in question substantially affects data subjects in more than one Member State and on what constitutes a relevant and reasoned objection.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud Services Lead SA Cooperation (2022)
example_background: Microsoft Ireland Operations Limited provided Azure and other cloud services to controllers in multiple EU states, with processing from its Irish main establishment substantially affecting data subjects EU-wide. National DPAs in Germany and Italy raised concerns about data retention practices, triggering cooperation under the lead authority framework.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC, as lead authority, engaged in joint case handling with concerned authorities, issuing joint guidance in 2022 on cloud processing compliance. No fines were issued, affirming the process.

example_name: Clearview AI Multi-State Enforcement (2022)
example_background: Clearview AI, a US-based facial recognition company with no EU establishment but processing scraped data of EU residents substantially affecting data subjects in multiple member states (France, Greece, Italy, etc.). Complaints led to fragmented national actions, highlighting the absence of a clear lead authority for non-established processors.
process_and_outcome: Multiple DPAs banned Clearview AI and imposed fines totaling over €30 million (e.g., French CNIL €20m, Italian €20m). EDPB coordinated for consistency despite no single lead SA.

involved_parties: French CNIL, Italian Garante, Greek DPA (national authorities)

example_name: EDPB Guidelines 2/2019 Implementation - Uber Case (2021)
example_background: Uber B.V., main establishment in the Netherlands, processed driver and rider data across EU member states from this single base, substantially affecting data subjects in France, Spain, Italy, etc. The Dutch DPA acted as lead authority following complaints about data sharing with US entities.
process_and_outcome: Dutch DPA fined Uber €10 million in 2021 after lead authority investigation with concerned authorities' input, enforcing consistent application per EDPB guidelines.

example_name: Amazon E-Commerce Data Processing Fine
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. engaged in personalized advertising using personal data of EU customers without adequate transparency or legal basis. The processing was conducted through establishments in Luxembourg but affected data subjects across the EU, including Italy. The Italian data protection authority (Garante) initiated proceedings based on complaints from Italian residents, demonstrating its competence over processing affecting its territory.
process_and_outcome: The Garante investigated and in 2021 fined Amazon €10 million. The case was part of broader EU scrutiny, leading Amazon to adjust its practices. Further investigations by other authorities followed.
source_rule:
Recital 122 Responsibility of the Supervisory Authorities*
1Each supervisory authority should be competent on the territory of its own Member State to exercise the powers and to perform the tasks conferred on it in accordance with this Regulation. 2This should cover in particular the processing in the context of the activities of an establishment of the controller or processor on the territory of its own Member State, the processing of personal data carried out by public authorities or private bodies acting in the public interest, processing affecting data subjects on its territory or processing carried out by a controller or processor not established in the Union when targeting data subjects residing on its territory. 3This should include handling complaints lodged by a data subject, conducting investigations on the application of this Regulation and promoting public awareness of the risks, rules, safeguards and rights in relation to the processing of personal data.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (Italian supervisory authority)

example_name: H&M Data Breach by German DPA
example_background: H&M employees in Germany shared sensitive personal data of over 1,000 employees obtained during onboarding with colleagues for non-work purposes, such as generating predictions about employee behavior. The processing occurred within H&M's German establishment, falling under the competence of the Hamburg DPA, which received complaints from affected data subjects.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg DPA investigated and in 2023 imposed a €35.25 million fine, one of the largest in Germany. H&M was required to enhance internal data handling policies.

involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (German supervisory authority)

example_name: CNIL's Public Awareness Campaign on Data Rights
example_background: The French CNIL launched extensive public awareness initiatives, including websites, videos, and events, to inform citizens about data protection rights, processing risks, and safeguards under GDPR. These activities were conducted as part of its mandate on French territory, covering processing by controllers and processors targeting French residents.
process_and_outcome: The campaigns reached millions, with evaluations showing increased public knowledge. CNIL reported successful promotion of awareness without formal enforcement, demonstrating proactive exercise of its powers.
source_rule:

example_name: Irish DPC Cross-Border Complaint Handling
example_background: The Irish DPC efficiently handled thousands of cross-border complaints against major tech firms established in Ireland, such as Facebook and Twitter, coordinating with other EU authorities under the one-stop-shop mechanism. This included investigations into data processing affecting data subjects across member states.
process_and_outcome: From 2018-2023, the DPC resolved over 10,000 complaints, issuing guidance and fines where needed, enhancing overall GDPR enforcement cooperation.
source_rule:
Recital 122 Responsibility of the Supervisory Authorities*
1Each supervisory authority should be competent on the territory of its own Member State to exercise the powers and to perform the tasks conferred on it in accordance with this Regulation. 2This should cover in particular the processing in the context of the activities of an establishment of the controller or processor on the territory of its own Member State, the processing of personal data carried out by public authorities or private bodies acting in the public interest, processing affecting data subjects on its territory or processing carried out by a controller or processor not established in the Union when targeting data subjects residing on its territory. 3This should include handling complaints lodged by a data subject, conducting investigations on the application of this Regulation and promoting public awareness of the risks, rules, safeguards and rights in relation to the processing of personal data.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited - Italian Employee Data Processing Case
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, the EU headquarters for Facebook's data processing, handled personal data of Italian users through its platform. The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) identified a specific processing activity concerning Italian users that was limited to Italy. This processing involved user data collected and managed solely within the Italian context, without cross-border implications to other Member States. The case arose from complaints regarding data handling practices specific to Italian users, prompting the Garante to assess whether it qualified as a local case under GDPR mechanisms. Meta's structure as a controller established in multiple Member States triggered the need to evaluate the one-stop-shop and local processing rules. The Garante proceeded to investigate the matter independently after determining its local nature, focusing on compliance with notification and decision-making protocols between supervisory authorities.
process_and_outcome: The Garante conducted an investigation and imposed a fine of €10 million on Meta Platforms Ireland Limited in 2023 for violations related to the processing of Italian users' data. The authority informed the lead supervisory authority, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC), which agreed that the case could be handled locally due to its confinement to Italy. No further escalation occurred under the one-stop-shop mechanism.
source_rule:
Recital 127 Information of the Supervisory Authority Regarding Local Processing*
1Each supervisory authority not acting as the lead supervisory authority should be competent to handle local cases where the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State, but the subject matter of the specific processing concerns only processing carried out in a single Member State and involves only data subjects in that single Member State, for example, where the subject matter concerns the processing of employees’ personal data in the specific employment context of a Member State. 2In such cases, the supervisory authority should inform the lead supervisory authority without delay about the matter. 3After being informed, the lead supervisory authority should decide, whether it will handle the case pursuant to the provision on cooperation between the lead supervisory authority and other supervisory authorities concerned (‘one-stop-shop mechanism’), or whether the supervisory authority which informed it should handle the case at local level. 4When deciding whether it will handle the case, the lead supervisory authority should take into account whether there is an establishment of the controller or processor in the Member State of the supervisory authority which informed it in order to ensure effective enforcement of a decision vis-à-vis the controller or processor. 5Where the lead supervisory authority decides to handle the case, the supervisory authority which informed it should have the possibility to submit a draft for a decision, of which the lead supervisory authority should take utmost account when preparing its draft decision in that one-stop-shop mechanism.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (local supervisory authority)
Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: TikTok Italia - Local Processing of Children's Data
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating through its Italian subsidiary, processes personal data of users in Italy, including a significant number of minors. The Italian Garante identified processing activities related to age verification and content recommendations that were specific to Italian users and did not extend to other Member States. This involved data of children under 14, collected via the app's features tailored to the Italian market. As TikTok has establishments across the EU, the Garante notified the lead supervisory authority, the Irish DPC, to determine handling under the cooperation framework. The case highlighted challenges in distinguishing local processing from cross-border activities in social media platforms.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Garante fined TikTok €10 million and issued orders for corrective measures. The Irish DPC was informed promptly, and after consultation, the Garante handled the case locally, enforcing decisions directly enforceable in Italy due to TikTok's local presence.
