involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: CNIL's Meta Platforms Biometric Data Decision
example_background: Meta Platforms processed biometric data via Facebook's photo tagging feature using AI to recognize faces without explicit consent, affecting EU users' sensitive data from 2012-2022. The feature was reactivated post-GDPR without proper legal basis updates.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €20 million injunction in 2022, ordering cessation within two months under threat of daily penalties, demonstrating temporary limitation powers. Meta complied after initial resistance.
source_rule:
Recital 129 Tasks and Powers of the Supervisory Authorities*
1In order to ensure consistent monitoring and enforcement of this Regulation throughout the Union, the supervisory authorities should have in each Member State the same tasks and effective powers, including powers of investigation, corrective powers and sanctions, and authorisation and advisory powers, in particular in cases of complaints from natural persons, and without prejudice to the powers of prosecutorial authorities under Member State law, to bring infringements of this Regulation to the attention of the judicial authorities and engage in legal proceedings. 2Such powers should also include the power to impose a temporary or definitive limitation, including a ban, on processing. 3Member States may specify other tasks related to the protection of personal data under this Regulation. 4The powers of supervisory authorities should be exercised in accordance with appropriate procedural safeguards set out in Union and Member State law, impartially, fairly and within a reasonable time. 5In particular each measure should be appropriate, necessary and proportionate in view of ensuring compliance with this Regulation, taking into account the circumstances of each individual case, respect the right of every person to be heard before any individual measure which would affect him or her adversely is taken and avoid superfluous costs and excessive inconveniences for the persons concerned. 6Investigatory powers as regards access to premises should be exercised in accordance with specific requirements in Member State procedural law, such as the requirement to obtain a prior judicial authorisation. 7Each legally binding measure of the supervisory authority should be in writing, be clear and unambiguous, indicate the supervisory authority which has issued the measure, the date of issue of the measure, bear the signature of the head, or a member of the supervisory authority authorised by him or her, give the reasons for the measure, and refer to the right of an effective remedy. 8This should not preclude additional requirements pursuant to Member State procedural law. 9The adoption of a legally binding decision implies that it may give rise to judicial review in the Member State of the supervisory authority that adopted the decision.

example_name: TikTok EDPB Binding Decision Case
example_background: TikTok Ireland Limited, the lead supervisory authority for TikTok in the EU, conducted an investigation into TikTok's processing of personal data of minors. Concerns arose regarding age verification, default privacy settings, and data protection measures. The Dutch Data Protection Authority (DPA) identified issues during its own inquiry and submitted a relevant and reasoned objection under GDPR Chapter VII, challenging TikTok's compliance. Due to the cross-border nature affecting users across multiple EU member states, the case was escalated to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) for resolution. The objection process highlighted discrepancies in enforcement approaches between national authorities.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA's objection was declared admissible by the EDPB. After deliberation, the EDPB adopted a binding decision on August 2, 2023, requiring TikTok to implement specific measures including age assurance tools, enhanced parental controls, and privacy settings by default for minors. TikTok was given deadlines for compliance, with ongoing monitoring by the lead authority and cooperation mandated among involved DPAs.
source_rule:
Recital 133 Mutual Assistance and Provisional Measures*
1The supervisory authorities should assist each other in performing their tasks and provide mutual assistance, so as to ensure the consistent application and enforcement of this Regulation in the internal market. 2A supervisory authority requesting mutual assistance may adopt a provisional measure if it receives no response to a request for mutual assistance within one month of the receipt of that request by the other supervisory authority.

example_name: Meta GDPRinz Principle Case
example_background: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) investigated Meta Platforms Ireland Limited's practices related to behavioral advertising and data retention periods under the GDPRin principle framework. Following an inquiry prompted by complaints about excessive data processing, the Garante submitted a relevant and reasoned objection, asserting that Meta's data retention exceeded what was necessary. This objection was part of a coordinated effort involving multiple EU supervisory authorities to ensure uniform application across the internal market, given Meta's cross-border processing affecting millions of users.
process_and_outcome: The case was referred to the EDPB under the GDPRin procedure. On April 20, 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision upholding the objection, mandating Meta to adjust its data retention periods and delete unlawfully stored data. Meta faced enforcement actions in Italy, including a temporary suspension of data processing, with further implementation monitored through mutual assistance among DPAs.
source_rule:
Recital 133 Mutual Assistance and Provisional Measures*
1The supervisory authorities should assist each other in performing their tasks and provide mutual assistance, so as to ensure the consistent application and enforcement of this Regulation in the internal market. 2A supervisory authority requesting mutual assistance may adopt a provisional measure if it receives no response to a request for mutual assistance within one month of the receipt of that request by the other supervisory authority.

example_name: Schrems II Follow-up Cooperation
example_background: Following the Court of Justice of the EU's Schrems II judgment in 2020, which invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield, multiple EU supervisory authorities initiated inquiries into data transfers by US-based tech companies. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC), as lead authority for Facebook (Meta), faced pressure from other DPAs like those in France (CNIL) and Germany (BayLDA) to address ongoing transfers using Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) without adequate safeguards. This led to structured mutual assistance requests to examine supplementary measures for data protection in transfers.
process_and_outcome: Through extensive mutual assistance under GDPR Chapter VII, the DPC coordinated with 11 other concerned SAs. In May 2023, the DPC imposed orders on Meta requiring suspension of transatlantic data transfers absent sufficient protections, with appeal pending. The process exemplified prolonged cooperation to achieve consistent enforcement.
source_rule:
Recital 133 Mutual Assistance and Provisional Measures*
1The supervisory authorities should assist each other in performing their tasks and provide mutual assistance, so as to ensure the consistent application and enforcement of this Regulation in the internal market. 2A supervisory authority requesting mutual assistance may adopt a provisional measure if it receives no response to a request for mutual assistance within one month of the receipt of that request by the other supervisory authority.

example_name: Clearview AI Cross-Border Enforcement
example_background: Clearview AI, a US facial recognition company, faced complaints across EU member states for scraping billions of facial images without consent for its database. Multiple DPAs, including those in France, Greece, Italy, and the Netherlands, initiated investigations. As no EU establishment existed initially, authorities used mutual assistance and one-stop-shop mechanisms, later designating the Dutch DPA as lead after Clearview's EU incorporation.
process_and_outcome: Joint enforcement through mutual assistance resulted in a €30.5 million fine by the Dutch AP in 2022, upheld despite appeals. Other DPAs imposed additional penalties totaling over €40 million, demonstrating coordinated provisional bans and data deletion orders.
source_rule:
Recital 133 Mutual Assistance and Provisional Measures*
1The supervisory authorities should assist each other in performing their tasks and provide mutual assistance, so as to ensure the consistent application and enforcement of this Regulation in the internal market. 2A supervisory authority requesting mutual assistance may adopt a provisional measure if it receives no response to a request for mutual assistance within one month of the receipt of that request by the other supervisory authority.

example_name: Google AdSense GDPRin Case
example_background: The French CNIL examined Google's AdSense consent platform for cookie-based advertising, finding insufficient user consent mechanisms. As Google's lead SA is Ireland's DPC, CNIL issued a reasoned objection under GDPRin, prompting a dispute resolution process involving multiple DPAs concerned with consistent ad tech enforcement.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision in 2022, requiring Google to ensure granular consent and pause processing. This followed mutual assistance exchanges, resulting in fines and remediation across EU markets.
source_rule:

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Cooperation
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a data breach exposing 400,000 customers' payment details via a Magecart attack on its website. The UK ICO investigated, but post-Brexit, EU DPAs requested mutual assistance under GDPR as BA (IAG) had EU operations. This involved sharing evidence on breach notification and security measures.
process_and_outcome: Through mutual assistance, the ICO issued a £20 million fine in 2020 (reduced from £183m on appeal). EU DPAs used the shared information for parallel probes, ensuring consistent application.
source_rule:

example_name: WhatsApp Metadata Processing Dispute
example_background: Germany's BayLDA investigated WhatsApp's processing of user metadata for advertising, integrated from Facebook. Finding potential violations of purpose limitation, BayLDA objected to Ireland's DPC, WhatsApp's lead SA, initiating a GDPRin procedure amid concerns from other DPAs about cross-border impacts.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB's binding decision in 2023 upheld parts of the objection, requiring WhatsApp to limit metadata use. This followed mutual assistance and led to a €225 million fine by the Irish DPC.

example_name: CNIL Meta Platforms Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms' Facebook service used 'cookie banners' that did not provide a proper refusal option equivalent to acceptance for non-essential cookies. Users were nudged towards consenting to tracking cookies for advertising, affecting data processing for behavioral advertising across EU users. The setup persisted despite regulatory feedback.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Meta €60 million in March 2022. Meta updated its cookie consent mechanisms to offer equal prominence for accept and refuse options.
source_rule:
Recital 132 Awareness-Raising Activities and Specific Measures*
Awareness-raising activities by supervisory authorities addressed to the public should include specific measures directed at controllers and processors, including micro, small and medium-sized enterprises, as well as natural persons in particular in the educational context.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: Italian DPA Small Business GDPR Fine
example_background: A small Italian parking company, Easypark Italia, was found processing vehicle license plate data via its app without adequate legal basis or transparency notices. The app scanned plates for parking payments, but lacked privacy information and proper data minimization, affecting drivers using the service.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Garante fined the company €30,000 in 2020. The company updated its app with privacy notices and consent mechanisms.
source_rule:
Recital 132 Awareness-Raising Activities and Specific Measures*

involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (Italian DPA)

example_name: EDPB SME Awareness Campaign
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) launched coordinated awareness-raising campaigns targeting controllers and processors, with a focus on micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). Activities included webinars, guides on GDPR compliance for SMEs, checklists for data protection officers, and simplified toolkits for privacy impact assessments, distributed across EU member states to enhance understanding of obligations.
process_and_outcome: Campaigns reached thousands of SMEs through national DPAs, resulting in increased engagement such as higher webinar attendance and resource downloads. No fines issued; instead, voluntary compliance improvements reported by participants.
source_rule:
Recital 132 Awareness-Raising Activities and Specific Measures*
Awareness-raising activities by supervisory authorities addressed to the public should include specific measures directed at controllers and processors, including micro, small and medium-sized enterprises, as well as natural persons in particular in the educational context.

example_name: ICO SME GDPR Support Program
example_background: The UK ICO developed a comprehensive support program for small businesses and organizations, including free online tools, plain-language guides, self-assessment checklists, and webinars on topics like data breach reporting and lawful basis for processing. The initiative targeted natural persons and SMEs to build practical GDPR knowledge without requiring legal expertise.
process_and_outcome: Over 100,000 small businesses accessed resources, with feedback surveys indicating improved compliance confidence. The program contributed to a rise in proactive breach notifications from SMEs.
source_rule:
Recital 132 Awareness-Raising Activities and Specific Measures*
Awareness-raising activities by supervisory authorities addressed to the public should include specific measures directed at controllers and processors, including micro, small and medium-sized enterprises, as well as natural persons in particular in the educational context.

example_name: Meta Platforms EDPB Binding Decision on Targeted Advertising
example_background: Meta Platforms operated behavioral advertising practices across the European Union that involved processing personal data of users in multiple member states through its platforms Facebook and Instagram. The practices included combining user data from various sources to create detailed profiles for delivering personalized ads, affecting millions of users throughout the EU. Initial handling by the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) proposed measures that other supervisory authorities contested due to concerns over uniform application of data processing rules. The process involved extensive submissions and arguments from Meta on its legal bases for processing, including legitimate interests and consent mechanisms, while supervisory authorities highlighted the need for consistent standards on automated decision-making and profiling across borders.
process_and_outcome: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) adopted a binding decision in 2023, requiring the Irish DPC to amend its draft decision. Meta was ordered to suspend the practice of behavioral advertising based on parametric behavioral signals for EU users until compliant mechanisms were implemented. The Irish DPC subsequently imposed fines totaling €1.2 billion on Meta in related proceedings for unlawful data transfers, with ongoing remediation requirements.
source_rule:
Recital 135 Consistency Mechanism*
1In order to ensure the consistent application of this Regulation throughout the Union, a consistency mechanism for cooperation between the supervisory authorities should be established. 2That mechanism should in particular apply where a supervisory authority intends to adopt a measure intended to produce legal effects as regards processing operations which substantially affect a significant number of data subjects in several Member States. 3It should also apply where any supervisory authority concerned or the Commission requests that such matter should be handled in the consistency mechanism. 4That mechanism should be without prejudice to any measures that the Commission may take in the exercise of its powers under the Treaties.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
European Data Protection Board (consistency mechanism body)

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing Binding Decision
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited processed personal data of minors across the European Economic Area via its short-video platform, applying age verification and default privacy settings that impacted users in numerous member states. The Irish DPC's draft decision on compliance with rules for child data processing was challenged by other EU supervisory authorities, who sought greater alignment on enforcement for cross-border services. The case examined settings allowing children under 13 to share public content and data retention practices, with input from TikTok on its algorithmic content moderation and parental controls.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision under the consistency mechanism, directing the Irish DPC to impose a €345 million fine on TikTok for infringements related to children's data. Additional orders included implementing age-appropriate design features and suspending certain data processing activities until compliance was achieved.

involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
EU supervisory authorities from Netherlands, France, etc. (objectors)

example_name: Amazon Advertising Services Consistency Dispute
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. engaged in data processing for online advertising services that utilized user data from multiple EU countries, involving tracking and profiling activities across its e-commerce platform. The Luxembourg CNPD's draft decision was objected to by the French CNIL and others, triggering the consistency mechanism due to the service's substantial effects on data subjects in several member states. Discussions focused on consent validity, data minimization, and transparency in ad personalization technologies.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision in 2023, mandating the CNPD to fine Amazon €746 million for GDPR violations in advertising practices. Amazon appealed the decision, with proceedings ongoing, and was required to implement corrective measures for cross-border compliance.

involved_parties: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. (data controller)

example_name: WhatsApp Ireland Transparency Obligations Case
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited provided messaging services processing data of users across the EU, with its privacy policy updates affecting transparency obligations for all member states. The Irish DPC's initial draft decision faced objections from other authorities like the Dutch AP, leading to consistency mechanism activation over uniform enforcement of information provision rules under GDPR.
process_and_outcome: EDPB's 2021 binding decision required the Irish DPC to impose a €225 million fine on WhatsApp for non-transparent processing disclosures. WhatsApp implemented policy revisions and faced further EU-wide audits.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Max Schrems Facebook Case
example_background: Facebook Ireland Ltd conducted data processing for social networking services impacting users in multiple EU states, with practices involving international data transfers. The Irish DPC's draft was disputed by Austrian and other authorities, invoking consistency for measures affecting significant numbers of data subjects across borders.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, EDPB binding decision under urgency procedure directed suspension of data transfers to the US and fines up to 4% of global turnover. Led to Irish DPC's €1.2 billion fine in 2023.

example_name: Google Analytics Consent Consistency Case
example_background: Multiple EU supervisory authorities addressed the use of Google Analytics tools by websites, involving cross-border data transfers and cookie consent practices affecting users in several member states. Coordinated actions highlighted the need for consistent application of consent requirements for analytics processing.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, authorities like CNIL (France) declared non-compliance, blocking transfers, with EDPB guidance promoting uniform enforcement. Google adjusted services EU-wide.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Cross-Border Enforcement
example_background: Clearview AI scraped and processed biometric data from public sources worldwide, supplying facial recognition services used by entities in multiple EU countries, substantially affecting data subjects across member states. Supervisory authorities coordinated via consistency mechanisms due to the technology's broad impact.
process_and_outcome: EDPB adopted a binding decision in 2022, leading to bans and fines totaling over €30 million from authorities in France, Greece, Italy, etc., with orders to cease processing.
source_rule:
Recital 135 Consistency Mechanism*

example_name: EDPB Dispute Resolution on Planet49 Consent Banners
example_background: The Planet49 case involved cookie consent banners on websites across EU states, with non-essential cookies set without proper consent, affecting users in multiple jurisdictions. The EDPB handled a dispute between lead and concerned authorities to ensure consistent rulings on pre-ticked boxes and consent validity.
process_and_outcome: EDPB's 2020 binding decision affirmed that pre-checked boxes do not constitute valid consent, influencing uniform enforcement and fines across EU for similar practices.

example_name: OneTrust VendorPortal GDPR Consistency Best Practice
example_background: OneTrust developed a data privacy management platform used by multinational companies to handle cross-border data processing agreements and transfer impact assessments, facilitating cooperation among EU supervisory authorities for consistent compliance documentation across member states.
process_and_outcome: Adopted by numerous EU entities, it supported successful EDPB consultations and avoided consistency disputes by standardizing DPIAs and transfer tools, recognized in industry reports for enabling compliant scaling.

example_name: Meta Platforms EDPB Cross-Border Case
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the lead supervisory authority for Facebook and Instagram services in the EU, handled multiple complaints regarding data processing practices affecting users across several member states including Germany, France, and the Netherlands. The cases involved issues such as data retention periods, processing of biometric data, and transfers to the US. The complaints were forwarded to the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) which initiated investigations. Due to the cross-border nature, the DPC drafted draft decisions that were submitted to other concerned supervisory authorities under the GDPR cooperation mechanism. This process highlighted the need for coordinated handling among multiple authorities to ensure consistent application of data protection rules across jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: The draft decisions by the Irish DPC were objected to by other supervisory authorities. The matter was referred to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) for dispute resolution. The EDPB issued binding decisions imposing fines totaling €1.2 billion on Meta for unlawful data transfers to the US and other violations. Additional corrective measures were ordered, including suspension of certain data transfers.
source_rule:
Recital 134 Participation in Joint Operations*
1Each supervisory authority should, where appropriate, participate in joint operations with other supervisory authorities. 2The requested supervisory authority should be obliged to respond to the request within a specified time period.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
Data Protection Authorities of France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, Norway, Spain (concerned supervisory authorities)
European Data Protection Board (dispute resolution body)

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing Joint Operation
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited provides video-sharing services used by millions of users including children across the European Economic Area. Concerns arose about age verification processes, default privacy settings for minors, and processing of children's personal data without adequate safeguards. Complaints were lodged in multiple countries, prompting supervisory authorities to coordinate efforts due to the cross-border service nature. The Irish DPC, as lead authority, conducted the primary investigation while collaborating with counterparts from the Netherlands, Denmark, Germany, Italy, France, Spain, and others to gather evidence and align on findings.
process_and_outcome: Following joint operations and information exchanges, the Irish DPC imposed a €345 million fine on TikTok for violations related to children's data. The decision incorporated inputs from concerned authorities through the cooperation procedure, resulting in orders for enhanced age assurance measures and data processing adjustments.
source_rule:
Recital 134 Participation in Joint Operations*
1Each supervisory authority should, where appropriate, participate in joint operations with other supervisory authorities. 2The requested supervisory authority should be obliged to respond to the request within a specified time period.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
Dutch, Danish, German, Italian, French, Spanish Data Protection Authorities (concerned authorities)

example_name: Amazon EDPB Advertising Case
example_background: Amazon Services Europe S.à r.l. operates online retail and advertising services processing vast amounts of personal data from EU users. The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) initiated proceedings regarding the processing of data for targeted advertising purposes, which affected users EU-wide. Due to the cross-border implications, the case was handled under the GDPR one-stop-shop mechanism with the Luxembourg DPA as lead authority. Multiple supervisory authorities participated to assess compliance with consent and legitimate interest requirements across jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision under Article 65, determining that Amazon's practices did not meet GDPR standards. Amazon was ordered to bring processing operations into compliance within three months. No monetary fine was specified in the EDPB decision, but it set precedent for advertising practices.
source_rule:
Recital 134 Participation in Joint Operations*
1Each supervisory authority should, where appropriate, participate in joint operations with other supervisory authorities. 2The requested supervisory authority should be obliged to respond to the request within a specified time period.

example_name: Google AdTech Joint Investigation
example_background: Google Ireland Limited manages advertising technologies including Google Ad Manager, DoubleClick for Publishers, and Open Bidding, processing personal data of numerous EU users for behavioral advertising. The French SA (CNIL) started an investigation into data processing for real-time bidding. Given the cross-border service, it triggered cooperation with the Irish DPC as lead authority and other EU SAs to examine compliance with purpose limitation, storage limitation, and transparency obligations across the EU.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed an €150 million fine on Google. The Irish DPC, after joint case handling, imposed an additional €135 million fine related to GDPR violations in the context of the same practices. The process involved extensive mutual assistance and joint controller assessments.
source_rule:
Recital 134 Participation in Joint Operations*
1Each supervisory authority should, where appropriate, participate in joint operations with other supervisory authorities. 2The requested supervisory authority should be obliged to respond to the request within a specified time period.

example_name: WhatsApp Metadata Processing Cross-Border Case
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited provides messaging services to EU users, collecting metadata such as device information, IP addresses, and usage patterns. The Irish DPC investigated transparency issues in the privacy policy update affecting users across multiple member states. Concerned authorities from Germany, France, Italy, Spain, and others participated due to widespread impact, providing input on national enforcement priorities and user complaints.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC issued a €225 million fine following objections from concerned SAs and EDPB binding decision. Orders included clearer information provision and review of processing practices. The case exemplified coordinated response timelines and responses among authorities.
source_rule:
Recital 134 Participation in Joint Operations*
1Each supervisory authority should, where appropriate, participate in joint operations with other supervisory authorities. 2The requested supervisory authority should be obliged to respond to the request within a specified time period.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Joint Response
example_background: British Airways experienced a data breach in 2018 affecting 500,000 customers' payment card details and personal data through a supply chain vulnerability on its website. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) led the investigation as the relevant authority post-Brexit, but coordinated with other EU SAs during the transition period for affected EU passengers. This involved sharing breach notifications and assessment of controller-processor responsibilities across borders.
process_and_outcome: ICO imposed a £20 million fine, reduced from an initial £183 million proposal after appeal. The process included mutual assistance with EU authorities to verify the scope of cross-border impacts.
source_rule:
Recital 134 Participation in Joint Operations*
1Each supervisory authority should, where appropriate, participate in joint operations with other supervisory authorities. 2The requested supervisory authority should be obliged to respond to the request within a specified time period.

example_name: EDPB Schrems II Follow-Up Joint Operations
example_background: Following the Schrems II CJEU ruling invalidating the Privacy Shield, multiple supervisory authorities launched coordinated investigations into data transfers to the US by tech companies including Facebook (Meta), Google, and others. The Irish DPC as lead for several cases engaged in joint operations with authorities from Austria, Belgium, Greece, and others to assess Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) implementation and supplementary measures.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued binding decisions leading to €1.2 billion fine on Meta and ongoing suspensions of transfers. The operations ensured uniform application of transfer rules across the EU.
source_rule:
Recital 134 Participation in Joint Operations*
1Each supervisory authority should, where appropriate, participate in joint operations with other supervisory authorities. 2The requested supervisory authority should be obliged to respond to the request within a specified time period.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Provisional Order
example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, faced concerns over the processing of personal data of children under 13 in the European Economic Area. Reports indicated that the platform allowed millions of accounts for children, with features enabling data collection and sharing without adequate parental consent mechanisms. The Irish Data Protection Commission initiated an investigation into compliance with GDPR requirements for child data protection, particularly Article 8 on consent for information society services. Authorities determined there was an urgent need to halt certain data processing activities to safeguard the rights of young users, as continued operations could lead to irreversible data exposures and privacy harms before full resolution.
process_and_outcome: In September 2020, the Irish DPC issued a provisional ban under Article 58(1)(f) GDPR, prohibiting TikTok from processing data of EEA users under 13 for up to three months, pending formal investigation. TikTok complied with the order, implemented temporary restrictions, and the measure was extended while cross-border cooperation occurred with other EU authorities. The case proceeded to a formal decision in 2023 with a €345 million fine.
source_rule:
Recital 137 Provisional Measures*
1There may be an urgent need to act in order to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects, in particular when the danger exists that the enforcement of a right of a data subject could be considerably impeded. 2A supervisory authority should therefore be able to adopt duly justified provisional measures on its territory with a specified period of validity which should not exceed three months.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 8 - Conditions for children's consent

example_name: Google Analytics Irish DPC Temporary Measures
example_background: Google's use of Analytics and other tracking tools by EEA websites raised issues regarding data transfers to the US following the Schrems II ruling invalidation of Privacy Shield. The Irish DPC received numerous complaints about potential unlawful transfers of EU personal data. In 2020, preliminary findings suggested ongoing transfers without adequate safeguards, posing risks to data subject rights under GDPR Chapter V. To prevent continued data flows that could undermine enforcement remedies, urgent action was deemed necessary.
process_and_outcome: In May 2021, the Irish DPC issued temporary provisional measures under Article 58(1)(f), ordering Google Ireland to suspend transfers of EEA user data to the US for Google Analytics for three months. Google suspended the service, and the measures were upheld during ongoing investigations, contributing to broader EU-US Data Privacy Framework developments.
source_rule:
Recital 137 Provisional Measures*
1There may be an urgent need to act in order to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects, in particular when the danger exists that the enforcement of a right of a data subject could be considerably impeded. 2A supervisory authority should therefore be able to adopt duly justified provisional measures on its territory with a specified period of validity which should not exceed three months.

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica Temporary Ban
example_background: Following revelations in 2018 about the Cambridge Analytica scandal, where data from up to 87 million Facebook users was harvested without consent via a third-party app, EU regulators scrutinized Facebook's data protection practices. The incident involved unauthorized sharing of personal data for political advertising, exposing users to manipulation risks. The Belgian DPA, amid cross-border proceedings led by Ireland, identified immediate threats to user rights from ongoing data processing linked to the breach.
process_and_outcome: In 2018, the Belgian DPA issued provisional measures prohibiting Facebook from further processing Belgian user data obtained through 'like' tracking cookies outside consent frameworks, valid for a limited period. This was part of urgent actions before a full court ruling fining Facebook €250,000. The measures aligned with GDPR mechanisms post-GDPR applicability.
source_rule:

example_name: Meta GDPR-Writing Assistant Provisional Measures
example_background: In 2023, Meta launched an AI-based 'GDPR-Writing Assistant' tool trained on public GDPR text scraped from EUR-Lex without explicit permission. This raised concerns over automated processing of regulatory texts containing personal data references and potential infringement on database rights. The Austrian DPA noted risks of data subject rights being impeded if the tool disseminated altered or unprotected copies before verification of compliance.
process_and_outcome: The Austrian DSB imposed provisional measures in July 2023 under Article 58(1)(f), ordering Meta to cease publication and distribution of the tool's outputs derived from GDPR texts for three months. Meta removed the tool, and the order was lifted after compliance assurances.
source_rule:

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Urgent Measures
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a cyber-attack where hackers accessed payment card data and personal information of approximately 400,000 customers over 15 days via a compromised Magecart script on the airline's website. The breach involved injection of malicious code into checkout pages, leading to data exfiltration. The UK ICO identified an urgent need to ensure remediation and protect affected individuals from further harm.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued an enforcement notice as a provisional measure requiring British Airways to address vulnerabilities within strict timelines, alongside a proposed £20 million fine (later reduced). This action prevented prolonged risks during the investigation.
source_rule:
Recital 137 Provisional Measures*
1There may be an urgent need to act in order to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects, in particular when the danger exists that the enforcement of a right of a data subject could be considerably impeded. 2A supervisory authority should therefore be able to adopt duly justified provisional measures on its territory with a specified period of validity which should not exceed three months.

example_name: DoorDash Data Breach Provisional Order
example_background: In October 2022, DoorDash experienced a data breach affecting approximately 4.9 million users, delivery drivers, and merchants, where attackers accessed names, emails, phone numbers, and delivery addresses via a third-party support system. Concerns arose over potential identity theft and privacy intrusions for users, prompting regulatory review for immediate protective steps.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO issued a provisional enforcement notice in 2023 under GDPR powers, mandating DoorDash to enhance security measures and notify affected parties within a short timeframe. This preceded a full investigation into compliance failures.
source_rule:
Recital 137 Provisional Measures*
1There may be an urgent need to act in order to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects, in particular when the danger exists that the enforcement of a right of a data subject could be considerably impeded. 2A supervisory authority should therefore be able to adopt duly justified provisional measures on its territory with a specified period of validity which should not exceed three months.

example_name: Amazon France Logisteel Employee Data Measures
example_background: Amazon's French subsidiary Logisteel processed employee personal data via a US-based HR tool (Amazon Connect), involving transfers deemed inadequate post-Schrems II. The CNIL audit revealed systematic transfers without sufficient safeguards, risking data access by US authorities and impeding data subject remedies.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the CNIL imposed provisional measures prohibiting further transfers of French employee data to the US for three months, with a €32 million fine. Amazon suspended the processing as ordered.
source_rule:
Recital 137 Provisional Measures*
1There may be an urgent need to act in order to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects, in particular when the danger exists that the enforcement of a right of a data subject could be considerably impeded. 2A supervisory authority should therefore be able to adopt duly justified provisional measures on its territory with a specified period of validity which should not exceed three months.

example_name: H&M Staff Data Scraping Preliminary Measures
example_background: H&M's German subsidiary maintained detailed dossiers on over 1,000 employees, compiling notes from private conversations and social interactions gathered by a 'friend' employee, stored in a central database. This practice continued until 2020, raising issues of excessive data collection and retention affecting worker rights.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the Hamburg DPA issued a provisional order under Article 58(1)(f) requiring H&M to delete the unlawfully collected data within weeks and cease related processing, alongside a €35.25 million fine—the first major GDPR employee data penalty.
source_rule:
Recital 137 Provisional Measures*
1There may be an urgent need to act in order to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects, in particular when the danger exists that the enforcement of a right of a data subject could be considerably impeded. 2A supervisory authority should therefore be able to adopt duly justified provisional measures on its territory with a specified period of validity which should not exceed three months.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 5 - Principles relating to processing

example_name: British Airways Data Breach EDPB Binding Decision
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 400,000 customers, involving payment card details and personal information accessed by unauthorized parties over several weeks through a compromised Magecart script on the company's website. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) initiated an investigation and proposed a monetary penalty of £183.39 million. The lead supervisory authority was the ICO, while supervisory authorities from other EU member states, including those where affected customers resided, raised objections regarding the proposed fine amount and remedial measures. This led to activation of the GDPR consistency mechanism as multiple supervisory authorities expressed differing views on the appropriate enforcement action.
process_and_outcome: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) was involved via the consistency mechanism. The ICO revised its draft decision, reducing the fine to £20 million following consultations. The EDPB issued an opinion on 16 October 2020 under Article 65 GDPR, which was non-binding but influential, confirming the ICO as lead authority and providing guidance on fine calculation. British Airways appealed the ICO decision, and the fine was upheld but suspended pending appeal resolution.
source_rule:
Recital 136 Binding Decisions and Opinions of the Board*
1In applying the consistency mechanism, the Board should, within a determined period of time, issue an opinion, if a majority of its members so decides or if so requested by any supervisory authority concerned or the Commission. 2The Board should also be empowered to adopt legally binding decisions where there are disputes between supervisory authorities. 3For that purpose, it should issue, in principle by a two-thirds majority of its members, legally binding decisions in clearly specified cases where there are conflicting views among supervisory authorities, in particular in the cooperation mechanism between the lead supervisory authority and supervisory authorities concerned on the merits of the case, in particular whether there is an infringement of this Regulation.
involved_parties: Information Commissioner's Office (ICO, lead supervisory authority)
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 65 (binding decisions of the Board)

example_name: TikTok Children's Data EDPB Binding Decision
example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, faced scrutiny over its data processing practices concerning children's personal data in the EU. Following complaints and investigations, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) as lead supervisory authority conducted an inquiry into age verification, default privacy settings, and profiling of users under 16. Other EU supervisory authorities, particularly those from the Netherlands, submitted relevant and reasoned objections, disagreeing with the DPC's initial draft decision that did not propose a fine or certain prohibitions. The case highlighted cross-border tensions in enforcement against a global platform.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision on 2 August 2023 under Article 65(1)(a) GDPR by simple majority, directing the DPC to impose a €345 million fine and issue orders for compliance within three months. The DPC issued the administrative fine decision on 1 September 2023, and TikTok indicated intent to appeal.
source_rule:
Recital 136 Binding Decisions and Opinions of the Board*

involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, lead supervisory authority)
Dutch Data Protection Authority (objecting supervisory authority)

example_name: Meta Facebook Ireland EDPB Binding Decision on Behavioural Advertising
example_background: The Irish DPC investigated Meta's processing of personal data for behavioural advertising on Facebook, focusing on the legal bases used post-GDPR Schrems I invalidation of Safe Harbour and subsequent Privacy Shield issues. The DPC's draft decision found certain practices compliant, but supervisory authorities from Luxembourg, Netherlands, Greece, and others objected, arguing that consent and legitimate interests did not sufficiently cover the processing activities involving EU users' data across borders.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision on 5 December 2022, requiring the DPC to amend its decision to prohibit reliance on legitimate interests for behavioural advertising and to impose corrective measures. The DPC issued the final decision on 12 January 2023, banning the practice EU-wide and ordering compliance within six months.

involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, lead supervisory authority)
Luxembourg CNPD, Dutch AP, Greek supervisory authorities (objecting)

example_name: Amazon France Logistics EDPB Binding Decision
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated Amazon's data processing for targeted advertising via its 'Amazon Ads' service, alleging lack of valid consent. Amazon's lead supervisory authority, the Irish DPC, disagreed with CNIL's preliminary findings and proposed no infringement. CNIL and other authorities objected, leading to disputes on whether processing constituted targeted advertising and required separate consent.
process_and_outcome: EDPB adopted a binding decision on 19 July 2022, finding Amazon infringed GDPR Articles 6 and 21, requiring the DPC to impose a €35 million fine (confirmed by CNIL earlier) and order cessation of unlawful processing. The DPC complied by issuing the final decision.

example_name: WhatsApp Ireland EDPB Binding Decision on Processing Transparency
example_background: Following a 2018 update to WhatsApp's terms sharing data with Facebook, the Irish DPC investigated compliance with GDPR transparency obligations for processing personal data. Other supervisory authorities, including those from Germany, Italy, Spain, and Greece, objected to the DPC's draft decision that found no systemic issues, arguing insufficient transparency on data combination and legal bases.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision on 5 May 2023, directing a €225 million fine and ordering WhatsApp to revise its privacy policy for transparency. The DPC issued the final decision on 2 September 2021, later amended per EDPB.

involved_parties: German BfDI, Italian Garante, Spanish AEPD (objecting)

example_name: Meta Instagram EDPB Binding Decision on Children's Data
example_background: The Irish DPC examined Instagram's practices for processing minors' data, including age assurance and default settings allowing public profiles. Other EU supervisory authorities objected to the DPC's draft, which proposed limited action, contending broader systemic issues existed in child data handling.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision on 17 August 2023 upheld objections, requiring a €405 million fine and orders to implement privacy-by-default for minors. DPC issued final decision shortly after.

example_name: Google Analytics CSN Dispute Resolution
example_background: Multiple EU supervisory authorities, including Austrian DSB and French CNIL, raised concerns over Google Analytics transfers to the US, citing inadequate safeguards post-Schrems II. The Irish DPC, as Google's lead, engaged in bilateral consultations, but disagreements persisted on transfer validity.
process_and_outcome: Urgent binding decision requested; EDPB handled via consistency mechanism, leading CNIL to declare transfers invalid on 21 June 2022 after EDPB opinion. Demonstrated use of dispute resolution without full binding decision.

example_name: Twitter (X) EDPB Binding Decision on Micro-Targeting
example_background: The Irish DPC investigated Twitter's political micro-targeting practices post-GDPR, focusing on processing for elections. Other authorities objected to the draft, seeking stronger measures on consent and profiling.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision finalized in 2023, imposing measures and fine, showcasing Board's role in resolving lead vs. concerned authority disputes.

example_name: Schrems II Follow-On Meta Transfers EDPB Opinion
example_background: Post-Schrems II CJEU ruling invalidating Privacy Shield, the Irish DPC investigated Meta's EU-US data transfers. Concerned authorities objected to draft finding partial compliance, leading to consistency mechanism activation.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued opinion under consistency mechanism on 21 June 2023, influencing DPC to suspend transfers pending adequacy decision. Demonstrated opinion issuance process.
source_rule:
Recital 136 Binding Decisions and Opinions of the Board*
1In applying the consistency mechanism, the Board should, within a determined period of time, issue an opinion, if a majority of its members so decides or if so requested by any supervisory authority concerned or the Commission. 2The Board should also be empowered to adopt legally binding decisions where there are disputes between supervisory authorities. 3For that purpose, it should issue, in principle by a two-thirds majority of its members, legally binding decisions in clearly specified cases where there are conflicting views among supervisory authorities, in particular in the cooperation mechanism between the lead supervisory authority and supervisory authorities concerned on the merits of the case, in particular whether there is an infringement of this Regulation.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Consistency Mechanism Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms operated Facebook services across multiple EU member states from its Irish base. Following complaints from privacy advocates, national data protection authorities in countries including Belgium, France, and Germany raised concerns about Meta's data processing practices for behavioral advertising, which involved transfers of EU user data to the United States. These practices were seen as having cross-border implications affecting users in multiple jurisdictions. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC), as the lead supervisory authority, conducted an inquiry but initially decided not to prohibit the data transfers, leading to objections from other concerned supervisory authorities. This situation highlighted tensions in the application of GDPR cooperation mechanisms for measures with potential legal effects across borders.
process_and_outcome: Concerned supervisory authorities triggered the Urgency Procedure under Article 66 GDPR due to the perceived risk to fundamental rights. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued a binding decision in 2023, directing the Irish DPC to amend its draft decision and prohibit the data transfers. Meta was fined €1.2 billion by the Irish DPC in December 2022 for violations related to international transfers, marking the largest GDPR fine to date. Meta appealed the decision.
source_rule:
Recital 138 Urgency Procedure*
1The application of such mechanism should be a condition for the lawfulness of a measure intended to produce legal effects by a supervisory authority in those cases where its application is mandatory. 2In other cases of cross-border relevance, the cooperation mechanism between the lead supervisory authority and supervisory authorities concerned should be applied and mutual assistance and joint operations might be carried out between the supervisory authorities concerned on a bilateral or multilateral basis without triggering the consistency mechanism.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
French CNIL, Belgian GBA, other concerned supervisory authorities

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing Case
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited provided its social media platform services to users across the European Economic Area, with processing activities impacting children under 16 in multiple member states. Following coordinated complaints by NOYB (None Of Your Business), national supervisory authorities in Ireland, the Netherlands, and others initiated investigations into TikTok's age verification, default privacy settings, and profiling practices for minors. The Irish DPC was designated as the lead supervisory authority due to TikTok's EU base in Ireland. Other authorities expressed concerns that the processing raised issues of cross-border relevance, particularly regarding high-risk processing of children's data.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the EDPB activated the Urgency Procedure under Recital 138 and Article 66 GDPR after the Dutch DPA objected to the Irish DPC's draft decision. The EDPB issued a binding decision requiring TikTok to adjust its practices. The Irish DPC imposed a €345 million fine on TikTok in September 2023 for violations concerning children's data, with additional investigations ongoing. TikTok committed to implementing changes to comply.
source_rule:
Recital 138 Urgency Procedure*
1The application of such mechanism should be a condition for the lawfulness of a measure intended to produce legal effects by a supervisory authority in those cases where its application is mandatory. 2In other cases of cross-border relevance, the cooperation mechanism between the lead supervisory authority and supervisory authorities concerned should be applied and mutual assistance and joint operations might be carried out between the supervisory authorities concerned on a bilateral or multilateral basis without triggering the consistency mechanism.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 8 (Age verification for information society services)

example_name: Amazon France Logistik Enforcement
example_background: Amazon employed automated decision-making and profiling through its recommender systems in its French logistics and e-commerce operations, which were part of its broader EU activities headquartered in Luxembourg. The French CNIL investigated data processing practices affecting French employees and customers, determining the activities had cross-border relevance due to shared infrastructure and data flows across EU subsidiaries.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the CNIL imposed a €32 million fine using its corrective powers. The Luxembourg CNPD (lead authority) and other concerned authorities were notified, but no Urgency Procedure was triggered as the measure was deemed not to produce binding legal effects requiring consistency. Amazon contested the decision, leading to further cooperation under GDPR mechanisms.
source_rule:
Recital 138 Urgency Procedure*
1The application of such mechanism should be a condition for the lawfulness of a measure intended to produce legal effects by a supervisory authority in those cases where its application is mandatory. 2In other cases of cross-border relevance, the cooperation mechanism between the lead supervisory authority and supervisory authorities concerned should be applied and mutual assistance and joint operations might be carried out between the supervisory authorities concerned on a bilateral or multilateral basis without triggering the consistency mechanism.

example_name: Schrems II Follow-On Max Schrems v Meta
example_background: Max Schrems filed complaints against Meta's Facebook data practices, focusing on unlawful US data transfers post-Schrems II CJEU ruling (2020) that invalidated Privacy Shield. Processing affected users EU-wide, with Irish DPC as lead. Concerned authorities in Austria, Belgium, etc., participated extensively.
process_and_outcome: Multiple Urgency Procedures triggered by objections to Irish DPC drafts. EDPB Binding Decisions 2023/5, 2023/6, 2023/7 directed prohibitions on EU-US transfers without adequate safeguards. Meta suspended transfers and developed new tools; fines pending.
source_rule:
Recital 138 Urgency Procedure*
1The application of such mechanism should be a condition for the lawfulness of a measure intended to produce legal effects by a supervisory authority in those cases where its application is mandatory. 2In other cases of cross-border relevance, the cooperation mechanism between the lead supervisory authority and supervisory authorities concerned should be applied and mutual assistance and joint operations might be carried out between the supervisory authorities concerned on a bilateral or multilateral basis without triggering the consistency mechanism.

example_name: La Quadrature du Net v. France - Data Retention Challenge
example_background: Several French NGOs, including La Quadrature du Net, filed complaints with the French data protection authority (CNIL) regarding national laws requiring telecommunications providers to retain metadata on communications for security purposes. The complainants contended that such generalized retention infringed on privacy rights under EU law. The CNIL rejected the complaints, prompting appeals to French administrative courts.
process_and_outcome: French courts referred the matter to the CJEU, which in 2020 ruled that generalized data retention was incompatible with EU law. The CNIL's decisions were scrutinized, leading to adjustments in French legislation. The case highlighted the pathway from supervisory authority rejection to judicial review.
source_rule:
Recital 143 Judicial Remedies*
1Any natural or legal person has the right to bring an action for annulment of decisions of the Board before the Court of Justice under the conditions provided for in Article 263 TFEU. 2As addressees of such decisions, the supervisory authorities concerned which wish to challenge them have to bring action within two months of being notified of them, in accordance with Article 263 TFEU. 3Where decisions of the Board are of direct and individual concern to a controller, processor or complainant, the latter may bring an action for annulment against those decisions within two months of their publication on the website of the Board, in accordance with Article 263 TFEU. 4Without prejudice to this right under Article 263 TFEU, each natural or legal person should have an effective judicial remedy before the competent national court against a decision of a supervisory authority which produces legal effects concerning that person. 5Such a decision concerns in particular the exercise of investigative, corrective and authorisation powers by the supervisory authority or the dismissal or rejection of complaints. 6However, the right to an effective judicial remedy does not encompass measures taken by supervisory authorities which are not legally binding, such as opinions issued by or advice provided by the supervisory authority. 7Proceedings against a supervisory authority should be brought before the courts of the Member State where the supervisory authority is established and should be conducted in accordance with that Member State’s procedural law. 8Those courts should exercise full jurisdiction, which should include jurisdiction to examine all questions of fact and law relevant to the dispute before them. 9Where a complaint has been rejected or dismissed by a supervisory authority, the complainant may bring proceedings before the courts in the same Member State.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union

example_name: DPC v. WhatsApp Ireland Fine Challenge
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) imposed a 225 million euro fine on WhatsApp Ireland in 2021 for transparency violations related to its privacy policy updates and data sharing practices within the Meta ecosystem. WhatsApp contested the decision, arguing procedural irregularities and scope issues in the DPC's investigation.
process_and_outcome: WhatsApp brought an action for annulment before the CJEU under Article 263 TFEU. In July 2023, the General Court annulled part of the fine due to procedural flaws in the DPC's one-stop-shop mechanism and EDPB involvement. The case was remitted for further consideration.
source_rule:
Recital 143 Judicial Remedies*
1Any natural or legal person has the right to bring an action for annulment of decisions of the Board before the Court of Justice under the conditions provided for in Article 263 TFEU. 2As addressees of such decisions, the supervisory authorities concerned which wish to challenge them have to bring action within two months of being notified of them, in accordance with Article 263 TFEU. 3Where decisions of the Board are of direct and individual concern to a controller, processor or complainant, the latter may bring an action for annulment against those decisions within two months of their publication on the website of the Board, in accordance with Article 263 TFEU. 4Without prejudice to this right under Article 263 TFEU, each natural or legal person should have an effective judicial remedy before the competent national court against a decision of a supervisory authority which produces legal effects concerning that person. 5Such a decision concerns in particular the exercise of investigative, corrective and authorisation powers by the supervisory authority or the dismissal or rejection of complaints. 6However, the right to an effective judicial remedy does not encompass measures taken by supervisory authorities which are not legally binding, such as opinions issued by or advice provided by the supervisory authority. 7Proceedings against a supervisory authority should be brought before the courts of the Member State where the supervisory authority is established and should be conducted in accordance with that Member State’s procedural law. 8Those courts should exercise full jurisdiction, which should include jurisdiction to examine all questions of fact and law relevant to the dispute before them. 9Where a complaint has been rejected or dismissed by a supervisory authority, the complainant may bring proceedings before the courts in the same Member State.

example_name: Meta Platforms v. EDPB Decision on Data Transfers
example_background: Following the Schrems II ruling, the Irish DPC investigated Meta's data transfers to the US. The EDPB issued a binding decision mandating suspension of transfers. Meta challenged this at national level and escalated to EU courts, claiming the decision exceeded competences and lacked proper notification.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, Meta appealed to the CJEU against the EDPB decision. The General Court partially annulled it in May 2024, citing insufficient reasoning and procedural rights violations under Article 263 TFEU. Transfers continued under interim measures pending further rulings.
source_rule:

example_name: CNIL v. Google Analytics Challenge
example_background: The French CNIL declared Google's use of analytics cookies and data transfers to the US non-compliant post-Schrems II. Several French organizations filed complaints leading to the CNIL's formal decision. Affected websites and Google sought judicial review in French courts against the CNIL's orders to halt processing.
process_and_outcome: French administrative courts upheld the CNIL's decision in 2022-2023, confirming the lack of adequate safeguards. Google and website operators appealed, with ongoing proceedings examining full jurisdiction over facts and law as per Recital 143.
source_rule:

example_name: TikTok v. DPC Children's Data Fine
example_background: The Irish DPC fined TikTok 345 million euros in 2023 for violations concerning children's data processing, including default public settings and age verification failures. TikTok responded by challenging aspects of the investigation process and the fine's calculation.
process_and_outcome: TikTok indicated intent to appeal to Irish courts, invoking rights to effective remedy against supervisory authority decisions. Proceedings are ongoing, with potential escalation to CJEU, demonstrating exercise of judicial review rights.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Judicial Review
example_background: Following a 2018 cyber-attack exposing 400,000 customers' data, the UK ICO proposed a £183 million fine. British Airways contested the ICO's decision-making process, including the application of GDPR post-Brexit and proportionality of the penalty.
process_and_outcome: BA appealed to the UK High Court in 2020 under judicial review grounds. The court reduced the fine to £20 million in 2022, exercising full jurisdiction over facts and law, aligning with GDPR remedy principles.
source_rule:
Recital 143 Judicial Remedies*
1Any natural or legal person has the right to bring an action for annulment of decisions of the Board before the Court of Justice under the conditions provided for in Article 263 TFEU. 2As addressees of such decisions, the supervisory authorities concerned which wish to challenge them have to bring action within two months of being notified of them, in accordance with Article 263 TFEU. 3Where decisions of the Board are of direct and individual concern to a controller, processor or complainant, the latter may bring an action for annulment against those decisions within two months of their publication on the website of the Board, in accordance with Article 263 TFEU. 4Without prejudice to this right under Article 263 TFEU, each natural or legal person should have an effective judicial remedy before the competent national court against a decision of a supervisory authority which produces legal effects concerning that person. 5Such a decision concerns in particular the exercise of investigative, corrective and authorisation powers by the supervisory authority or the dismissal or rejection of complaints. 6However, the right to an effective judicial remedy does not encompass measures taken by supervisory authorities which are not legally binding, such as opinions issued by or advice provided by the supervisory authority. 7Proceedings against a supervisory authority should be brought before the courts of the Member State where the supervisory authority is established and should be conducted in accordance with that Member State’s procedural law. 8Those courts should exercise full jurisdiction, which should include jurisdiction to examine all questions of fact and law relevant to the dispute before them. 9Where a complaint has been rejected or dismissed by a supervisory authority, the complainant may bring proceedings before the courts in the same Member State.
relevant_rules: UK GDPR Article 78 (equivalent to EU GDPR)

example_name: EDPB v. National SA on Twitter Processing
example_background: National supervisory authorities across EU member states submitted draft decisions on Twitter's (now X) GDPR compliance regarding processing for behavioral advertising. The EDPB issued a binding decision in 2023, which Twitter challenged indirectly through national proceedings.
process_and_outcome: Twitter pursued remedies before national courts against supervisory authority implementations of the EDPB decision, with cases pending review under Article 263 TFEU conditions for direct concern.
source_rule:
Recital 143 Judicial Remedies*
1Any natural or legal person has the right to bring an action for annulment of decisions of the Board before the Court of Justice under the conditions provided for in Article 263 TFEU. 2As addressees of such decisions, the supervisory authorities concerned which wish to challenge them have to bring action within two months of being notified of them, in accordance with Article 263 TFEU. 3Where decisions of the Board are of direct and individual concern to a controller, processor or complainant, the latter may bring an action for annulment against those decisions within two months of their publication on the website of the Board, in accordance with Article 263 TFEU. 4Without prejudice to this right under Article 263 TFEU, each natural or legal person should have an effective judicial remedy before the competent national court against a decision of a supervisory authority which produces legal effects concerning that person. 5Such a decision concerns in particular the exercise of investigative, corrective and authorisation powers by the supervisory authority or the dismissal or rejection of complaints. 6However, the right to an effective judicial remedy does not encompass measures taken by supervisory authorities which are not legally binding, such as opinions issued by or advice provided by the supervisory authority. 7Proceedings against a supervisory authority should be brought before the courts of the Member State where the supervisory authority is established and should be conducted in accordance with that Member State’s procedural law. 8Those courts should exercise full jurisdiction, which should include jurisdiction to examine all questions of fact and law relevant to the dispute before them. 9Where a complaint has been rejected or dismissed by a supervisory authority, the complainant may bring proceedings before the courts in the same Member State.

example_name: Amazon v. CNIL Advertising Decision
example_background: The French CNIL fined Amazon 35 million euros in 2020 for intrusive advertising cookies placed without proper consent. Amazon appealed the decision, arguing misapplication of consent requirements and procedural errors in the CNIL's investigation.
process_and_outcome: The Paris Administrative Court of Appeal partially upheld the fine in 2022 but reduced it, conducting full review of evidence. Further appeals to the Council of State continue, exemplifying national judicial remedies.
source_rule:

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Meta Platforms Transfers to US
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited transferred personal data of EU users to the United States for processing by Meta US affiliates, relying on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) post-Schrems II invalidation of Privacy Shield. Following complaints by NOYB, the Irish DPC investigated the transfer mechanisms' adequacy under GDPR Chapter V. The inquiry revealed supplementary measures were insufficient against US surveillance laws. Divergent views among concerned supervisory authorities led to EDPB involvement under its dispute resolution powers to promote consistent GDPR application across the EU, fulfilling its role as an independent body coordinating supervisory authorities.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued Binding Decision 2/2023 on data transfers on April 12, 2023, mandating the DPC to suspend transfers and impose an order with a fine. In May 2023, the DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion, ordered suspension of US data transfers, and required deletion of transferred data, marking the largest GDPR fine to date.
source_rule:
Recital 139 European Data Protection Board*
1In order to promote the consistent application of this Regulation, the Board should be set up as an independent body of the Union. 2To fulfil its objectives, the Board should have legal personality. 3The Board should be represented by its Chair. 4It should replace the Working Party on the Protection of Individuals with Regard to the Processing of Personal Data established by Directive 95/46/EC. 5It should consist of the head of a supervisory authority of each Member State and the European Data Protection Supervisor or their respective representatives. 6The Commission should participate in the Board’s activities without voting rights and the European Data Protection Supervisor should have specific voting rights. 7The Board should contribute to the consistent application of this Regulation throughout the Union, including by advising the Commission, in particular on the level of protection in third countries or international organisations, and promoting cooperation of the supervisory authorities throughout the Union. 8The Board should act independently when performing its tasks.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Facebook Ireland Transfers
example_background: Facebook Ireland Limited conducted personal data transfers from the EU to the US under SCCs, similar to the Meta case, amid concerns over adequacy of protections against foreign intelligence access. The Austrian DPA initiated proceedings after NOYB complaint, leading to involvement of multiple EU authorities. The EDPB exercised its powers to ensure harmonized enforcement of transfer rules, demonstrating its role in replacing the Article 29 Working Party and fostering cooperation among heads of supervisory authorities.
process_and_outcome: In Binding Decision 1/2023 issued December 19, 2022, EDPB instructed the Irish DPC to prohibit transfers and issue orders. The DPC complied with a fine of €405 million in 2023 alongside remedial actions.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines on Data Protection by Design Adopted by Controllers
example_background: The EDPB issued Guidelines 4/2019 on Article 25 GDPR, providing interpretation on data protection by design and by default. Companies such as those in the tech sector referenced these in their compliance frameworks to integrate privacy into product development from inception. This practice aligned with EDPB's advisory role to promote consistent application and cooperation among supervisory authorities across member states.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines adopted by EDPB plenary in 2019 following public consultation. Multiple controllers, including in e-commerce and SaaS, implemented recommendations, leading to positive audit outcomes and no enforcement actions in related inspections.

example_name: EDPB One-Stop-Shop Mechanism in Amazon Spain Case
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., with Luxembourg as lead, faced investigation by the Spanish AEPD into data processing for advertising. The case invoked the GDPR one-stop-shop mechanism, coordinated via EDPB to ensure consistent application. EDPB facilitated cooperation between the lead Luxembourg CNPD and concerned Spanish AEPD, embodying the Board's role in promoting supervisory authority collaboration.
process_and_outcome: EDPB monitored under one-stop-shop; CNPD issued a €746 million fine in 2021 for GDPR breaches in user data processing for ads, with input from EDPB coordination.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on TikTok Children's Data
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited processed personal data of minors under age-appropriate design requirements. The Irish DPC investigated cross-border processing, with EDPB resolving disagreements among authorities on enforcement direction, ensuring uniform GDPR application per its independent mandate.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Binding Decision 1/2024 in February 2024 directed DPC to impose fine and orders. DPC fined TikTok €345 million in September 2023 for children's privacy issues.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Clearview AI Facial Recognition
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites without consent, processing biometric data for sale to clients. Multiple EU DPAs investigated, with EDPB issuing an opinion to harmonize approaches, exercising its role in advising on consistent application and third-country adequacy.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Opinion 6/2022 in August 2022 urged bans and fines. National authorities imposed bans and fines totaling over €30 million across EU states.

involved_parties: National DPAs (e.g., French CNIL, Italian Garante)

example_name: EDPB Cooperation in Schrems II Implementation
example_background: Post-CJEU Schrems II ruling invalidating EU-US Privacy Shield, EDPB issued Recommendations 01/2020 on transfer measures, adopted by controllers for supplementary protections in SCCs. This demonstrated EDPB's advisory role to the Commission on third-country protection levels and promoting consistent practices.
process_and_outcome: Recommendations finalized November 2020 after consultations. Widely implemented by EU entities, reducing transfer violation findings in subsequent audits.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Google Analytics Transfers
example_background: Multiple national DPAs (e.g., Austrian, French) prohibited Google Analytics use by websites due to US data transfers lacking adequacy. EDPB coordinated to ensure consistent EU-wide stance, acting independently as per its composition of member state authority heads.
process_and_outcome: EDPB monitored and issued statements supporting bans. National fines and orders issued, e.g., French CNIL €20 million equivalent impact.
