example_name: SAP SuccessFactors GDPR Employment Compliance
example_background: SAP's SuccessFactors cloud HR platform is used by numerous employers for processing employee data in recruitment, performance reviews, learning management, and payroll. SAP implemented GDPR-compliant features including data minimization by design, EU-based hosting options, DPIAs for modules handling health or biometric data, and robust data processing agreements. Customers are guided on using legitimate interests assessments for employment purposes, avoiding consent, with audit logs and deletion tools provided for termination scenarios.
process_and_outcome: SAP underwent multiple GDPR audits and certifications (ISO 27701), with no significant fines related to employment processing. The platform supported thousands of companies in achieving compliance, as evidenced by case studies and regulatory approvals.
source_rule:
Recital 155 Processing in the Employment Context*
Member State law or collective agreements, including ‘works agreements’, may provide for specific rules on the processing of employees’ personal data in the employment context, in particular for the conditions under which personal data in the employment context may be processed on the basis of the consent of the employee, the purposes of the recruitment, the performance of the contract of employment, including discharge of obligations laid down by law or by collective agreements, management, planning and organisation of work, equality and diversity in the workplace, health and safety at work, and for the purposes of the exercise and enjoyment, on an individual or collective basis, of rights and benefits related to employment, and for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship.

example_name: British Airways HR Data Transfer Case
example_background: British Airways processed employee personal data for HR functions including shift rostering, training records, and health monitoring for safety-critical roles, transferring data to non-EU service providers for payroll and benefits administration. Initially reliant on Privacy Shield, post-Schrems II, transfers continued without immediate supplementary measures, affecting flight crew and ground staff data.
process_and_outcome: The ICO investigated as part of broader BA data incidents. In 2021, while primarily fined £20m for customer breach, HR practices were scrutinized, leading to remediation orders. BA adopted EU Standard Contractual Clauses with addendums.
source_rule:
Recital 155 Processing in the Employment Context*
Member State law or collective agreements, including ‘works agreements’, may provide for specific rules on the processing of employees’ personal data in the employment context, in particular for the conditions under which personal data in the employment context may be processed on the basis of the consent of the employee, the purposes of the recruitment, the performance of the contract of employment, including discharge of obligations laid down by law or by collective agreements, management, planning and organisation of work, equality and diversity in the workplace, health and safety at work, and for the purposes of the exercise and enjoyment, on an individual or collective basis, of rights and benefits related to employment, and for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship.

example_name: CNIL Enforcement against DeepMind
example_background: Alphabet's DeepMind unit conducted a research project called Streams, which involved processing over 1.6 million patient records from 216 hospitals in England. The project aimed to develop a machine learning system to predict acute kidney injury. Patient data was shared with researchers without explicit consent from individual patients. The data included identifiable information such as names, dates of birth, postcodes, and NHS numbers. The UK Health Research Authority approved the project under its standard approval process, but the French data protection authority CNIL examined the case after receiving complaints. CNIL determined that the processing did not meet the requirements for scientific research exemptions under GDPR, as individual consent was not obtained and pseudonymisation was insufficient to qualify as anonymous data. The research relied on a broad dataset from the National Health Service for applied health research purposes.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued a formal decision on October 5, 2023, finding violations of GDPR articles on lawfulness of processing, purpose limitation, data minimisation, and legal basis. No fine was imposed due to corrective actions already taken by the parties involved. DeepMind and the Royal Free London NHS Trust had ceased the data sharing and implemented improvements prior to the decision.
source_rule:
Recital 159 Processing for Scientific Research Purposes*
1Where personal data are processed for scientific research purposes, this Regulation should also apply to that processing. 2For the purposes of this Regulation, the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes should be interpreted in a broad manner including for example technological development and demonstration, fundamental research, applied research and privately funded research. 3In addition, it should take into account the Union’s objective under Article 179(1) TFEU of achieving a European Research Area. 4Scientific research purposes should also include studies conducted in the public interest in the area of public health. 5To meet the specificities of processing personal data for scientific research purposes, specific conditions should apply in particular as regards the publication or otherwise disclosure of personal data in the context of scientific research purposes. 6If the result of scientific research in particular in the health context gives reason for further measures in the interest of the data subject, the general rules of this Regulation should apply in view of those measures.
involved_parties: Royal Free London NHS Foundation Trust
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 9(2)(j) - Processing for scientific research

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into Facebook Research Data
example_background: Facebook (Meta) conducted a research study in 2014 involving nearly 700,000 users whose news feeds were manipulated to study emotional contagion. News feeds were altered to show more positive or negative content without user notification or consent. This processing occurred for scientific research purposes in the field of social psychology. The data processed included user interactions, likes, comments, and emotional indicators derived from content. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated following complaints and media coverage, focusing on whether the processing complied with data protection rules applicable to scientific research, including transparency and lawful basis requirements. The study was published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.
process_and_outcome: The DPC concluded in 2018 that the processing violated GDPR predecessor rules and equivalent principles. Meta was ordered to review internal practices for research processing. No monetary penalty was issued due to the timing before full GDPR enforcement, but it led to enhanced compliance frameworks for research activities.
source_rule:

relevant_rules: GDPR Article 89 - Safeguards for scientific research

example_name: New Scientist GDPR Research Consent Case
example_background: New Scientist magazine conducted a survey for a research article on public attitudes toward GDPR, processing personal data from respondents including emails and opinions on data protection. The processing was framed as journalistic research but involved storing identifiable responses for analysis and potential follow-up. Subscribers complained to the UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) about lack of clear consent and purpose specification for the research use. The case highlighted tensions between journalistic exemptions and scientific research processing under GDPR, where data was collected via online forms without explicit mention of research archiving or secondary analysis.
process_and_outcome: ICO issued a decision notice in 2020 requiring New Scientist to delete the personal data and implement consent mechanisms. The controller appealed but ultimately complied, updating privacy notices for future surveys.

example_name: Helsinki Declaration GDPR Compliance in Finnish Biobank
example_background: The Finnish Biobank from THL (Finnish Institute for Health and Welfare) processes personal health data for epidemiological and genetic research studies. Participants provide broad consent for future scientific research purposes, covering fundamental and applied research in public health. Data is pseudonymised, stored securely, and access is granted via ethical review processes. The biobank interprets scientific research broadly, including privately funded projects aligned with European Research Area goals. Regular audits ensure compliance with GDPR conditions for research publication and disclosure.
process_and_outcome: THL received approval from the National Supervisory Authority for Welfare and Health (Valvira) and has operated without enforcement actions since GDPR implementation. It serves as a model for compliant research processing, with over 500,000 samples processed annually.

example_name: UK Biobank GDPR Scientific Research Model
example_background: UK Biobank is a large-scale biomedical database containing genetic, lifestyle, and health data from 500,000 UK participants. Data is processed for scientific research purposes, including technological development and public health studies. Participants gave informed consent for broad research use, with data pseudonymised and access controlled through an application process requiring ethical approval. The resource supports studies in areas like genomics and epidemiology, aligning with EU objectives for a European Research Area. Specific safeguards address re-identification risks in research outputs.
process_and_outcome: UK Biobank has maintained full compliance through GDPR audits by the ICO and ethics committees. It has enabled over 13,000 research projects without data protection enforcement.

example_name: CNIL Fine Against FIJ (Journalistic Research Data)
example_background: The French Union of Independent Journalists (FIJ) processed personal data of over 1,000 individuals for a journalistic investigation into sexual harassment cases. Data included names, contact details, and testimonies collected for research and publication purposes. The processing was defended as scientific research in social sciences but lacked documented consent and lawful basis. CNIL reviewed whether journalistic exemptions applied or if GDPR research rules required additional safeguards like data minimisation for disclosure.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CNIL imposed a €20,000 fine for violations including lack of legal basis and transparency. FIJ was ordered to delete non-essential data.

1Where personal data are processed for scientific research purposes, this Regulation should also apply to that processing. 2For the purposes of this Regulation, the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes should be interpreted in a broad manner including for example technological development and demonstration, fundamental research, applied research and privately funded research. 3In addition, it should take into account the Union’s objective under Article 179(1) TFEU of achieving a European Research Area. 4Scientific research purposes should also include studies conducted in the public interest in the area of public health. 5To meet the specificities of processing personal data for scientific research purposes, specific conditions should apply in particular as regards the publication or otherwise disclosure of personal data in the context of scientific research purposes. 6If the result of scientific research in particular in the health context gives reason for further measures in the interest of the data subject, the general rules of this Regulation should apply in view of those measures.

example_name: 23andMe Data Processing for Research
example_background: 23andMe collects genetic and health data from customers for direct-to-consumer testing and shares de-identified data with pharmaceutical partners for drug development research. Users opt-in to research participation, with data pseudonymised for studies in areas like Parkinson's disease and cancer. The company applies GDPR-compliant consent for EU users, interpreting scientific research to include privately funded applied research. Processing includes genotype data, phenotypes, and self-reported health information.
process_and_outcome: 23andMe passed GDPR adequacy reviews and has partnerships with GSK and Regeneron without enforcement. It withdrew from China market to ensure compliance.
source_rule:
Recital 159 Processing for Scientific Research Purposes*
1Where personal data are processed for scientific research purposes, this Regulation should also apply to that processing. 2For the purposes of this Regulation, the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes should be interpreted in a broad manner including for example technological development and demonstration, fundamental research, applied research and privately funded research. 3In addition, it should take into account the Union’s objective under Article 179(1) TFEU of achieving a European Research Area. 4Scientific research purposes should also include studies conducted in the public interest in the area of public health. 5To meet the specificities of processing personal data for scientific research purposes, specific conditions should apply in particular as regards the publication or otherwise disclosure of personal data in the context of scientific research purposes. 6If the result of scientific research in particular in the health context gives reason for further measures in the interest of the data subject, the general rules of this Regulation should apply in view of those measures.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Case Against University Research Project
example_background: A Spanish university processed student personal data, including health and academic records, for a sociological study on mental health impacts of remote learning during COVID-19. Data was collected via surveys without explicit consent for research reuse, and results were published with aggregated but potentially re-identifiable statistics. The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) investigated complaints from participants regarding the broad interpretation of scientific research purposes without Article 89 safeguards.
process_and_outcome: AEPD issued a 2021 sanction of €3,000 and required implementation of pseudonymisation protocols. The university revised its research ethics policies.
source_rule:
Recital 159 Processing for Scientific Research Purposes*

involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: Estonian Health Information Exchange Research Compliance
example_background: Estonia's national health information system processes patient data for public health research through the Estonian Genome Project and CORAL cohort studies. Data is handled under broad consent models for scientific research, including genetic and epidemiological analyses. Pseudonymisation and federated access ensure compliance, supporting EU public interest research in health.
process_and_outcome: Certified compliant by Estonian Data Protection Inspectorate in annual audits since 2018, enabling cross-border research collaborations.

example_name: Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 Vaccine Trial Consent Issues
example_background: During the Phase 3 clinical trial for the BNT162b2 COVID-19 vaccine, Pfizer and BioNTech conducted a large-scale study involving over 44,000 participants across multiple countries including sites in the EU. The informed consent process was managed through an electronic platform called vCoVIN, where participants reviewed and signed digital consent forms detailing trial procedures, risks such as potential side effects like myocarditis, benefits, data handling under GDPR, and voluntary withdrawal rights. Some reports emerged from trial sites indicating that certain participants felt pressured by healthcare providers or misunderstood compensation as incentivizing enrollment, leading to questions about the voluntariness of consent. EU data protection authorities reviewed whether personal data processing in the trial aligned with GDPR requirements for scientific research, particularly recital 161 referencing Clinical Trials Regulation consent standards.
process_and_outcome: EMA authorized the vaccine in December 2020 after reviewing trial data. Data protection authorities in Germany and other EU states conducted audits but found no systemic consent failures warranting fines. Pfizer implemented enhanced consent training for sites post-trial.
source_rule:
Recital 161 Consenting to the Participation in Clinical Trials*
For the purpose of consenting to the participation in scientific research activities in clinical trials, the relevant provisions of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council¹ should apply.
involved_parties: National data protection authorities (e.g., BfDI Germany)

example_name: AstraZeneca COVID-19 Vaccine Trial Participant Complaints
example_background: AstraZeneca's Phase 3 trial for the AZD1222 COVID-19 vaccine enrolled over 30,000 participants globally, including EU sites. Informed consent forms outlined risks like thrombosis, trial duration, and data sharing for regulatory approval. In 2021, media reports highlighted cases in the UK and EU where participants claimed inadequate disclosure of risks or felt coerced due to pandemic pressures, prompting scrutiny from ethics committees. GDPR compliance was examined for processing health data in research, with recital 161 specifying that clinical trial consent follows CTR standards including explicit, informed, and documented consent.
process_and_outcome: UK MHRA and EMA approved the vaccine. Italian ethics committee investigated a site for consent irregularities, leading to protocol amendments but no trial halt. No GDPR fines issued.
source_rule:
Recital 161 Consenting to the Participation in Clinical Trials*
For the purpose of consenting to the participation in scientific research activities in clinical trials, the relevant provisions of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council¹ should apply.

example_name: Novartis Gene Therapy Zolgensma Consent Review
example_background: Novartis's Zolgensma (onasemnogene abeparvovec) for spinal muscular atrophy underwent EU clinical trials involving infants. Consent from parents included details on one-time infusion, potential immune responses, high cost implications, and GDPR data processing for rare disease research. Trials used centralized ethics review under CTR, with digital consent tools ensuring comprehension through multilingual forms and audio explanations. Some families reported post-trial that long-term risks were downplayed.
process_and_outcome: EMA granted conditional approval in 2019 after positive trial data review. No formal investigations into consent processes.
source_rule:
Recital 161 Consenting to the Participation in Clinical Trials*
For the purpose of consenting to the participation in scientific research activities in clinical trials, the relevant provisions of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council¹ should apply.

example_name: GSK Trelegy Ellipta Trial Informed Consent Audit
example_background: GlaxoSmithKline (GSK) ran multinational Phase 3 trials for Trelegy Ellipta COPD treatment, enrolling thousands including EU participants. Consent procedures followed CTR with paper and e-consent options, covering adverse events like pneumonia risk, randomization, and data pseudonymization under GDPR. A 2019 audit by German BfArM found documentation gaps at some sites where signatures were obtained without recorded discussions of voluntariness.
process_and_outcome: BfArM required site corrections and retraining. No fines or trial suspension.
source_rule:
Recital 161 Consenting to the Participation in Clinical Trials*
For the purpose of consenting to the participation in scientific research activities in clinical trials, the relevant provisions of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council¹ should apply.

example_name: Roche Perjeta Breast Cancer Trial Consent Success
example_background: Roche's CLEOPATRA trial for Perjeta (pertuzumab) in HER2-positive breast cancer involved over 8,000 patients worldwide, with EU sites using standardized consent forms approved by ethics committees. Forms detailed combination therapy risks, survival benefits, biomarker testing, and GDPR-compliant data sharing across sponsors. The process included participant education sessions and was praised in EMA reviews for transparency.
process_and_outcome: EMA approved Perjeta in 2012 based on trial results. No consent-related issues reported.
source_rule:
Recital 161 Consenting to the Participation in Clinical Trials*
For the purpose of consenting to the participation in scientific research activities in clinical trials, the relevant provisions of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council¹ should apply.

example_name: Bayer Essure Device Clinical Trial Consent Litigation
example_background: Bayer's pre-market trials for Essure permanent birth control device in the EU involved consent forms outlining migration risks, pain, and nickel allergy potential. Post-approval, thousands of women reported inadequate risk disclosure in consents, leading to investigations into trial conduct. GDPR aspects reviewed for health data in adverse event reporting.
process_and_outcome: EMA withdrew Essure marketing authorization in 2018. Bayer paid over €500 million in settlements. French and Dutch authorities probed consent validity.
source_rule:
Recital 161 Consenting to the Participation in Clinical Trials*
For the purpose of consenting to the participation in scientific research activities in clinical trials, the relevant provisions of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council¹ should apply.

example_name: Janssen Ebola Vaccine Trial Consent Model
example_background: Janssen (Johnson & Johnson) conducted Phase 3 trials for Zabdeno/Mvabea Ebola vaccine in Africa and Europe, using ring vaccination with community engagement for consent. EU sites employed layered consent processes: initial community info, individual forms detailing ring design, immunogenicity, and data governance under GDPR and CTR, ensuring cultural appropriateness.
process_and_outcome: EMA recommended approval in 2020. WHO prequalified the vaccine. Consent processes endorsed as exemplary.
source_rule:
Recital 161 Consenting to the Participation in Clinical Trials*
For the purpose of consenting to the participation in scientific research activities in clinical trials, the relevant provisions of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council¹ should apply.

example_name: Merck Keytruda Oncology Trial Consent Standardization
example_background: Merck's KEYNOTE trials for Keytruda (pembrolizumab) in various cancers enrolled tens of thousands, including EU participants. Consent processes were harmonized across sites with e-consent platforms detailing PD-1 inhibitor risks like immune-related adverse events, companion diagnostics, and GDPR data minimization for research purposes.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EMA approvals from 2015 onward. Routine audits confirmed consent compliance.
source_rule:
Recital 161 Consenting to the Participation in Clinical Trials*
For the purpose of consenting to the participation in scientific research activities in clinical trials, the relevant provisions of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council¹ should apply.

example_name: Irish Data Protection Commission vs Meta Platforms Ireland (2023)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited engaged in processing personal data of Facebook users in the European Union for behavioral advertising purposes. The processing involved combining data from various sources to create targeted advertisements. The Irish Data Protection Commission conducted an inquiry following complaints from privacy advocacy groups. The investigation determined that the processing did not meet the requirements for legitimate interest as a legal basis and involved extensive profiling that could lead to individualized decision-making. The data processing occurred over several years, affecting millions of users across the EU, with data being transferred and analyzed in ways that raised concerns about the necessity and proportionality of the operations.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission issued a final decision in 2023, imposing a fine of 1.2 billion euros on Meta Platforms Ireland Limited. The decision required the company to cease unlawful processing within five months and delete certain data. Meta announced plans to appeal the decision.
source_rule:
Recital 162 Processing for Statistical Purposes*
1Where personal data are processed for statistical purposes, this Regulation should apply to that processing. 2Union or Member State law should, within the limits of this Regulation, determine statistical content, control of access, specifications for the processing of personal data for statistical purposes and appropriate measures to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject and for ensuring statistical confidentiality. 3Statistical purposes mean any operation of collection and the processing of personal data necessary for statistical surveys or for the production of statistical results. 4Those statistical results may further be used for different purposes, including a scientific research purpose. 5The statistical purpose implies that the result of processing for statistical purposes is not personal data, but aggregate data, and that this result or the personal data are not used in support of measures or decisions regarding any particular natural person.

example_name: French CNIL vs Google LLC (2020)
example_background: Google LLC processed personal data of users through its advertising services, including Google Ads and DoubleClick, involving the creation of user profiles based on browsing behavior, location data, and interests. The processing was part of a system that aggregated data for advertising purposes across multiple websites and apps. The French data protection authority investigated after receiving complaints about lack of transparency and consent. The operations included real-time bidding in ad auctions where personal data was shared extensively without adequate safeguards, potentially leading to inferences about individuals.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL imposed a fine of 50 million euros in 2019, followed by additional enforcement. Google was ordered to cease processing without valid consent and implement compliance measures. Further investigations led to ongoing monitoring.

involved_parties: French Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: UK ICO vs Clearview AI (2022)
example_background: Clearview AI collected billions of facial images from publicly available websites and processed them into a searchable database using facial recognition technology. The processing involved scraping images without consent, creating biometric data profiles that could be queried for identification purposes. The UK Information Commissioner's Office investigated after complaints and its own inquiry, finding that the database enabled law enforcement and private entities to perform searches that directly identified individuals, rather than producing anonymized aggregate results.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued an enforcement notice in 2022 requiring Clearview AI to cease unlawful processing and delete data of UK residents. A monetary penalty was under consideration. Clearview appealed but compliance was mandated.

example_name: Hamburg DPA vs H&M (2020)
example_background: H&M operated a call center in Nuremberg, Germany, where employees gathered extensive personal information from returning customers, including sensitive details about health, relationships, and finances. This data was stored in a central database and processed to create customer profiles for sales purposes. The processing went beyond necessary customer service and involved retaining data for profiling without explicit consent, potentially used in interactions targeting specific individuals.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Data Protection Authority fined H&M 35.3 million euros in October 2020. The company was required to review and adjust its data processing practices. H&M discontinued the practice and implemented remediation measures.
source_rule:

involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Statistics Netherlands GDPR Compliance Framework
example_background: Statistics Netherlands (CBS) processes large volumes of personal data from national registers, surveys, and administrative sources to produce official statistics on population, economy, and society. The agency established strict protocols for data collection, pseudonymization, and aggregation to ensure outputs are statistical results without identifying individuals. Access is controlled through secure environments, and data is used solely for aggregate reporting, with safeguards like differential privacy techniques applied in some cases to prevent re-identification.
process_and_outcome: CBS maintains compliance through regular audits and has not faced major enforcement actions. It publishes transparency reports and collaborates with the Dutch DPA on best practices, demonstrating sustained adherence over years.

relevant_rules: GDPR Article 89 (safeguards for scientific research and statistics)

example_name: Eurostat Statistical Processing Practices
example_background: Eurostat, the statistical office of the European Union, collects and processes personal data from member states for EU-wide statistics on topics like migration, employment, and health. The processing follows standardized methodologies ensuring data is aggregated into non-personal statistical outputs. Strict access controls, anonymization protocols, and confidentiality measures are enforced, with results used for policy-making without targeting individuals.
process_and_outcome: Eurostat undergoes regular compliance reviews by the European Data Protection Supervisor and has maintained a clean record, serving as a model for statistical agencies across the EU.

example_name: Spanish AEPD vs Various Political Parties (2021)
example_background: Several Spanish political parties processed voter data obtained from public sources and commercial databases for micro-targeting in election campaigns. The processing involved profiling individuals based on political leanings, purchasing habits, and demographics to deliver personalized messages, which went beyond aggregate statistical analysis and directly influenced voter outreach.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) fined multiple parties, including Vox with 10,000 euros and others up to 75,000 euros, for unlawful profiling. Remediation orders were issued to halt such processing.

example_name: UK ICO vs Experian (2020)
example_background: Experian, a credit reference agency, processed personal financial data to create credit scores and risk profiles shared with lenders. An investigation revealed that some processing outputs were used in automated decision-making affecting loan approvals for specific individuals, with insufficient anonymization in certain datasets shared for analytical purposes.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a fine of 250,000 pounds under pre-GDPR law, but post-GDPR scrutiny led to enhanced compliance requirements. Experian updated its practices to better align with aggregation standards.

example_name: Finnish DPA vs Posti (2021)
example_background: Posti, the Finnish postal service, processed customer address data along with delivery information for market research purposes. The data was analyzed to derive customer segments and preferences, but some internal uses involved linking back to individual records for targeted marketing, diverging from pure statistical aggregation.
process_and_outcome: The Finnish Data Protection Ombudsman fined Posti 100,000 euros. The company was directed to revise its data handling procedures and enhance anonymization.

example_name: UK ONS Data Breach 2015
example_background: The Office for National Statistics (ONS) in the United Kingdom experienced an incident where personal data from the 2011 Census was exposed due to a misconfigured server. Approximately 1,900 individuals' names, addresses, and household composition data became accessible online for a short period. The data was part of official national statistics collection used for demographic analysis and policy planning. The breach occurred because the server was inadvertently made publicly accessible without password protection, highlighting issues in data handling practices for statistical authorities.
process_and_outcome: The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated the incident. The ONS was required to notify affected individuals and implement enhanced security measures. No monetary penalty was issued, but the ICO issued a monetary penalty notice warning and mandated improvements in data protection protocols.
source_rule:
Recital 163 Production of European and National Statistics*
1The confidential information which the Union and national statistical authorities collect for the production of official European and official national statistics should be protected. 2European statistics should be developed, produced and disseminated in accordance with the statistical principles as set out in Article 338(2) TFEU, while national statistics should also comply with Member State law. 3Regulation (EC) No 223/2009 of the European Parliament and of the Council¹ provides further specifications on statistical confidentiality for European statistics.

example_name: Eurostat INSEE Microdata Leak 2018
example_background: France's National Institute of Statistics and Economic Studies (INSEE), in collaboration with Eurostat, managed a research data center handling confidential microdata for European statistical production. A configuration error in the secure access system allowed unauthorized researchers to download anonymized individual-level data on income and employment without proper approval. The data was intended solely for statistical research under strict confidentiality rules, affecting thousands of records collected for official European and national statistics.
process_and_outcome: Eurostat and the French data protection authority conducted a joint audit. Access was suspended, affected datasets were reviewed for re-identification risks, and new authentication protocols were enforced. No public fine was levied, but procedural reforms were mandated across EU secure data centers.
source_rule:

example_name: Statistics Netherlands CBS Hack 2021
example_background: Centraal Bureau voor de Statistiek (CBS), the Dutch national statistical office, suffered a ransomware attack where hackers accessed servers containing personal data used in national statistics production. The breach involved data on vaccination records and mobility statistics, collected under EU statistical confidentiality obligations. Attackers exploited vulnerabilities in remote access systems, potentially compromising information on millions of citizens used for official European and national reporting.
process_and_outcome: Dutch authorities and the Data Protection Authority investigated. CBS paid no ransom, restored systems from backups, and notified 4 million affected individuals. Enhanced cybersecurity measures were implemented, with ongoing monitoring required.
source_rule:

example_name: Irish CSO Data Breach 2019
example_background: The Central Statistics Office (CSO) of Ireland had a security incident where an employee's laptop containing sensitive survey data was stolen. The data included personal identifiers linked to labor force and household finance statistics, gathered for both national and European statistical outputs. The laptop was encrypted, but the incident raised concerns about physical security of devices holding confidential statistical information.
process_and_outcome: The Data Protection Commission (DPC) reviewed the case. No data loss was confirmed due to encryption, but CSO updated its mobile device policies and conducted staff training. The incident was reported within GDPR timelines.

example_name: Eurostat Secure Data Access Compliance Program
example_background: Eurostat established a network of European Secure Data Access Points (ESSDAP) to provide controlled access to confidential microdata for research while maintaining statistical confidentiality. Researchers access data in secure environments without downloading, ensuring protection of information collected for official European statistics on topics like economy, population, and environment. This system supports compliance with EU statistical principles across member states.
process_and_outcome: The program has facilitated over 1,000 research projects since inception without reported confidentiality breaches. Regular audits confirm adherence, serving as a model for national statistical offices.

example_name: Statistics Sweden SCB Confidentiality Framework
example_background: Statistics Sweden (SCB) implemented a comprehensive confidentiality regime for handling data from population registers, economic surveys, and social statistics used in both national and European reporting. Protocols include pseudonymization, remote access controls, and legal safeguards preventing disclosure of individual-level data, aligning with EU-wide statistical production standards.
process_and_outcome: SCB has maintained a clean record in data protection audits by the European Data Protection Supervisor. The framework supported flawless delivery of statistics during multiple EU data calls without incidents.

example_name: German Destatis Research Data Center
example_background: The Federal Statistical Office of Germany (Destatis) operates a Research Data Center providing access to confidential microdata for scientific use in producing national and European statistics on employment, income, and migration. Strict on-site and remote access rules ensure no personal data leaves secure environments, protecting information collected under official statistical mandates.
process_and_outcome: Annual compliance reviews by national and EU bodies have validated the center's operations, enabling high-quality statistical outputs without breaches over 15 years.

example_name: Spanish INE Data Protection Audit Success 2022
example_background: The National Statistics Institute (INE) of Spain underwent a comprehensive GDPR compliance audit focusing on its handling of census and economic data for European statistics. The institute demonstrated robust encryption, access logging, and anonymization techniques for confidential datasets used in EU-wide compilations.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish Data Protection Agency issued a positive compliance certification, with no recommendations for remediation needed. INE continued operations seamlessly.

example_name: Polish GUS Cyber Incident Response 2020
example_background: Statistics Poland (GUS) faced a phishing attack targeting systems holding agricultural and labor statistics data for national and EU reporting. The attempt sought to access confidential respondent information collected under statistical confidentiality provisions.
process_and_outcome: GUS's incident response team isolated affected systems, conducted a forensic analysis, and confirmed no data exfiltration. Multi-factor authentication and training were strengthened post-incident.

example_name: Deloitte Ireland GDPR Fine 2021
example_background: Deloitte Ireland, a professional services firm, was investigated after a cyber-attack in 2017 exposed personal data of 147 employees via an unpatched server. The incident came to light during GDPR enforcement, where the Data Protection Commission (DPC) sought access to internal records and systems to assess compliance. Deloitte invoked professional secrecy obligations related to client engagements and audit data, limiting full disclosure. The investigation focused on balancing supervisory access powers with the firm's duties to maintain confidentiality over sensitive professional information.
process_and_outcome: The DPC imposed a €1.38 million fine in 2021 for inadequate technical measures. Deloitte cooperated under restricted access protocols and upgraded its cybersecurity framework. The case set a precedent for handling secrecy in professional services audits.
source_rule:
Recital 164 Professional or Other Equivalent Secrecy Obligations*
1As regards the powers of the supervisory authorities to obtain from the controller or processor access to personal data and access to their premises, Member States may adopt by law, within the limits of this Regulation, specific rules in order to safeguard the professional or other equivalent secrecy obligations, in so far as necessary to reconcile the right to the protection of personal data with an obligation of professional secrecy. 2This is without prejudice to existing Member State obligations to adopt rules on professional secrecy where required by Union law.

example_name: Facebook WhatsApp GDPR Fine 2021
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland (formerly Facebook) was fined €225 million by the Irish DPC for unlawful data transfers from WhatsApp EU users to the US between 2018-2020. The investigation required access to internal transfer mechanisms and compliance records, where the company asserted professional secrecy over proprietary data routing technologies. Regulators balanced these claims with the need for transparency in Schrems II compliance post-CJEU ruling.
process_and_outcome: The DPC's decision followed a draft coordinated with other EU authorities. WhatsApp implemented new Standard Contractual Clauses and supplementary measures. Further fines followed in related proceedings.

example_name: Equifax Data Breach and Investigation 2017
example_background: Equifax, a US credit reporting agency operating in Europe, suffered a breach exposing 147 million individuals' data due to unpatched Apache Struts software. EU supervisory authorities, including the ICO, investigated GDPR applicability for EU residents' data. Equifax limited access to proprietary scoring algorithms and breach response logs, invoking professional secrecy equivalent to financial services obligations. The probe examined controller responsibilities and supervisory powers under GDPR.
process_and_outcome: ICO fined Equifax £500,000 (pre-GDPR maximum). The company paid over $1 billion in US settlements and enhanced EU compliance programs, including data access protocols for regulators.
source_rule:

example_name: CNIL v. Google LLC 2019
example_background: CNIL investigated Google's ad personalization practices, requesting access to processing activities and consent logs. Google provided partial documentation but restricted full server access citing professional secrecy over machine learning models and advertiser data. The case tested boundaries of supervisory powers against tech firms' secrecy obligations in reconciling data protection with innovation secrecy.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €50 million fine, upheld by the French Council of State. Google adjusted cookie consent banners EU-wide, demonstrating adaptive compliance amid ongoing EDPB proceedings.
source_rule:

example_name: Amazon France Logboooks Fine 2020
example_background: CNIL fined Amazon €35 million for intrusive employee monitoring via vendor data and internal logs. The investigation involved on-site access requests to fulfillment centers and data systems, where Amazon cited professional secrecy for operational efficiency metrics and third-party vendor contracts. This balanced logistics secrecy with data subject rights in processing activities.
process_and_outcome: CNIL confirmed the fine after targeted controls. Amazon modified tracking practices and improved transparency reports, influencing supply chain data policies.

example_name: DPC v. Meta Platforms Ireland 2023
example_background: The Irish DPC concluded inquiries into Meta's Facebook processing post-Cambridge Analytica, requiring access to vast internal datasets on behavioral advertising. Meta applied limitations based on professional secrecy for algorithmic trade secrets and user profiling methods. The one-stop-shop mechanism addressed cross-border complexities while respecting Member State secrecy rules.
process_and_outcome: DPC imposed €1.2 billion fine for unlawful transfers, leading to forced suspension of US data flows. Meta appealed and restructured EU-US data mechanisms.

1As regards the powers of the supervisory authorities to obtain from the controller or processor access to personal data and access to their premises, Member States may adopt by law, within the limits of this Regulation, specific rules in order to safeguard the professional or other equivalent secrecy obligations, in so far as necessary to reconcile the right to the protection of personal data with an obligation of professional secrecy. 2This is without prejudice to existing Member State obligations to adopt rules on professional secrecy where required by Union law.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Standard Contractual Clauses Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the lead entity for data processing activities of Facebook users in the European Economic Area, transferred personal data of EU users to the United States for processing by its affiliates. The company relied on the previous version of Standard Contractual Clauses adopted under Directive 95/46/EC. Following the Schrems II judgment by the Court of Justice of the European Union in July 2020, which invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield and required supplementary measures for data transfers under SCCs, Meta continued these transfers without implementing appropriate safeguards to address risks related to US surveillance laws. The Irish Data Protection Commission conducted an investigation into the lawfulness of these international data transfers, focusing on compliance with Chapter V of the GDPR, particularly the requirements for adequate safeguards such as SCCs and supplementary measures.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission issued a decision in 2023 determining that Meta's data transfers violated GDPR Article 46(1). A fine of 1.2 billion euros was imposed on Meta Platforms Ireland Limited. The company was ordered to cease unlawful processing of EU user data in the US within five months and to bring data transfers into compliance by implementing necessary measures.
source_rule:
Recital 168 Implementing Acts on Standard Contractual Clauses*
The examination procedure should be used for the adoption of implementing acts on standard contractual clauses between controllers and processors and between processors; codes of conduct; technical standards and mechanisms for certification; the adequate level of protection afforded by a third country, a territory or a specified sector within that third country, or an international organisation; standard protection clauses; formats and procedures for the exchange of information by electronic means between controllers, processors and supervisory authorities for binding corporate rules; mutual assistance; and arrangements for the exchange of information by electronic means between supervisory authorities, and between supervisory authorities and the Board.

example_name: Schrems II CJEU Judgment on Data Transfers
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, challenged Facebook Ireland's data transfers to the US under the then-applicable EU-US Privacy Shield framework and Standard Contractual Clauses. The case highlighted discrepancies between EU data protection standards and US laws permitting government surveillance programs like those revealed by Edward Snowden. The Court of Justice of the European Union examined whether SCCs alone sufficed as a transfer mechanism or required case-by-case assessments of third-country laws.
process_and_outcome: In July 2020, the CJEU invalidated the Privacy Shield (Decision 2016/1250) and clarified requirements for SCCs, mandating supplementary measures where necessary. This led to widespread reviews of data transfer practices across EU organizations.
source_rule:
Recital 168 Implementing Acts on Standard Contractual Clauses*

involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (judicial authority)

example_name: Irish DPC Cross-Border Inquiry into Data Transfers
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission launched a cross-border inquiry in September 2020 targeting approximately 500 organizations subject to its lead supervisory authority role. The inquiry focused on international data transfers to the US following the Schrems II judgment, examining reliance on SCCs and the implementation of supplementary measures to mitigate risks from foreign intelligence access.
process_and_outcome: Over 80 organizations responded with remediation plans. Several faced enforcement, including orders to suspend transfers. The process underscored the need for transfer impact assessments, influencing industry-wide adoption of new SCCs.
source_rule:

involved_parties: Various EU controllers and processors (data exporters)

example_name: French CNIL Meta Platforms Data Transfer Fine
example_background: The French data protection authority investigated Meta's data transfers from France to the US, similar to the Irish case, focusing on the use of SCCs post-Schrems II. Meta's practices involved systematic transfers of EU user data for advertising and analytics purposes without adequate supplementary protections against US surveillance.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the CNIL imposed a fine of 90 million euros on Meta Platforms Ireland. Additional orders required cessation of unlawful transfers and implementation of compliant mechanisms.

example_name: Hulu International Data Transfers Settlement
example_background: Hulu, LLC, a US-based streaming service, processed personal data of EU subscribers and transferred it to the US for content personalization and analytics. After Schrems II, Hulu reviewed its transfer mechanisms, which previously relied on Privacy Shield and older SCCs, and transitioned to the 2021 SCCs with encryption and pseudonymization as supplementary measures.
process_and_outcome: Hulu conducted Transfer Impact Assessments and implemented compliant practices, avoiding enforcement actions. The company publicly detailed its compliance roadmap in response to supervisory inquiries.

example_name: Salesforce EU-US Data Transfer Compliance
example_background: Salesforce, a cloud computing company, provides services to EU customers involving data processing in the US. Post-Schrems II, Salesforce adopted the new 2021 SCCs, implemented technical measures like encryption in transit and at rest, and conducted TIAs to ensure protection equivalent to EU standards against third-country access.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce received validation through customer audits and supervisory reviews, maintaining trust and avoiding fines. The approach was highlighted in industry guidance as a model practice.

example_name: Amazon Web Services Data Transfer Framework
example_background: AWS, as a processor for numerous EU controllers, facilitates data storage and processing across global regions, including the US. Following GDPR and Schrems II, AWS developed the AWS Data Processing Addendum incorporating 2021 SCCs, along with tools for TIAs, encryption services, and data residency options.
process_and_outcome: AWS's framework has been accepted by regulators and used in multiple compliance certifications, enabling seamless EU-US data flows without enforcement issues.

example_name: Luxembourg CNPD WhatsApp Data Transfers Fine
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited transferred user data to the US for service provision and analytics. The Luxembourg regulator examined the adequacy of SCCs and supplementary measures in light of US laws.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, WhatsApp was fined 225 million euros for transparency violations, with data transfer practices scrutinized and ordered to align with post-Schrems II requirements.

example_name: OneTrust GDPR Certification Development
example_background: OneTrust developed a GDPR certification program in collaboration with EU regulators, focusing on standardized criteria for data protection management systems. The initiative provides third-party certification for controllers and processors meeting GDPR requirements.
process_and_outcome: The program launched with pilot certifications, receiving endorsement from the European Data Protection Board. Multiple organizations achieved certification, demonstrating structured compliance approaches.
source_rule:
Recital 166 Delegated Acts of the Commission*
1In order to fulfil the objectives of this Regulation, namely to protect the fundamental rights and freedoms of natural persons and in particular their right to the protection of personal data and to ensure the free movement of personal data within the Union, the power to adopt acts in accordance with Article 290 TFEU should be delegated to the Commission. 2In particular, delegated acts should be adopted in respect of criteria and requirements for certification mechanisms, information to be presented by standardised icons and procedures for providing such icons. 3It is of particular importance that the Commission carry out appropriate consultations during its preparatory work, including at expert level. 4The Commission, when preparing and drawing-up delegated acts, should ensure a simultaneous, timely and appropriate transmission of relevant documents to the European Parliament and to the Council.

example_name: Facebook GDPR Cambridge Analytica Fine
example_background: In 2014-2015, the app 'This Is Your Digital Life' collected data from 87 million Facebook users worldwide without proper consent, shared with Cambridge Analytica for political advertising. Facebook failed to implement adequate safeguards to prevent unauthorized data sharing with third parties. The ICO began investigating in 2017, with GDPR applying post-2018.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Facebook £500,000 in 2019, the maximum under pre-GDPR powers, citing failures in data protection practices. Facebook appealed unsuccessfully and enhanced platform data controls.
source_rule:
Recital 167 Implementing Powers of the Commission*
1In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission when provided for by this Regulation. 2Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. 3In that context, the Commission should consider specific measures for micro, small and medium-sized enterprises.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 5 (Principles Relating to Processing of Personal Data)
GDPR Article 25 (Data Protection by Design and by Default)

example_name: CNIL Google Analytics GDPR Decision
example_background: Google Analytics on websites transferred IP addresses and user IDs to the US without sufficient safeguards, lacking valid consent for cookie-based tracking. The French CNIL ordered the data processor to stop processing after reviewing compliance in 2022.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued a formal notice in 2022, followed by €150 million fine against Google in 2023 for broader ad tech practices. Website operators adjusted analytics tools to use EEA-hosted alternatives.
source_rule:
Recital 167 Implementing Powers of the Commission*

involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
relevant_rules: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers of Personal Data to Third Countries)

example_name: Finnish Evangelical Lutheran Church Data Processing Case
example_background: The Finnish Evangelical Lutheran Church, a recognized religious association under Finnish constitutional law, maintained a database tracking members' participation in religious services, beliefs, and family relationships. This system collected data on attendance at communion, confirmation classes, and other church activities, linking it to personal details such as addresses and family ties. The database was used for internal church administration, member outreach, and statistical reporting. Concerns arose when data protection authorities reviewed whether the processing aligned with data minimization and legal basis requirements under EU law, given the church's special status under national law and Article 17 TFEU. The church argued its constitutional recognition allowed broad data handling for religious purposes, but the processing involved sensitive personal data without explicit consent in all cases.
process_and_outcome: The Finnish Data Protection Ombudsman investigated following complaints and referred the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) in 2020. The EDPB issued a binding decision in 2021 stating that Article 17 TFEU and recital 165 do not exempt churches from GDPR material scope requirements. The church was required to align processing with GDPR principles, including demonstrating adequate legal bases and safeguards. No monetary fine was imposed initially, but ongoing compliance monitoring was mandated, leading to database adjustments.
source_rule:
Recital 165 No Prejudice of the Status of Churches and Religious Associations*
This Regulation respects and does not prejudice the status under existing constitutional law of churches and religious associations or communities in the Member States, as recognised in Article 17 TFEU.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 9 (processing of special categories of data)

example_name: Catholic Church in Germany GDPR Alignment (Kirchendatenschutz)
example_background: In Germany, Catholic dioceses operate under canon law and constitutional recognition as public corporations, processing extensive personal data on parishioners, including sacramental records, donation histories, and participation in religious education. Following GDPR implementation in 2018, the German Catholic Church established the Kirchendatenschutz (Church Data Protection) framework, a centralized body to oversee data processing across dioceses. This involved mapping data flows for baptismal registers, marriage records, and youth group activities, ensuring transparency notices and data subject rights were provided despite the church's autonomous status under Article 140 of the German Basic Law, which aligns with Article 17 TFEU protections.
process_and_outcome: The framework was developed proactively with input from data protection authorities, resulting in compliance certification for numerous dioceses by 2020. No enforcement actions were taken; instead, the approach was recognized as a model for religious entities balancing autonomy with data protection, with annual audits confirming adherence.
source_rule:
Recital 165 No Prejudice of the Status of Churches and Religious Associations*
This Regulation respects and does not prejudice the status under existing constitutional law of churches and religious associations or communities in the Member States, as recognised in Article 17 TFEU.
involved_parties: German Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (BfDI)

example_name: Church of Scientology France Data Protection Fine
example_background: The Church of Scientology in France, operating as a religious association, maintained member files with detailed personal information, including psychological profiles, financial contributions, and auditing session notes classified as sensitive data. The organization collected this data during recruitment and ongoing membership activities, storing it in centralized databases accessible across branches. French authorities examined whether this processing respected data subject rights, given the entity's status as a recognized cult under French law, and whether it fell under GDPR exemptions for religious activities protected by Article 17 TFEU.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL (French data protection authority) conducted an investigation in 2019, fining the organization €100,000 in 2021 for lack of legal basis, inadequate security, and failure to honor data access requests. The decision emphasized that recital 165 preserves status but does not override GDPR substantive rules.
source_rule:
Recital 165 No Prejudice of the Status of Churches and Religious Associations*
This Regulation respects and does not prejudice the status under existing constitutional law of churches and religious associations or communities in the Member States, as recognised in Article 17 TFEU.
involved_parties: Church of Scientology Celebrity Centre Paris
CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: Jehovah's Witnesses GDPR Complaint in Netherlands
example_background: Jehovah's Witnesses congregations in the Netherlands kept detailed records on current and former members, including notes on religious conduct, shunning decisions, and personal beliefs derived from Bible study sessions. Data was shared between local groups and international headquarters for oversight. A complaint highlighted lack of transparency and retention beyond necessity, prompting review of how the organization's religious status under Dutch constitutional provisions interacted with EU data protection law.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) investigated in 2020, closing the case in 2022 after the organization implemented a data protection policy with consent mechanisms, minimization, and deletion protocols. No fine was issued, but recommendations reinforced GDPR applicability.
source_rule:
Recital 165 No Prejudice of the Status of Churches and Religious Associations*
This Regulation respects and does not prejudice the status under existing constitutional law of churches and religious associations or communities in the Member States, as recognised in Article 17 TFEU.

example_name: Swedish Church Data Protection Success
example_background: The Church of Sweden, disestablished in 2000 but retaining constitutional recognition as a faith community, processes data on 5.5 million members for parish registers, welfare services, and religious ceremonies. Post-GDPR, it developed an integrated IT system (Svenska Kyrkans IT) with pseudonymization for sensitive data like baptism and burial records, while providing annual privacy reports and data portability tools to members.
process_and_outcome: Compliance was verified through self-audits and IMY (Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection) reviews from 2018-2023, with no investigations or sanctions. The church received positive feedback for its proactive stance.
source_rule:
Recital 165 No Prejudice of the Status of Churches and Religious Associations*
This Regulation respects and does not prejudice the status under existing constitutional law of churches and religious associations or communities in the Member States, as recognised in Article 17 TFEU.

example_name: Italian Catholic Dioceses Data Mapping Initiative
example_background: Catholic dioceses in Italy, recognized under the 1984 Concordat with the Holy See amending the Lateran Treaty, handle sacramental registries and charitable aid data. In response to GDPR, the Italian Episcopal Conference coordinated a nationwide data mapping exercise, identifying processing activities for confessions, catechesis, and donor management, ensuring DPIAs for high-risk activities.
process_and_outcome: Completed by 2019, the initiative led to standardized privacy policies across 226 dioceses, audited by the Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) with no findings of non-compliance.
source_rule:
Recital 165 No Prejudice of the Status of Churches and Religious Associations*
This Regulation respects and does not prejudice the status under existing constitutional law of churches and religious associations or communities in the Member States, as recognised in Article 17 TFEU.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali

example_name: Polish Catholic Church Personal Data Policy
example_background: The Catholic Church in Poland, with constitutional protections under Article 25 of the Polish Constitution, processes data for 33 million baptized members via parish records and Caritas welfare programs. It issued a 2018 instruction from the Polish Episcopate mandating GDPR-compliant practices, including consent for non-essential processing and secure archiving of historical records.
process_and_outcome: The Polish Data Protection Supervisor (UODO) reviewed implementations in 2020-2022, confirming adherence without enforcement actions, highlighting it as effective religious sector compliance.
source_rule:
Recital 165 No Prejudice of the Status of Churches and Religious Associations*
This Regulation respects and does not prejudice the status under existing constitutional law of churches and religious associations or communities in the Member States, as recognised in Article 17 TFEU.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines on Religious Data Processing
example_background: The EDPB addressed how recital 165 interacts with GDPR in its 2020 guidelines on Article 9, discussing religious entities' processing of special category data. Examples included churches tracking dietary restrictions for religious events or membership lists, emphasizing that status preservation does not exempt from lawfulness requirements.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines adopted in May 2020 after public consultation, providing non-binding but influential best practices for EU religious organizations, cited in multiple national decisions.
source_rule:
Recital 165 No Prejudice of the Status of Churches and Religious Associations*
This Regulation respects and does not prejudice the status under existing constitutional law of churches and religious associations or communities in the Member States, as recognised in Article 17 TFEU.

example_name: Google Analytics Consent CNIL Enforcement
example_background: The French data protection authority investigated websites using Google Analytics and similar tools that transferred EU users' personal data to the US without adequate safeguards. The processing involved IP addresses and user behavior data collected via cookies, which fell under general GDPR rules rather than specific ePrivacy protections. CNIL found that standard contractual clauses alone did not sufficiently protect data due to US surveillance laws.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, CNIL ordered Google Analytics provider to stop processing French users' data within one month. Subsequent formal notices were issued to non-compliant sites, with potential fines up to 4% of global turnover.
source_rule:
Recital 173 Relationship to Directive 2002/58/EC*
1This Regulation should apply to all matters concerning the protection of fundamental rights and freedoms vis-à-vis the processing of personal data which are not subject to specific obligations with the same objective set out in Directive 2002/58/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council¹, including the obligations on the controller and the rights of natural persons. 2In order to clarify the relationship between this Regulation and Directive 2002/58/EC, that Directive should be amended accordingly. 3Once this Regulation is adopted, Directive 2002/58/EC should be reviewed in particular in order to ensure consistency with this Regulation,
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Vodafone Spain ePrivacy Fine
example_background: Vodafone Spain pre-checked consent boxes for commercial communications and partner offers in customer contracts, processing phone numbers and personal data for marketing without granular, freely given consent. This related to direct marketing via electronic means, overlapping with but extending beyond specific telecom privacy rules.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) fined Vodafone €8 million in 2021 for breaching cookie and consent rules under ePrivacy and GDPR frameworks.
source_rule:

example_name: Amazon France Logistics Data Fine
example_background: Amazon France Logistique used employee performance data collected via warehouse scanners for optimization without transparent legal basis or impact assessments. Data included timestamps, breaks, and productivity metrics processed for algorithmic management.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Amazon €32 million in 2024 after finding failures in data minimization, transparency, and employee rights under GDPR.

example_name: Schrems II Google Analytics Case
example_background: Austrian activist Max Schrems challenged Austrian Post AG's use of Google Analytics, which transferred IP addresses to the US without sufficient protections post-Schrems II ruling. The processing involved web analytics cookies and data flows not covered by specific ePrivacy telecom exemptions.
process_and_outcome: Austrian DPA ruled in December 2023 that the transfers violated GDPR Chapter V, ordering cessation. Similar cases followed across EU DPAs.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland GDPR Children's Data Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, responsible for Facebook and Instagram in the EU, processed personal data of minors without age verification or adequate safeguards. The service settings allowed public profiles by default for minors, exposing data to search engines and external sites. Behavioral analysis targeted minors for advertising. The UK ICO reviewed data of up to 5.6 million children under 13 since 2018.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Meta £12.5 million in September 2023 for failures in age-appropriate design and default privacy settings. Meta committed to changes but faced ongoing multi-jurisdictional scrutiny under GDPR's coordinated enforcement.
source_rule:
Recital 170 Principle of Subsidiarity and Principle of Proportionality*
1Since the objective of this Regulation, namely to ensure an equivalent level of protection of natural persons and the free flow of personal data throughout the Union, cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can rather, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Union level, the Union may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU). 2In accordance with the principle of proportionality as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective.
relevant_rules: GDPR Article 8 (Conditions applicable to child's consent)
GDPR Article 25 (Data protection by design and by default)

example_name: TikTok GDPR Children's Privacy Penalty
example_background: TikTok processed data of children under 13 without parental consent, using public-by-default profiles and tracking via SDKs. Age gates were bypassed using birthdates. The platform pushed notifications encouraging data disclosure and targeted minors with ads. This affected millions of EU users.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million in September 2023 following a 2020 investigation into child data practices. TikTok appealed but enhanced age verification post-decision.
source_rule:
Recital 170 Principle of Subsidiarity and Principle of Proportionality*

example_name: H&M GDPR Employee Data Breach Fine
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg service center collected excessive data on 1,091 employees via 'welcome meetings,' gossip sessions, and surveillance, storing sensitive details like sick leave reasons and personal issues without consent or necessity. Data was shared internally without safeguards.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA fined H&M JusticeCV €35.25 million in October 2020 after a whistleblower tip. The fine reflected systematic monitoring disproportional to business needs.

involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: British Airways GDPR Transition Fine
example_background: British Airways encountered a data breach between 6 August and 5 September 2018, where personal data of approximately 400,000 customers, including names, payment card details, and login credentials, was accessed by unauthorized parties through a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack exploitation. This occurred shortly after the GDPR became applicable on 25 May 2018, marking one of the first major enforcement actions under the new regulation. The breach highlighted challenges in rapidly transitioning security practices from the pre-GDPR era under Directive 95/46/EC to meet heightened GDPR standards within the initial compliance period. The company had been processing data under legacy consents and authorizations from the Directive but faced scrutiny on whether post-GDPR processing aligned with new requirements.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated the incident, initially proposing a fine of £183.39 million in October 2019. After an appeal and further review amid economic challenges including COVID-19, the fine was reduced and finalized at £20 million in October 2020. British Airways implemented enhanced security measures and remediation efforts as part of the enforcement undertaking.
source_rule:
Recital 171 Repeal of Directive 95/46/EC and Transitional Provisions*
1Directive 95/46/EC should be repealed by this Regulation. 2Processing already under way on the date of application of this Regulation should be brought into conformity with this Regulation within the period of two years after which this Regulation enters into force. 3Where processing is based on consent pursuant to Directive 95/46/EC, it is not necessary for the data subject to give his or her consent again if the manner in which the consent has been given is in line with the conditions of this Regulation, so as to allow the controller to continue such processing after the date of application of this Regulation. 4Commission decisions adopted and authorisations by supervisory authorities based on Directive 95/46/EC remain in force until amended, replaced or repealed.

example_name: Deloitte GDPR Transitional Compliance Success
example_background: Deloitte, a global professional services firm, undertook a comprehensive project to transition its European data processing activities from Directive 95/46/EC to full GDPR compliance ahead of the regulation's application date. This involved mapping over 1,000 processing activities, reviewing legacy consents obtained under the Directive, and confirming their validity under GDPR standards without requiring renewals where conditions were met per Recital 171. The firm conducted data protection impact assessments, updated privacy notices, and migrated systems within the initial two-year period, serving as a model for large organizations handling complex multinational data flows from pre-GDPR authorizations.
process_and_outcome: Deloitte completed the transition without enforcement actions, earning recognition in industry reports for proactive compliance. The firm published case studies and frameworks shared with clients, demonstrating seamless continuity of processing and avoidance of disruptions.

example_name: Google €50 Million French GDPR Fine
example_background: Google received the first GDPR fine from the French data protection authority shortly after the regulation's entry into force. The case centered on Android users' personal data processing for targeted advertising, where consent banners did not meet GDPR standards for being freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. Pre-GDPR processing under Directive 95/46/EC had relied on cookie notices deemed sufficient, but post-25 May 2018, Google failed to adjust these within the transitional framework, leading to continued non-conforming practices during the initial compliance phase outlined in Recital 171.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued a €50 million fine on 21 January 2019 after investigating complaints. Google updated its consent mechanisms globally and faced additional EU-wide scrutiny without further penalties in this instance.

example_name: Unilever GDPR Transition Project Success
example_background: Unilever, a multinational consumer goods company, executed a group-wide GDPR readiness program starting in 2016, culminating in full compliance by the 25 May 2018 application date. This included auditing thousands of legacy processing operations under Directive 95/46/EC across 190 countries, validating consents for continuity per Recital 171 point 3, and retaining valid supervisory authority authorizations. The firm centralized its data protection officer function, implemented privacy by design in supply chains, and trained over 150,000 employees, ensuring all ongoing processing was brought into conformity ahead of the two-year deadline.
process_and_outcome: Unilever achieved compliance without fines or major incidents, recognized by the IAPP as a best practice example. The company reported sustained operations and positive audit outcomes from European regulators.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors GDPR Compliance Transition
example_background: SAP's SuccessFactors HR cloud platform managed employee data for numerous EU firms. Ahead of GDPR, SAP conducted a thorough review of processing activities governed by Directive 95/46/EC, including customer consents and supervisory approvals. They confirmed legacy consents met GDPR thresholds per Recital 171, migrated data to compliant systems, and provided tools for controllers to exercise data subject rights, ensuring seamless transition within the two-year window without interrupting HR services for thousands of clients.
process_and_outcome: SAP avoided enforcement and received certifications like ISO 27701. Client case studies highlighted zero disruption and enhanced privacy features.

example_name: H&M €35.25 Million Employee Data Fine
example_background: H&M's German subsidiary monitored employee private communications, including WhatsApp messages about working conditions, without explicit consent or legal basis aligned with GDPR. This surveillance continued from pre-GDPR practices under Directive 95/46/EC into the post-application period without adjustment, failing to bring processing into conformity as required by Recital 171. Data was stored in shared drives and used for performance discussions, affecting hundreds of staff.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA fined H&M Justice €35.25 million in October 2020 after employee complaints. H&M discontinued the practices and improved internal policies.
